Popular Skills

Whānau friendlyHandouts can be used alone, with whānau, or with support workers.
Print readyDesigned for appointments, kitchen tables, waiting rooms, and follow-up plans.
Try one skill now

Start gently

Different moments need different levels of effort. Pick the lightest useful action for right now.

Not sure where to start? Open quick situations

Choose the closest option. Each pathway starts with one practical first skill rather than a long article.

What are you dealing with today?

Choose the closest card

These are simple entry points, not diagnoses. Choose the closest fit. Each card opens one suggested first step before showing more options.

Low energy option: open one card, use the first suggested skill, then stop if that is enough for now.

Other things Kete can help withMore specific situationsOpen this if your situation is not in the main cards.

These links keep Kete broad without putting every topic on the front page at once. Choose the closest fit, or skip this section and use search.

Try a practical tool

High-use skills that are likely to be useful between appointments. Open one and use the first suggested step.

Grounding

Reconnect attention to the present when overwhelmed, numb, panicky, or triggered.

ACT defusion

Unhook from sticky thoughts without needing to argue with them.

Social anxiety practice

Map the social anxiety loop, reduce safety behaviours, and practise gradual real-life experiments.

Distress tolerance

Stabilise first when emotions are too high to solve everything.

Behavioural activation

Use small actions to rebuild routine, mastery, connection, and mood.

Why Kete is different

Kete is not a chatbot, diagnostic tool, or therapy replacement. It is a practical basket of skills and handouts.

Use alongside support

The structure keeps skills, handouts, crisis guidance, and support links calm and easy to scan.

Early-stage resource

This prototype should be clinically and culturally reviewed before formal service use. Version: public prototype draft, 2026.

Skills

Skills

Choose the closest problem, or search by a word that fits. Core safety and common distress pathways appear first; more specific life situations and support topics come later. You do not need to do everything at once.

Safety note: Kete skills are self-help tools, not a replacement for urgent help or professional care. If you feel unsafe, at immediate risk, or unable to keep yourself safe, use the Crisis Help section or contact local emergency support.

No matching skills found. Try a broader word like anxiety, sleep, grounding, mood, voices, crisis, or relationships.

Printable handout library

Printable Handouts

Open a category, search by topic, then choose one handout. Cards are intentionally short; the full handout opens below when selected.

No matching handouts found. Try a broader word like anxiety, sleep, grounding, crisis, values, voices, relationships, or alcohol.

Grounding: coming back to the present

What this is for: grounding can help when you feel panicky, overwhelmed, numb, disconnected, triggered, or pulled into frightening thoughts, memories, voices, or body sensations.

When not to rely on this alone: use urgent support if you feel unable to stay safe, are at risk of harming yourself or someone else, or feel so disconnected that you cannot orient to where you are.

Why this helps

Grounding is not about forcing yourself to calm down. It is a way of helping your brain notice, “I am here, now.” This can give the thinking part of the brain more room to return.

Try this step by step

  • Orient: keep your eyes open if that feels safer. Look around and name where you are, the date, and one thing that tells you this is the present.
  • Feet and contact: press your feet into the floor. Notice the chair, wall, clothing, or object touching your body.
  • 5–4–3–2–1: name five things you can see, four you can feel, three you can hear, two you can smell, and one you can taste.
  • Sensory anchor: hold something textured, sip cold water, wrap in a blanket, notice a scent, or use safe movement such as stretching your hands.
  • Safety statement: say, “I am here. This is today. I am in this room. I can take one next step.”

Other grounding options

  • Mental grounding: name categories, count slowly, read words backwards, or describe an ordinary task in detail.
  • Physical grounding: run cool water over your hands, grip the chair, touch different textures, or notice your feet.
  • Soothing grounding: use a blanket, safe scent, music, warm drink, or kind phrase.

If this does not work straight away

Grounding can feel too simple or may need repeating. Try changing the sense you use: visual grounding, sound, touch, temperature, movement, or connection with another person. Practise when calm so it is easier to use when distressed.

Try this now: look around and name three colours, three shapes, and three sounds.

Safety note: some mindfulness or body-focused exercises can increase distress for people with trauma, dissociation, panic, or psychosis vulnerability. Use eyes-open, external, sensory grounding if internal focus feels unsafe.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Panic support: riding out the alarm wave

What this is for: sudden waves of panic, racing heart, breathlessness, shaking, dizziness, chest tightness, nausea, fear of losing control, or feeling that something awful is about to happen.

When to use it: use this when the body alarm is high but you are basically safe, you recognise the pattern as panic or anxiety, and you need a simple way to ride out the first few minutes.

When not to rely on this alone: seek medical or urgent help if symptoms are new, severe, different from usual, linked with chest pain, fainting, injury, overdose, substance use, pregnancy, withdrawal, or if safety feels uncertain. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111.

Why this helps

Panic is a strong alarm response. The sensations are real and frightening, but panic usually rises, peaks, and falls like a wave. The aim is not to force calm or prove nothing is wrong. The aim is to lower extra alarm, stay oriented, and give the body time to come down.

Try this step by step

  • Name it gently: “This feels like panic or an alarm response.” You do not need to be completely certain.
  • Check basic safety once: ask, “Am I in immediate danger, or is this my alarm system firing?” If there may be medical danger, violence, overdose, or injury, get help rather than using this alone.
  • Orient outward: keep your eyes open, look around the room, and name three neutral objects, one sound, and one colour.
  • Unclench: lower your shoulders, loosen your jaw, press your feet into the floor, and let your hands open if possible.
  • Lengthen the out-breath: breathe in normally, then make the out-breath a little longer. Do not force deep breathing if that makes panic worse, and do not use paper-bag breathing.
  • Describe sensations neutrally: “fast heart,” “tight chest,” “warm face,” “shaky legs.” These are body sensations, not instructions.
  • Choose one safe action: sit, stand, walk slowly, sip water, text someone, step outside with support, or return gently to the task if safe.

Short example

A person feels their heart racing in a supermarket and thinks, “I’m going to collapse.” They move to the side of the aisle, put both feet on the ground, name five things they can see, breathe out slowly, and text a support person: “Panic wave. I’m safe, but I’m riding it out.” After a few minutes they decide whether to buy one item, leave calmly, or ask for support.

Common difficulties

  • “Breathing makes me focus on my body too much.” Use eyes-open orientation, slow walking, cold water on hands, or naming objects instead.
  • “I keep checking my pulse or symptoms.” Try one safety check, then shift to external grounding. Repeated checking can keep the alarm loop going.
  • “I want to escape immediately.” Leaving is sometimes sensible. If the place is safe, try staying for ten more seconds first so your body learns the wave can pass.
  • “I feel ashamed afterwards.” Panic is a body alarm, not a character flaw. Recovery often starts with one small, non-judging reset.

After the panic wave

Notice what helped even slightly. Avoid judging yourself for having panic. If you begin avoiding more places or activities, consider gradual exposure with support. Repeated safe practice can help the brain relearn that discomfort can be survived.

Try this now: breathe out slowly once, place both feet on the floor, and name the room you are in.

If this does not work

If panic is frequent, worsening, linked with trauma, causing major avoidance, or feels hard to distinguish from medical symptoms, talk with a GP, nurse, counsellor, peer worker, or crisis/support service. If you might harm yourself or someone else, cannot stay safe, feel severely confused, or are in immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. For free 24/7 mental health support in New Zealand, call or text 1737.

Safety note: panic skills support coping; they do not replace medical assessment when symptoms are unusual, severe, or medically concerning.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

ACT defusion: unhooking from sticky thoughts

What this is for: repetitive, cruel, frightening, shame-based, or bossy thoughts that feel fused with reality, such as “I can’t cope,” “I’m useless,” “everyone hates me,” or “something bad will happen.”

When to use it: use this when a thought is loud, sticky, or believable enough that it starts deciding what you do next.

When not to rely on this alone: use support if thoughts are linked with self-harm, suicide, command voices, escalating paranoia, mania, trauma flooding, unsafe relationships, or feeling unable to stay safe.

Why this helps

Defusion is an ACT skill. It does not try to prove a thought wrong, argue with it, or replace it with positive thinking. It helps you notice a thought as something your mind is doing, so there is a little more room between the thought and your next action.

The thought may still feel true. The useful question is not “Can I delete this thought?” but “Can I notice it and still take one small safe step?”

Try this step by step

  1. Pause and ground first: feel your feet, look around the room, and name one thing you can see. If your distress is very high, start with grounding or support before using thought work.
  2. Write the thought exactly as it shows up: for example, “I am useless,” “I will mess this up,” or “I am not safe.”
  3. Add distance: put “I’m having the thought that…” in front of it.
  4. Add one more layer: try “I’m noticing I’m having the thought that…”
  5. Name the pattern: “This is the shame story,” “This is the danger alarm,” “This is the not-good-enough story,” or “This is my mind trying to protect me.”
  6. Let the thought be present without obeying it: imagine it as a radio in another room, words on a screen, a cloud passing, or a leaf floating down a stream.
  7. Ask the workability question: “If I let this thought run my behaviour, does it move me toward or away from the person I want to be?”
  8. Choose one small values step: send the message, take the shower, eat something, step outside, ask for help, or do five minutes of the task.

Short example

Thought: “I’m useless. I should not even try.”

Defusion: “I’m noticing I’m having the old useless story.”

Next step: “I will not solve my whole life right now. I will put one load of washing on and text one person back.”

Common difficulties

  • “The thought still feels true.” That is common. Defusion is about changing your relationship to the thought, not forcing yourself to stop believing it instantly.
  • “This feels silly.” Use plainer wording: “My mind is saying…” or “A thought is showing up.” You do not have to use playful voices or metaphors.
  • “The thought is about real danger.” Do not use defusion to talk yourself out of genuine safety signals. Check the facts, move toward safety, and contact support.
  • “The thought gets louder when I notice it.” Stop the exercise, use grounding, open your eyes, orient to the room, and try again later or with support.

If this does not work

Use a more stabilising skill first: grounding, paced breathing, cold water, movement, sensory soothing, or contacting someone safe. Defusion is usually easier once your body is less flooded.

If thoughts are telling you to harm yourself or someone else, if voices are giving commands, if paranoia is escalating, if you have not slept and feel unusually energised or impulsive, or if you cannot stay safe, do not treat this as a private worksheet. Contact urgent support. In New Zealand, call 111 if there is immediate danger. Call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support.

Try this now: write one sticky thought, then put “I’m noticing I’m having the thought that…” in front of it. Then choose one safe action that matters more than the thought.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Behavioural activation: small actions before motivation

What this is for: low mood, withdrawal, low motivation, loss of routine, reduced pleasure, feeling stuck, or waiting until you “feel ready” before doing anything.

When not to rely on this alone: use professional, whānau, peer, GP, nurse, counsellor, or crisis support if depression is severe, risk is increasing, you cannot care for basic needs, sleep has changed drastically, or hopelessness feels unsafe.

Why this helps

Low mood often says, “wait until you feel better first.” That can quietly shrink the day. Less activity can then reduce mood, confidence, energy, connection, and routine. Behavioural activation gently works the other way around: take one small, doable action first, then notice whether mood, energy, or confidence shifts even slightly. Motivation often comes after action, not before it.

Choose one activity type

TypeWhat it can look likeSmallest useful version
PleasureMusic, warm drink, sunlight, nature, humour, a favourite show, a small hobby.One song, one sip outside, one page, one photo of something pleasant.
MasteryShower, dishes, one email, medication routine, opening curtains, paying one bill.Two minutes only, one item only, or set things up without finishing.
ConnectionText someone, sit near whānau, go to a group, greet a neighbour.Send one low-pressure message or sit in the same room without needing to talk much.
ValuesOne tiny action that reflects the kind of person you want to be.One caring, steady, honest, creative, spiritual, or practical action under five minutes.

Try this step by step

  • Start with the smallest real action: choose something so small it almost feels too easy.
  • Make it specific: write what, when, where, and for how long. “After lunch, I will stand outside for two minutes” is better than “get outside more.”
  • Lower the entry point: prepare the first step rather than the whole task. Put shoes by the door, open the document, place a towel in the bathroom, or write the first sentence.
  • Track gently: rate mood, energy, or tension before and after from 0–10. The number is information, not a grade.
  • Repeat before judging: small repeated actions usually help more than one large effort followed by collapse.

Short example

Example: A person has been staying in bed until midday and waiting to feel motivated. They choose one mastery action: open the curtains and drink water before checking their phone. They rate energy as 2/10 before and 3/10 after. It is not a cure, but it gives the day a slightly stronger starting point.

Common difficulties

  • “It did not make me happy.” The first aim is not instant happiness. It is to reduce shutdown, rebuild routine, or make the next step easier.
  • “I chose too much and crashed.” Shrink the task by 90%. Choose the version you could do on a bad day.
  • “Everything feels pointless.” Use connection or support first. Ask someone to sit with you, text you, or help choose one tiny action.
  • “I only did part of it.” Partial completion still counts. Behavioural activation is about re-entering life in small pieces.

If this does not work

The task may be too big, too vague, too lonely, or not connected to what matters. Shrink it, add support, choose a connection task, or choose a body-based starter such as water, food, light, medication routine, or sleep preparation. If low mood is worsening, daily life is becoming unmanageable, or you are having thoughts of self-harm or not wanting to be alive, use Kete alongside real support. In New Zealand, call 111 if there is immediate danger. Call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support.

Try this now: choose one five-minute action for today: open curtains, drink water, step outside, put three things away, shower, eat something simple, or message one safe person. Make it small enough that you do not need to feel motivated first.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Distress tolerance: getting through the wave safely

What this is for: emotional distress that is too intense for problem-solving, including urges to lash out, shut down, self-harm, use substances, send unsafe messages, spend money, drive while flooded, or make big decisions before the wave has passed.

When to use it: use this when your body and mind are already in emergency mode. At this point the first job is not insight, perfect calm, or a life decision. The first job is to get through the next few minutes without making things less safe.

When not to rely on this alone: if you may harm yourself or someone else, have taken an overdose, cannot stay safe, feel out of control, are in immediate danger, or voices are commanding harm, move toward urgent support. In New Zealand call 111 for immediate danger or call/text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support.

Why this helps

Distress tolerance is crisis survival. It does not mean approving of the situation, ignoring what happened, or forcing yourself to be calm. It means lowering the chance of extra harm while the nervous system is at its loudest. When distress is very high, body-based and safety-based skills usually come before analysis, problem-solving, repair conversations, or big choices.

Use the intensity ladder

IntensityWhat it may look likeBest first move
8–10Overwhelmed, unsafe, impulsive, frozen, panicky, or unable to think clearly.Body and safety first: move away from means of harm, cool water or a cool pack if safe for your body, safe movement, longer out-breath, contact support, or use your crisis plan.
6–8Strong urges, racing thoughts, wanting to escape, send a message, drink/use, argue, or disappear.Strategic distraction, grounding, soothing, support contact, delay the decision, or use a short time container.
4–6Distressed but partly able to choose, notice patterns, and follow steps.Make room for the feeling, defusion, urge surfing, thought check, values step, or one small problem-solving action.
0–4Settled enough to reflect, learn, repair, or plan ahead.Review what helped, plan prevention, repair if safe, update your crisis plan, or practise a small values-based action.

Try this step by step

  1. Name the wave: say, “This is a distress wave. I do not need to solve everything while it is this high.”
  2. Pause the decision: delay major actions, messages, spending, substances, driving, quitting, confronting, or relationship decisions until the wave drops.
  3. Reduce access to harm: put distance between you and anything you might use to hurt yourself or someone else. Move to a safer room, public space, or support person if needed.
  4. Use biology first: splash cool water, hold a cool pack wrapped in cloth, use safe movement, then lengthen the out-breath. Adapt this for medical conditions, pain, trauma, disability, pregnancy, or sensory needs.
  5. Use a time container: set 10–20 minutes and do one neutral task such as folding washing, showering, walking outside, making tea, or sitting with a support person. You are setting the problem down briefly, not abandoning it.
  6. Ground: hold an object, name five things you can see, feel your feet, describe the room, or say today’s date and where you are.
  7. Choose the next safe step: contact a support person, use your crisis plan, eat something simple, drink water, take medication only as prescribed, rest, or write the issue down for later.

Short example

Example: After an argument, Aroha feels a strong urge to send ten angry messages and drink to calm down. She rates the wave as 8/10. She puts her phone in another room for 20 minutes, runs cool water over her hands, texts one safe person “I am flooded and need to not act on this yet,” and waits until the wave drops before deciding what to say.

Common difficulties

  • “This feels fake.” That is common. The skill does not need to feel convincing. It only needs to reduce harm by a small amount.
  • “I want to fix it now.” Write the problem down and promise to return to it when the intensity is lower. Problem-solving works better once the wave has dropped.
  • “Distraction feels like avoidance.” In a crisis wave, short-term distraction can be safety. Avoidance is when the problem is never returned to; distress tolerance is a pause.
  • “My body feels too activated.” Start with movement, cool temperature, grounding, or support contact before trying thinking-based skills.
  • “I feel numb, not distressed.” Use sensory grounding, safe connection, light, warmth, texture, or movement. Do not push yourself into trauma memories to prove something is wrong.

If this does not work

If distress stays high, urges are getting stronger, you are using alcohol or drugs to get through the wave, you cannot move away from means of harm, or you feel unsafe with yourself or someone else, do not keep trying skills alone. Contact a trusted person, your GP, crisis team, counsellor, nurse, peer support, or call/text 1737. Call 111 in New Zealand if there is immediate danger.

Try this now: rate distress from 0–10. Choose one matching first move from the table and do it for the next 10 minutes before making any major decision.

Kete handout · general education, not a crisis service · use alongside professional, whānau, peer, GP, nurse, counsellor, or crisis support when risk is high or things are worsening.

Distress ladder: choosing the right skill first

What this is for: moments when distress is high and you are not sure whether to ground, distract, breathe, problem-solve, contact someone, or use a safety plan.

When to use it: use this before choosing a coping skill, especially when you are overwhelmed, panicky, angry, flooded, numb, ashamed, or pulled toward an urge you may regret.

When not to rely on this alone: if there is immediate danger, you may harm yourself or someone else, you cannot stay safe, you are hearing commands to hurt yourself or others, paranoia is escalating, you are intoxicated or withdrawing, or someone else is threatening or controlling you, do not spend time finding the perfect skill. Move toward another person, your safety plan, crisis support, emergency care, 1737, or 111.

Why this helps

A flooded brain has less room for complex reflection. The aim is not to force calm or analyse everything. The aim is to match the first step to your current level of distress, then shift to a different kind of skill as your system settles.

Use the ladder

8–10: full alarm
Start with safety and body-based actions. Move away from danger if possible, lower stimulation, use a cold cue only if safe for you, slow the out-breath, plant feet on the floor, and contact someone rather than handling it alone.
6–7: too loud to think
Use simple attention tasks. Try a timed neutral activity such as sorting, dishes, shower, walking, craft, a puzzle, naming categories, or holding something textured. Keep it short and concrete.
4–5: mind racing but workable
Use grounding and naming. Describe the room, name the date and place, notice body sensations, write the main worry in one sentence, or choose one next step for the next 10 minutes.
1–3: settled enough to reflect
Use thinking, values, repair, planning, or prevention tools. This is the better time for a thought check, problem-solving, values step, conversation plan, or review of what made distress rise.

Step by step

  • Rate the intensity: choose a rough number from 0–10. It does not need to be exact.
  • Choose the matching first move: use the ladder above. Start smaller than you think you need to.
  • Set a short timer: try the first move for 2–10 minutes. You are not committing to it forever.
  • Check again: if distress drops, move down the ladder. If distress rises, move up the ladder and add support.
  • Name the next support step: write who you will text, call, sit near, or contact if this stays high.

Example

A person rates their distress at 8/10 after a conflict. They want to send several angry messages. Instead of analysing the relationship straight away, they put the phone out of reach, drink water, stand outside for two minutes, text a trusted person “I’m really activated and need to not make this worse,” then re-rate after 10 minutes.

Common difficulties

  • “I do not know my number.” Use a broad category instead: full alarm, too loud to think, workable, or settled enough to reflect.
  • “Grounding is not working.” Move up the ladder. You may need lower stimulation, movement, connection, or a safety step before reflective grounding helps.
  • “Distraction feels like avoidance.” Strategic distraction can be a temporary stabilising step. You can return to the problem when your system has more room.
  • “I keep choosing thinking tools too early.” Put a note somewhere visible: body first, attention second, thinking later.

If this does not work

If the distress stays high, keeps returning, or is linked with self-harm urges, violence risk, command voices, escalating paranoia, severe insomnia, trauma flooding, substance withdrawal, or unsafe relationships, use this handout alongside support rather than alone. In New Zealand, call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support. If there is immediate danger, call 111.

Try this now: write: “My distress is about ___ /10. My first move is ___. If it rises or stays high, I will contact ___.”

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Sleep reset: rebuilding the sleep signal

What this is for: irregular sleep, trouble falling asleep, waking often, lying awake worrying, sleeping late, or sleep problems that worsen mood, anxiety, concentration, irritability, or voices/paranoia vulnerability.

When not to rely on this alone: seek medical or mental health support for severe persistent insomnia, suspected sleep apnoea, restless legs, medication side effects, trauma nightmares, substance withdrawal, or reduced need for sleep with unusually high energy, racing thoughts, impulsivity, or elevated mood.

Why this helps

Sleep and distress feed each other. Poor sleep increases emotional reactivity and makes coping skills harder to access; distress then makes sleep harder. CBT-I-informed sleep support works by rebuilding repeated cues: wake time, daylight, reduced stimulation, bed as a sleep cue, and a calmer response to being awake.

Start with pattern, not pressure

Not every sleep problem is the same. Before adding rules, notice the pattern: trouble falling asleep, waking often, waking too early, sleeping at the wrong time, nightmares, breathing pauses/snoring, restless legs, medicines/substances, pain, trauma reminders, or reduced need for sleep with high energy. The pattern helps decide the safest next step.

Try this step by step

  • Anchor wake time: choose a realistic wake time and keep it as steady as possible for several days. This is usually more useful than forcing an early bedtime.
  • Use light: get daylight early in the day where possible. Open curtains, step outside, or sit near a bright window.
  • Protect the evening: reduce late caffeine, nicotine/vaping, alcohol, bright screens, gaming, heated conversations, and clock-watching if they affect you.
  • Wind down predictably: create a 20–40 minute routine that lowers stimulation: dim lights, prepare tomorrow, shower, gentle stretching, reading, calming audio, or a short relaxation practice.
  • Park worries before bed: write worries and one next step earlier in the evening. Bed is not the place for problem-solving if you can help it.
  • Rebuild bed as a sleep cue: keep bed mainly for sleep, rest, and intimacy. Avoid long periods of scrolling, arguing, working, or analysing distress in bed.
  • If awake and frustrated: turn the clock away. Tell yourself: “I am resting even if I am not sleeping.” If you are awake for a long time and wound up, get up briefly if practical and do something quiet, dim, and boring. Return when sleepy.

Three-night experiment

NightOne cue I will repeatWhat I noticed next morning
1
2
3

If sleep does not improve

Do not keep adding more rules until sleep becomes another source of pressure. Choose one or two changes first and repeat them for several nights. A brief sleep diary can help identify patterns: wake time, naps, caffeine, alcohol/nicotine, screens, light, stress, medicines, and night waking. CBT-I, GP review, medication review, or mental health support may be appropriate.

Seek extra support: get advice if sleep problems are severe, persistent, linked with breathing pauses/snoring, restless legs, trauma nightmares, substance withdrawal, medication concerns, severe daytime impairment, or a reduced need for sleep with high energy, racing thoughts, impulsivity, or risky behaviour.

Try this now: choose tomorrow’s wake time and one wind-down action you can repeat. Keep it small enough that you can do it on a bad day.

Kete handout · general education · informed by Health NZ, Healthify NZ, and CCI sleep resources · review before formal clinical use.

Sleep hygiene: small habits that support sleep

What this is for: making sleep easier by adjusting daily habits, bedroom cues, light, caffeine, screens, naps, and wind-down routines.

When to use it: when sleep is patchy, bedtime has become stressful, evenings are too stimulating, mornings drift later, or poor sleep is affecting mood, anxiety, concentration, irritability, voices, paranoia, or coping capacity.

When not to rely on this alone: seek health or mental health advice for severe or persistent insomnia, suspected sleep apnoea, restless legs, trauma nightmares, substance withdrawal, medication side effects, or reduced need for sleep with high energy, racing thoughts, impulsivity, or elevated mood.

Why this helps

Sleep hygiene means the ordinary habits and environment that tell the brain and body when to be alert and when to wind down. It does not force sleep. It makes sleep more likely by repeating steady cues: light in the morning, less stimulation at night, a calmer sleep space, and a bed that feels associated with rest rather than worry.

Choose one or two changes first

Trying to fix every sleep habit at once can turn sleep into another pressure task. Pick the smallest change that is likely to help and repeat it for three nights before judging whether it is useful.

AreaSmall experimentMy choice
Morning anchorKeep wake time reasonably consistent and get daylight early where possible.
Caffeine / nicotine / vapingNotice whether coffee, energy drinks, tea, cola, nicotine, or vaping affect sleep, especially later in the day.
Sleep spaceMake the room as cool, dark, quiet, and comfortable as possible. Turn clocks away if checking the time fuels stress.
Wind-downUse a short repeatable routine: dim lights, prepare tomorrow, shower, reading, calm audio, karakia/prayer if meaningful, or gentle stretching.
Bed as a sleep cueAvoid using bed for long scrolling, work, conflict, or problem-solving.
Naps and catch-up sleepIf naps make night sleep worse, shorten them or move them earlier. Avoid using long daytime sleep as the main fix for poor nights unless advised.

If you are awake and frustrated

  • Turn the clock away if checking the time increases pressure.
  • Use a quiet phrase such as: “Rest still counts. I do not have to force sleep.”
  • If you are awake for a long time and becoming more wound up, get up briefly if practical and do something quiet, dim, and boring. Return when sleepy.
  • Avoid problem-solving, arguments, intense media, work tasks, or repeated symptom checking in bed where possible.

Short example

Example: A person notices they stay on their phone in bed and then lie awake worrying. For three nights they charge the phone across the room, turn the clock away, use calm audio for 15 minutes, and write tomorrow’s first task on paper before getting into bed.

Common difficulties

  • “I tried once and it did not work.” Sleep cues usually need repetition. Judge a small experiment after a few nights, not one difficult night.
  • “The more I try, the worse it gets.” Reduce the number of rules. Choose one gentle cue and make the aim “resting better,” not “making myself sleep.”
  • “Night-time is when worries arrive.” Move worry-list time earlier in the evening. Write one next step, then return to a boring wind-down cue.
  • “I feel wired even with little sleep.” If reduced sleep comes with unusually high energy, racing thoughts, impulsivity, elevated mood, or risky behaviour, seek professional advice early.

If this does not work

Do not keep adding more rules. Sleep pressure often gets worse when sleep becomes a performance test. Track patterns for a week and take the information to a GP, prescriber, pharmacist, therapist, or mental health team if sleep remains difficult.

Seek extra support: get advice if sleep problems are severe, persistent, linked with breathing pauses or snoring, restless legs, trauma nightmares, substance withdrawal, medication concerns, severe daytime impairment, or a reduced need for sleep with high energy, racing thoughts, impulsivity, or risky behaviour.

Try this now: choose one sleep hygiene experiment for the next three nights: same wake time, morning light, caffeine cut-off, clock turned away, phone out of bed, or a 15-minute wind-down routine.

Kete handout · informed by Healthify NZ, NHS, and CCI sleep hygiene resources · general education.

Night distress: when everything feels worse at 2am

What this is for: night-time worry, shame, fear, trauma reminders, voices, loneliness, nightmares, or catastrophic thoughts that become stronger when you are tired and trying to sleep.

When not to rely on this alone: if you feel unable to stay safe, might act on self-harm or suicidal thoughts, or voices are commanding harm, contact urgent support. In New Zealand call 111 for immediate danger or call/text 1737 for support.

Why night makes distress louder

At night there are fewer distractions, less social contact, more quiet, and more fatigue. A tired brain has less access to perspective and problem-solving. The thought may feel certain, but it may be being judged by an exhausted threat system.

Night plan

  • Do not debate your whole life in bed. Write one line: “This is a night-time thought. I will review it tomorrow at ___.”
  • Remove the clock. Watching time pass usually adds pressure and arousal.
  • Use gentle settling, not intensity. Try a longer out-breath, relaxing jaw/shoulders, warm non-caffeinated drink, soft audio, prayer/karakia if this fits you, or a grounding object.
  • If wound up, leave bed briefly if practical. Do something dim, boring, and safe. Return when sleepy. Avoid bright screens and big decisions.
  • Park the problem. Write one next step for tomorrow: call, email, appointment, support person, or task.
  • If safety is uncertain, do not stay alone with it. Contact a person, crisis line, 1737, emergency service, or 111 depending on risk.

If you wake from a nightmare or flashback

  • Orient first: turn on a soft light if needed, name the room, date, and one sign that you are in the present.
  • Use a body anchor: feet on the floor, hands around a mug, cool water, a blanket, or a textured object.
  • Do not analyse the dream straight away: write one neutral line if needed, then return to grounding or a low-stimulation activity.
  • If nightmares repeat: consider talking with a clinician. Nightmare-focused work, imagery rehearsal/rescripting, trauma therapy, medicine review, or sleep assessment may be appropriate, but it is best done with support if the images are trauma-linked or overwhelming.
My 2am script:
“This is night distress. I am in __________. The date is __________. One safe thing I can do now is __________. If safety drops, I will contact __________.”

Try this now: write: “I do not have to solve this tonight. Tomorrow’s first step is…”

Kete handout · general education · informed by Healthify NZ, 1737, and CCI nightmare/sleep resources · review before formal clinical use.

Crisis plan: what to do when things get unsafe

What this is for: times when distress, suicidal thoughts, self-harm urges, voices, paranoia, substance use, hopelessness, agitation, or exhaustion may make it harder to think clearly.

When not to rely on this alone: if there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111 now or go to the nearest emergency department. If you need urgent mental health support, call or text 1737. A plan is a tool; it is not a substitute for emergency or clinical care.

Fill this out when you are calmer

  • My warning signs: changes in sleep, eating, isolation, agitation, giving up, unsafe urges, intense shame, voices becoming threatening, paranoia increasing, substance use, or feeling trapped.
  • My triggers or pressure points: conflict, grief, anniversaries, substances, trauma reminders, money pressure, pain, loneliness, lack of sleep, or feeling rejected.
  • My reasons to stay safe: people, pets, whānau, values, future moments, faith, culture, responsibilities, unfinished hopes, or “I do not have to decide forever today.”
  • Internal coping strategies: grounding, cold water, safe movement, music, distraction, breathing space, shower, blanket, step outside, simple routine.
  • People and places that help: safe whānau, friends, neighbours, peer support, marae/community supports, public places, clinician, GP, crisis team.
  • Professional supports: GP, key worker, mental health team, crisis team, after-hours service, 1737, Lifeline, Youthline, emergency department, 111.
  • Environmental safety steps: move away from means of harm, ask someone to hold medication or sharp objects if appropriate, avoid alcohol/drugs, stay near safe people, do not drive if unsafe.

If risk escalates

Do not wait to be “sure enough.” Use the next level of support early. Ask someone to stay with you, go to a safer place, call a crisis line, contact your local crisis team, go to emergency care, or call 111 if immediate danger is present.

My not-alone threshold: If I might act on self-harm, suicide, overdose, violence, unsafe driving, or frightening commands, I will not stay alone. I will contact: __________. If immediate danger is present, I will use 111.

Try this now: write one warning sign, one coping action, one support person, and one urgent service.

NZ contacts: 111 for immediate danger. 1737 call/text for free 24/7 support. Manawa — My Own Survival Plan may help with a safety plan, but does not replace urgent help.

Kete handout · informed by Healthify suicide prevention and safety-planning resources · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Self-harm urges: getting through the next wave

What this is for: urges to hurt yourself, punish yourself, feel something when numb, escape shame, relieve pressure, communicate pain, or obey frightening voices.

When not to rely on this alone: if you have injured yourself, taken an overdose, feel unable to stay safe, or may act on urges soon, use urgent support now. In New Zealand call 111 for immediate danger or call/text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support.

Start with understanding, not judgement

Self-harm can become a powerful way of surviving overwhelming feelings. That does not mean it is safe, and it does not mean you are bad. The aim is to increase safety, reduce immediate access to harm, and build more options over time.

First 10 minutes: lower immediate risk

  • Name the urge: “An urge to self-harm is here.” Naming it can create a small gap between the urge and the next action.
  • Delay: set a 5–10 minute timer. You only need to get through this short window first.
  • Move toward people: go near another person, leave the bathroom/bedroom if needed, sit in a shared space, or message someone: “I am not safe alone right now.”
  • Move away from means: put distance between you and objects, medication, alcohol/drugs, driving, heights, or places that increase risk. Ask someone safe to hold items if appropriate.
  • Use body-based coping: cold water on hands, feet on floor, safe movement, holding ice wrapped in cloth, squeezing a towel, or wrapping in a blanket.
  • Use expression: write uncensored thoughts, draw the feeling, rip scrap paper, cry, or say the feeling out loud to a safe person.

Support ladder

Urge levelNext step
Low to moderateDelay, ground, use a safer sensory action, and write what triggered the urge.
Strong or risingDo not stay alone. Contact a support person, key worker, GP, crisis team, or call/text 1737.
Immediate danger, injury, overdose, or unable to stay safeCall 111, go to emergency care, or ask another person to call emergency help and stay with you.

After the wave

Write down what happened before the urge, what you needed, what helped even slightly, and what made risk worse. This can become part of a crisis plan. If self-harm is repeated, escalating, or linked with voices, intoxication, withdrawal, trauma flooding, or feeling trapped, use professional support.

Try this now: write three safer actions, one person who can be near you, one professional support, and one urgent service you can use before acting on an urge.

If you are supporting someone else: listen, ask directly about safety, stay nearby if risk is immediate, reduce access to means where safe, and get backup. Do not promise secrecy when someone may be unsafe.

NZ support: 111 for immediate danger. Call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support. Manawa — My Own Survival Plan may help some people build a survival plan, but it does not replace urgent help.

Kete handout · informed by Healthify self-harm and suicide-prevention guidance, 1737, and NZ safety-planning resources · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Soothing and safety: calming the threat system

What this is for: feeling frightened, ashamed, raw, unsafe, overwhelmed, frozen, disconnected, or stuck in a threat response.

When to use it: after grounding, before sleep, after conflict, after a hard appointment, during shame or grief, or when your body needs a safer signal.

When not to rely on this alone: use crisis, clinical, whānau, peer, GP, counsellor, nurse, or specialist support if risk is escalating, trauma memories are flooding, voices command harm, substances or withdrawal are involved, someone else is making you unsafe, or you cannot stay safe.

Why this helps

Soothing is a learned skill. Some people did not have enough safe soothing from others, or trauma taught the body to stay on alert. Soothing practice is not weakness; it is nervous-system care. The aim is not to force calm. The aim is to give your body repeated evidence that this moment may be safer than the alarm is saying.

Step 1: check safety first

  • Ask: “Am I physically safe enough to use a calming skill right now?”
  • If there is immediate danger, move toward people, light, a safer room, a public place, or emergency help.
  • If another person is threatening, monitoring, stalking, coercing, or pressuring you, ordinary calming may not be enough. Use specialist support and careful safety planning.

Step 2: choose one soothing channel

  • Touch: blanket, warm shower, comfortable clothing, grounding object, pet, hand cream, or weighted pressure if safe.
  • Sound: calm music, nature sounds, a familiar voice, quiet music through one earbud, karakia/prayer if this fits you, or silence if silence feels safe.
  • Sight: soft light, photos of safe people or places, nature, a tidy corner, a candle, plant, or something with a calming colour.
  • Smell/taste: warm drink, mint, soap, safe scent, familiar food, or slowly noticing one bite or sip.
  • Connection: sit near someone safe, send a simple message, visit a public safe place, be near a pet, or contact support.

Step 3: create a small survival kit

Choose 3–5 items: grounding object, photo, note to self, sensory item, music list, crisis numbers, medication reminder if relevant, or a reminder of reasons to keep going. Put it somewhere easy to reach. A kit can be physical, digital, or both.

Example: After an argument, a person notices shame and panic. They sit with a blanket, put calm music quietly through one earbud, hold a smooth stone, text “rough night, can you check in?”, and avoid making big decisions until morning.

Common difficulties

  • “Soothing feels fake.” That is common. Start with neutral comfort rather than trying to feel peaceful.
  • “I do not know what feels safe.” Choose the least bad option: a wall behind you, lights on, feet on floor, cool water, or sitting near another person.
  • “Comfort makes me emotional.” Go smaller. Use 10–20 seconds, keep eyes open, and return to grounding if feelings rise too fast.
  • “I feel undeserving.” Treat soothing as basic care, like water or warmth, not as a reward you have to earn.

Try this now: choose one soothing item, one grounding object, and one safe contact or service. Write where you will keep them.

If this does not work: stop or shrink the exercise. Use grounding, movement, light, food, water, a safer person, or a support service instead. If you might hurt yourself or someone else, are being harmed, or are in immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. For free 24/7 mental health support, call or text 1737.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Mindful pause: noticing without judging

What this is for: stress, autopilot, rumination, emotional reactivity, or needing a short pause before choosing what to do next.

When to use it: when you are safe enough to pause, want to step out of automatic reaction, or need a small bridge between feeling something and doing something.

When not to rely on this alone: if internal focus increases panic, dissociation, trauma memories, voices, paranoia, self-harm urges, or feeling unreal, use eyes-open grounding, movement, another person, or urgent support instead.

Why this helps

Mindfulness is not about emptying the mind or forcing calm. It is a way to notice what is happening without immediately arguing with it, obeying it, or pushing it away. A brief pause can create enough space to choose the next safe step.

Try a 60-second mindful pause

  • Settle your body where it is: sit, stand, walk slowly, hold a warm drink, or place both feet on the floor.
  • Notice: name what is here: “thoughts are racing”, “tight chest”, “sadness”, “anger”, “sound of traffic”, “feet on floor”.
  • Anchor: choose one safe focus: feet, sound, object, breath, hand movement, walking, or the room around you.
  • Return gently: when the mind wanders, say “thinking”, “remembering”, or “worrying”, then come back to the anchor.
  • Widen: look around the room and ask, “What is one useful next step from here?”
Example: “I notice the thought that I have ruined everything. I notice heat in my face. I can feel the chair under me. I am going to drink water before replying.”

Common difficulties

  • “My mind will not stop.” That is expected. The practice is noticing and returning, not stopping thoughts.
  • “My body feels worse when I focus inside.” Use an external anchor: a colour, object, sound, pet, plant, wall, or the feeling of your shoes on the ground.
  • “I feel numb or unreal.” Keep your eyes open, name the date and place, move your body, or use grounding before trying mindfulness again.

Adapt it safely

Keep eyes open. Use external objects rather than body scans. Use walking, stretching, colouring, dishes, gardening, karakia/prayer, waiata/song, or a cup of tea as mindful activities if sitting still is uncomfortable.

Try this now: notice one sound, one colour, one point of contact, and one next step.

If this does not work: stop the exercise. Try grounding, movement, contacting someone safe, or using a different Kete handout. If you might hurt yourself or someone else, or you are in immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. For free 24/7 mental health support, call or text 1737.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Mood early warning signs: noticing shifts sooner

What this is for: noticing mood shifts earlier, especially when changes in sleep, energy, irritability, impulsive decisions, withdrawal, or distress tend to build before things get harder.

When to use it: when you are mostly safe but can tell your mood, energy, sleep, or behaviour is starting to change. It is useful between appointments, after a difficult week, or when trusted people have noticed a pattern before you have.

When not to rely on this alone: seek professional or urgent support early if mood changes include very little sleep without tiredness, racing thoughts, risky behaviour, suicidal thoughts, self-harm urges, command voices, paranoia, violence risk, or feeling out of control.

Why this helps

People often describe mood as “good” or “bad”, but there are many mood states in between. Naming patterns earlier can help you protect sleep, reduce pressure, postpone risky decisions, and involve support before things escalate.

Step 1: notice your early signs

  • Body: restless energy, heavy tiredness, tight chest, appetite changes, pain, agitation, or feeling slowed down.
  • Mind: racing thoughts, hopeless thoughts, suspicious thoughts, shame loops, big plans, or difficulty concentrating.
  • Sleep: sleeping much less, sleeping much more, waking often, staying up late, or not feeling tired when you usually would.
  • Behaviour: spending, messaging more than usual, withdrawing, arguing, driving faster, using more alcohol or drugs, avoiding everything, or taking unusual risks.

Step 2: check what may be making you vulnerable

  • Sleep loss, skipped meals, pain, substances, conflict, trauma reminders, isolation, work stress, family stress, hormones, medication worries, or recent life changes.
  • Use this as information, not blame. The question is: “What would make today a little safer or steadier?”

Step 3: choose one stabilising action

  • Protect sleep: lower stimulation tonight, reduce screens late, avoid alcohol, and keep the next morning simple if possible.
  • Reduce risk: postpone big spending, major messages, major decisions, driving while worked up, or substance use.
  • Use support: text or call one trusted person, GP, nurse, counsellor, peer worker, support line, or crisis service if risk is rising.
  • Return to basics: food, water, shower, medication as already prescribed, fresh air, grounding, and one manageable task.

Short example

A person notices they have slept only four hours, feel unusually wired, and are making big plans late at night. They decide not to send important messages, put their bank card away, text a trusted person, eat something, and make sleep the main priority for the next two nights.

Common difficulties

  • “I only notice once it is already intense.” Start with other people’s observations, sleep changes, or behaviour changes. They are often easier to notice than mood.
  • “I do not want to overreact.” Early action can be small. You are not labelling yourself; you are reducing risk while you gather information.
  • “I feel ashamed.” Try using neutral wording: “This is a pattern I am learning to spot earlier.”

If this does not work

If mood keeps escalating, sleep drops sharply, you feel unsafe, or other people are worried about your behaviour, contact support early. If you might hurt yourself or someone else, or you are in immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. For free 24/7 mental health support, call or text 1737.

Try this now: write two early warning signs, one stabilising action, and one person or service you would contact if the signs continue.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Personal wellbeing plan: one small step in five areas

What this is for: building routine, resilience, connection, and recovery supports without turning self-care into pressure or blame.

When to use it: when life feels thin, disconnected, stuck, stressful, or hard to restart after a rough patch. This works best when the step is small enough to actually do.

When not to rely on this alone: if risk is high, basic needs cannot be met, housing, food, safety, violence, withdrawal, medication concerns, or symptoms are severe, use practical, whānau, peer, GP, counsellor, nurse, or crisis support as well.

Why this helps

Wellbeing is easier to rebuild when it is not treated as one big project. A small plan gives you several gentle entry points: connection, movement, learning, giving, and noticing. The aim is not to do all five perfectly; it is to choose one realistic action that supports life a little.

The Five Ways to Wellbeing

  • Connect: talk, listen, text someone safe, sit with whānau, attend a group, or reconnect with community.
  • Be active: walk, stretch, garden, play, move gently, clean one small area, or use a low-energy version.
  • Keep learning: try a small skill, podcast, course, hobby, waiata/song, recipe, language word, or return to an old interest.
  • Give: thank someone, help briefly, volunteer, share kai, or do a small kindness without overextending.
  • Take notice: notice seasons, breath, nature, kai, sounds, colours, whakapapa/place, or one ordinary good thing.

Make a one-week plan

  1. Choose one area only: connect, be active, keep learning, give, or take notice.
  2. Make it tiny: reduce the action until it can fit your real energy, time, money, transport, pain level, caregiving, shift work, culture, disability, and current stress.
  3. Name the when and where: “after breakfast”, “before bed”, “on the bus”, “after school drop-off”, or “during my lunch break”.
  4. Add support if needed: ask someone to join you, remind you, make it safer, or help remove a barrier.
  5. Review kindly: ask “What made this easier or harder?” rather than “Did I fail?”
Example: “This week I will connect by sending one low-pressure text to my cousin on Sunday afternoon. If I have no energy, I will just send a photo or one sentence.”

Common difficulties

  • “I cannot face people.” Choose indirect connection first: sit near someone safe, send a neutral emoji, reply with one sentence, or be around others without explaining everything.
  • “Movement hurts or exhausts me.” Use the low-energy version: stretch hands, step outside for one minute, change position, or rest deliberately.
  • “Giving turns into overdoing.” Keep giving small and boundaried. Helping should not mean abandoning your own safety, health, or recovery.
  • “I missed the plan.” Treat that as information. Shrink the step, change the timing, or choose a different area.

Try this now: complete: “This week, one small way I will support my wellbeing is _____. I will do it when _____. The smallest version is _____.”

If this does not work: choose a smaller step, switch to grounding, or use a practical support route first. If you feel unsafe, might hurt yourself or someone else, or there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. For free 24/7 mental health support, call or text 1737.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Coping with voices: reducing distress and increasing choice

What this is for: hearing voices, sounds, or messages that are intrusive, critical, distracting, threatening, comforting, confusing, or more active under stress, sleep loss, isolation, or substance use.

When not to rely on this alone: seek urgent support if voices command harm, you feel unable to stay safe, distress is escalating, sleep has collapsed, substances are involved, or you are frightened you may act on what voices say.

A non-judgemental starting point

Some people hear voices or have unusual sensory experiences. Hearing voices is not always the same for everyone. The first aim is usually not to force the experience to disappear; it is to reduce distress, increase choice, and stay connected with support.

Notice patterns without over-analysing

  • When: time of day, how long it lasted, and whether it followed poor sleep, stress, conflict, boredom, silence, alcohol/drugs, or a trauma reminder.
  • Where: home, outside, bed, shower, bus, work, school, social media, or a high-stimulation place.
  • Voice style: critical, threatening, distracting, comforting, commenting, or commanding.
  • Distress level: 0–10, and whether you felt pulled to do something unsafe.
  • What helped: sound, movement, connection, food, sleep, grounding, medication routine if prescribed, or lower stimulation.

Choose a coping option that fits the moment

  • If the voice is intrusive: try competing sound such as music, radio, a podcast, humming, reading aloud, or headphones in one ear.
  • If silence makes it louder: use structured activity — walking, chores, puzzles, drawing, showering, gardening, gaming, or being near another person.
  • If fear is high: orient to the present by naming the room, date, time, and three ordinary objects around you.
  • If the voice is critical: try a short boundary such as, “I hear you, but I am choosing what I do next.” Stop if this increases distress.
  • If the voice gives unsafe commands: do not debate, test, or negotiate with it. Move toward people, reduce access to means, and contact urgent support.
  • If patterns repeat: protect sleep, food, medication routines if prescribed, connection, low stimulation, and early support contact.

Mini support ladder

LevelWhat I noticeNext step
EarlyVoices are present but I can still choose.Use sound, grounding, routine, and write one pattern.
AmberVoices are louder, threatening, or linked with poor sleep, substances, or isolation.Tell one trusted person and contact my GP, key worker, peer support, or mental health team early.
RedVoices command harm, I might act, or I cannot stay safe alone.Do not stay alone. Use urgent support, crisis response, or 111 if immediate danger is present.

If this does not work

Do not blame yourself. Voices can become harder to manage during stress, poor sleep, substance use, trauma reminders, isolation, or relapse. Increase support early rather than waiting until things are unmanageable.

Try this now: write one pattern you have noticed, one safe coping strategy to test, one amber warning sign, and one person or service you could contact early.

NZ supports: Hearing Voices Network Aotearoa NZ, Talking Minds NZ, Healthify psychosis information, 1737, your GP or mental health team. Call 111 if immediate danger is present.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Suspicious or unusual beliefs: staying safe without arguing with yourself

What this is for: feeling watched, targeted, unsafe, controlled, threatened, followed, monitored, or strongly convinced something frightening is happening, especially when this leads to distress, isolation, repeated checking, confrontation urges, unsafe travel, or risky decisions.

When to use it: use this when the belief or fear feels intense enough that you need a pause plan before deciding what to do next. The aim is not to prove yourself wrong. The aim is to reduce escalation, protect safety, and get support.

When not to rely on this alone: seek urgent support if fear is escalating quickly, you may confront someone, you feel unsafe at home, voices are commanding harm, sleep loss is severe, substances are involved, you feel driven to act immediately, or you may harm yourself or someone else.

Start with distress and safety

This handout does not ask you to label your belief as true or false. When fear is intense, direct argument can make distress stronger. A safer first question is: “What helps me stay safe and reduce escalation right now?”

If the urge is to...Try a lower-risk first step
Confront, accuse, message repeatedly, or post publiclyDelay for 30 minutes, write the message in notes without sending it, and contact a trusted support person first.
Check, search, record, follow, drive around, or gather proofChoose one grounding action, move to a calmer place, and set a short timer before making another decision.
Isolate because nobody will understandContact one person using simple distress language: “I’m feeling very unsafe and need help staying calm.”
Stop sleep, food, medication routines, or appointments because of fearProtect the basics first. Eat something small, drink water, rest if possible, and contact your GP, mental health team, pharmacist, or support person before changing routines.

Use the ABC check

  • A — Activating event: what happened? What did I notice? Keep this to observable facts where possible.
  • B — Belief or interpretation: what did my mind conclude? What feels threatening?
  • C — Consequence: how did I feel? What did I want to do next?
  • D — Delay: what can safely wait for 30 minutes?
  • E — Escalation check: would this action make me safer, or could it increase risk, conflict, shame, legal trouble, or harm?

Check vulnerability factors

  • How much sleep have I had in the last two nights?
  • Have stress, conflict, trauma reminders, substances, pain, sensory overload, mood changes, or isolation increased?
  • Have I eaten, had fluids, taken usual medication if prescribed, or contacted support?
  • Have similar fears happened before, and what helped me get through them safely?
  • Is there a trusted person who can sit with me, talk by phone, or help me delay risky actions?

Choose the least escalating action

  • Delay confrontations, accusations, public posts, major decisions, repeated checking, or unsafe travel while fear is high.
  • Move to a safer, calmer, lower-stimulation place if possible.
  • Contact a trusted person and describe your distress rather than trying to prove the whole situation.
  • Use grounding, food and fluids, sleep/stress support, and lower stimulation.
  • Ask, “What is another possible explanation?” only if that feels tolerable. If it does not, focus on the least escalating action.

Thirty-minute pause plan

PauseQuestionExample answer
BodyWhat is my distress level?8/10, heart racing, not slept much.
ActionWhat action might make this worse?Messaging, confronting, posting, driving around to check.
Safer stepWhat can wait 30 minutes?Sit somewhere calmer, drink water, text support, avoid checking.
SupportWho can help me stay steady?Text my sister: “I’m really frightened and need help not acting on it.”

Short example

A person has slept poorly and becomes convinced neighbours are watching them. They want to go outside and confront them. They rate distress at 9/10, move away from the window, put their phone on the table, drink water, text a support person, and delay any contact for 30 minutes. Later they decide to contact their mental health team rather than confronting anyone.

Common difficulties

  • “It feels too urgent to wait.” Treat urgency as a sign to slow the next action down. Choose a tiny delay, such as five minutes, then repeat.
  • “People keep telling me it is not real.” You do not have to debate the belief. Ask for practical help with distress, sleep, grounding, food, transport, or staying away from confrontation.
  • “Checking makes me feel safer for a moment.” Repeated checking can keep the alarm active. Try one check only if needed for basic safety, then shift to support and grounding.
  • “I feel ashamed talking about it.” You can use simple wording: “I’m having frightening thoughts and need help staying safe.” You do not have to explain everything at once.

Try this now: write one ABCDE line and one non-escalating action you can take for the next 30 minutes. Example: “I will delay sending messages, sit somewhere calmer, and contact one trusted support.”

If this does not work: if fear is increasing, you feel driven to confront someone, you are not sleeping, substances are involved, voices are telling you to harm yourself or someone else, or you feel unable to stay safe, contact urgent support. In New Zealand, call 111 if there is immediate danger. Call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support. You can also contact your GP, mental health team, crisis team, whānau, or another trusted support person.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Voices, paranoia, or psychosis: early warning plan

What this is for: a simple plan for noticing early changes before voices, suspiciousness, unusual beliefs, or distress become harder to manage.

Use this when: you have had experiences such as hearing voices, feeling very suspicious, feeling watched or unsafe, or having beliefs that become stronger during stress, sleep loss, substance use, medication changes, or mood changes.

Do not rely on this alone when: voices are telling you to harm yourself or someone else, you feel unable to stay safe, you are not sleeping and feel unusually energised, paranoia is escalating, or other people are worried about immediate risk. In New Zealand, call 111 for immediate danger, contact urgent mental health support, or call/text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support.

1. Notice your early warning signs

  • Sleep: less sleep, broken sleep, staying up all night, sleeping much more, or feeling unusually energised without sleep.
  • Thinking: racing thoughts, suspiciousness, feeling watched, stronger beliefs, difficulty concentrating, or feeling that ordinary events have special meaning.
  • Voices or perceptions: voices louder, more frequent, more threatening, harder to ignore, or other unusual sensory experiences becoming stronger.
  • Behaviour: withdrawing, not eating, not showering, missing appointments, checking repeatedly, using more alcohol/drugs, or feeling unsafe outside.
  • Support feedback: trusted people notice you seem more fearful, sleepless, withdrawn, agitated, confused, or unlike yourself.

2. Use a traffic-light plan

  • Green: usual routine is mostly working. Keep sleep, food, connection, appointments, prescribed treatment routines, and coping skills going.
  • Amber: early warning signs are showing. Lower stimulation, reduce alcohol/drugs, simplify demands, tell one trusted person, and contact support earlier.
  • Red: risk is rising, sleep has collapsed, command voices are present, paranoia is escalating, or you feel unable to stay safe. Use urgent support or crisis response.

3. Choose early stabilisers

  • Lower stimulation: fewer demands, quieter spaces, less conflict, less scrolling, and more routine.
  • Protect sleep: regular wake time, reduce caffeine/alcohol/drugs, and ask for help early if sleep drops.
  • Use grounding: name the room, date, and what you are doing; hold a grounding object; use music or movement.
  • Stay connected: tell one trusted person what is changing before it becomes a crisis.
  • Ask for medicine advice safely: if side effects, missed doses, pregnancy, substance use, or stopping medication are involved, speak with your prescriber, GP, pharmacist, or mental health team rather than changing it alone.

4. Write the contact threshold

If I notice…For this long…I will contact…
Voices louder or more threateningMore than ___ day(s), or sooner if unsafe___
Sleep drops or I stay up most of the night___ night(s)___
Suspicion or fear makes me want to confront, flee, or isolateSame day___
Command voices, immediate danger, or I cannot stay safeNow111 / crisis support / safe person

Try this now

Write one green sign, one amber sign, one red sign, three stabilisers, and one person or service you would contact early.

Safety note: this handout supports early planning. It does not replace assessment, medication advice, crisis response, or support from your mental health team.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Te Whare Tapa Whā reflection: a wellbeing check-in

What this is for: a simple reflection on wellbeing across different parts of life. It can be used alone, with whānau, or with a clinician/support worker.

Important note: Te Whare Tapa Whā is a Māori model of health developed by Tā Mason Durie. This handout is a basic reflection tool only. It is not a cultural assessment, Māori-led care, or a substitute for clinical, cultural, whānau, kaumātua, marae-based, iwi, or local support.

The wharenui image

Te Whare Tapa Whā uses the image of a wharenui. The four taha are often described as the walls of the house: tinana, hinengaro, whānau, and wairua. Many current wellbeing resources also use whenua — connection to land, place, and belonging — as the foundation. If one area is under strain, the whole structure may feel less steady.

The taha

  • Taha tinana — physical wellbeing: sleep, food, movement, pain, medication routine if prescribed, substance use, body health, rest.
  • Taha hinengaro — mental and emotional wellbeing: thoughts, feelings, stress, concentration, coping skills, self-talk, creativity, learning.
  • Taha whānau — family and social wellbeing: whānau, friends, support people, belonging, conflict, loneliness, safe connection, community.
  • Taha wairua — spiritual wellbeing: meaning, values, identity, culture, faith, whakapapa, purpose, whenua, nature, hope. This does not need to mean religion unless that fits the person.
  • Whenua — foundation: connection to place, land, home, community, ancestry, or spaces where you feel grounded.

Using this respectfully

  • Use the model as an invitation, not a test. People connect with culture, whānau, wairua, whenua, and identity in different ways.
  • Do not assume every Māori person wants the same kind of support. Ask what fits, who should be involved, and what would feel respectful.
  • If this brings up grief, disconnection, colonisation, family pain, or spiritual distress, slow down and involve culturally appropriate support if wanted.

Reflection questions

  • Which taha feels strongest at the moment?
  • Which taha feels most under pressure?
  • What has helped this area before?
  • Who should be involved, if anyone? This could include whānau, a trusted friend, kaumātua, cultural advisor, GP, clinician, peer worker, or Māori-led service.
  • What is one small step I could take in the next day or week?

One-small-step plan

Choose one taha only. Pick a step small enough to be realistic: drink water, contact someone safe, attend a group, spend time outside, pray/karakia if this fits you, reconnect with culture, rest, book an appointment, or ask for help.

If this brings up more than expected

Reflection can sometimes bring up grief, disconnection, trauma, family conflict, shame, or spiritual distress. If that happens, pause the exercise, use grounding, and consider support from someone safe or culturally appropriate. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. For free 24/7 mental health support, call or text 1737.

Try this now: complete the sentence: “One area I want to care for gently this week is…”

Respect note: use this framework with humility. For many people, wellbeing is collective as well as individual. Māori-led services, whānau, kaumātua, cultural advisors, marae-based support, or local iwi services may be important where appropriate.

Kete handout · general education · informed by NZ Te Whare Tapa Whā and kaupapa Māori wellbeing resources · review before formal clinical use.

Lifestyle foundations for mood: one small base-layer step

What this is for: supporting mood, stress, sleep, energy, recovery, and relapse prevention through everyday foundations. This is not about blame, perfection, weight loss, dieting, or forcing a lifestyle overhaul.

When not to rely on this alone: use professional support if depression is severe, safety is uncertain, sleep has collapsed, high energy/risk-taking is present, eating feels unsafe, substances are causing harm, housing/food security is unstable, pain or disability limits activity, or medication concerns are involved.

Why this helps

Everyday foundations can make symptoms easier or harder to manage. Sleep, light, movement, food, connection, routine, meaningful roles, nature, and substance/caffeine patterns all affect the nervous system. This does not mean distress is your fault. It means there may be one small lever that can support the rest of your care.

Choose one foundation

FoundationSmall, realistic version
Morning lightOpen curtains, sit near a bright window, or step outside for two minutes.
MovementWalk to the letterbox, stretch in a chair, garden briefly, dance to one song, or use safe movement that fits pain, disability, fatigue, pregnancy, or fitness level.
Food and fluidsAdd one regular meal, water, protein-rich food, fruit, vegetable, legumes, yoghurt, eggs, nuts/seeds, fish, or another affordable option.
ConnectionText one person, sit near safe people, reconnect with whānau, join one low-pressure activity, or ask for practical help.
Green or blue spaceSpend a few minutes near a park, garden, tree, river, beach, sky, plant, or open window.
RoutineAnchor one part of the day: wake time, shower, medication as prescribed, meal, walk, school run, work/study step, or bedtime cue.
Substances, smoking/vaping, caffeineNotice timing and effects. Get support before big changes if withdrawal, medication, pregnancy, psychosis, mania, or safety risk is involved.

Three-day experiment

Pick one tiny change and test it for three days. The aim is information, not perfection.

DayOne small actionWhat changed?
1
2
3

Make it kind and realistic

  • Start smaller than you think: two minutes counts if that is what is possible today.
  • Adapt to your body: use chair movement, pacing, rest breaks, mobility aids, support people, or a health professional if needed.
  • Do not use lifestyle as self-blame: poverty, trauma, shift work, caring roles, disability, pain, medication side effects, and unsafe housing can all make foundations harder.
  • Look for patterns: ask, “What gives me 5% more steadiness?” rather than “What fixes everything?”

Try this now: choose one foundation, one tiny action, and one time you will try it.

NZ note: for smoking or vaping support, Quitline offers free NZ support by phone, text, and online. For alcohol/drug concerns, contact Alcohol Drug Helpline. For medication questions, talk with a GP, pharmacist, prescriber, psychiatrist, midwife, or mental health team before making changes.

Kete handout · informed by NZ public health and wellbeing sources · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Mood levers map: one practical place to start

What this is for: low mood, flatness, stress, irritability, fatigue, or loss of routine where “healthy habits” advice feels too vague or blaming.

When to use it: use this when you can tell something is off, but you are not sure where to start. It is especially useful when everything feels too big and you need one small, practical experiment.

When not to rely on this alone: use professional, whānau, peer, GP, nurse, counsellor, or crisis support if mood is severe, persistent, unsafe, mixed with unusually high energy or risk-taking, affected by substances or withdrawal, or making daily functioning very difficult.

Why this helps

Mood is affected by thoughts and life events, but also by body systems: sleep, light, movement, food, stress, connection, routine, substances, and meaningful roles. This does not mean distress is your fault. It means there may be one lever you can gently adjust.

The aim is not to fix everything at once. The aim is to find one small place where effort is possible, then watch whether it gives even a slight shift.

Choose one lever

LeverSmall versionGood first cue
SleepSame wake time for two days; morning light; reduce late caffeine.After waking, open curtains or step outside briefly.
MovementWalk to the letterbox, stretch while the kettle boils, or move to one song.Before lunch, do two minutes rather than waiting for motivation.
FoodAdd one regular meal, protein, fruit, vegetable, legumes, yoghurt, eggs, nuts, or another affordable option.Choose the easiest meal to make slightly steadier.
ConnectionText one person, sit near safe people, attend one group briefly, or ask for help.Send a low-pressure message: “No need to fix it. Can you check in later?”
Green/blue spaceTwo minutes near a tree, garden, river, beach, sky, plant, or open window.Pair it with tea, coffee, a smoke break, or taking rubbish out.
RoutineOne anchor: wake, shower, medication as prescribed, meal, walk, or bedtime cue.Pick one anchor you can repeat even on a low day.
Substances/caffeine/nicotineNotice timing and effects. Get support before major changes if medication, withdrawal, or safety is involved.Write down what you used, when, and what mood did afterwards.

Step-by-step

  1. Circle the lever that feels most possible, not the one you think you “should” choose.
  2. Make the action smaller than your first idea. Two minutes counts.
  3. Link it to something already happening, such as waking, boiling the kettle, feeding a pet, taking medication as prescribed, or getting into bed.
  4. Try it once today or tomorrow. Do not wait until you feel ready.
  5. Afterwards, rate mood, energy, or steadiness from 0–10. A shift from 2 to 3 still matters.
  6. Repeat for two or three days before deciding whether it helps.

Short example

A person feels flat, sleeps late, skips breakfast, and avoids messages. They choose one lever: morning light. For the next three mornings, they open the curtains, stand near the door for two minutes, and eat something simple before checking their phone. They do not feel suddenly better, but mornings become slightly less stuck.

Common difficulties

  • “This is too basic.” Basic does not mean pointless. Low mood often improves through repeated small anchors, not one big insight.
  • “I missed a day.” Restart with the next small cue. The plan has not failed.
  • “I picked too much.” Cut the step in half, then in half again.
  • “Nothing changed.” Try a different lever, or ask someone you trust to help you choose one that is more realistic.

If this does not work

If mood is worsening, you are not sleeping for long periods, you feel unusually energised or impulsive, you are using substances to get through, or daily life is becoming hard to manage, this handout should sit alongside real support rather than replace it. Contact a GP, nurse, counsellor, peer support, whānau, or another trusted support person.

Safety note: If there is immediate danger, call 111 in New Zealand. If you might harm yourself or someone else, feel unable to stay safe, or need urgent mental health support, call or text 1737 for free 24/7 support, and involve a trusted person or local crisis support.

Try this now: pick one lever, one tiny action, one cue, and one time to review whether it helped.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Choosing a self-help resource: one useful next step

What this is for: choosing one practical website, workbook, app, handout, or support option without getting lost in too many tabs, links, or opinions.
