When to use it: use this when you know you want help, but you are not sure where to start, or when resource-searching has become a way of avoiding the first small step.

When not to rely on self-help alone: use professional or urgent support if symptoms are severe, risky, worsening, linked with psychosis/mania, eating disorder risk, trauma flooding, addiction withdrawal, self-harm, suicidal thoughts, family violence, unsafe housing, or inability to manage basic needs.

Why this helps

When distress is high, more information can make things harder. A useful resource is not the most popular one. It is the one that matches the problem, feels usable today, and points toward a safe next step.

Step 1: name what you need today

NeedGood fitBe careful if…
Calm my body nowA grounding, breathing, distress-tolerance, or sensory safety tool.You keep reading about skills but do not practise one.
Understand a patternA CBT, ACT, DBT, sleep, alcohol-and-mood, or mood-tracking worksheet.The worksheet turns into self-criticism or rumination.
Make a planA crisis plan, relapse plan, problem-solving sheet, or appointment-prep handout.The risk is active now and you need a real person, not a worksheet.
Find supportA trusted NZ helpline, GP, peer support, whānau/support person, counsellor, nurse, or local service.You feel unsafe, pressured, monitored, or unable to wait.

Step 2: choose by need, not by popularity

  • I want a structured NZ CBT course: Just a Thought, or Beating the Blues for adults with depression/anxiety.
  • I need a quick calming tool: Smallsteps, grounding, breathing space, sensory safety, or a distress tolerance handout.
  • I want printable worksheets: Centre for Clinical Interventions, Therapist Aid, Get Self Help, ACT Mindfully, or DBT worksheet resources.
  • I am young or supporting a young person: SPARX, Headstrong, The Lowdown, or Youthline may fit better.
  • I need a safety plan: write a crisis plan, consider Manawa — My Own Survival Plan, and include real people/services.
  • I hear voices or feel paranoid: consider Hearing Voices Network Aotearoa NZ, Talking Minds NZ, Healthify, Get Self Help psychosis resources, and professional support.
  • I want local connection: peer groups, community wellbeing courses such as Reframe Wānanga in Ōtautahi Christchurch, nature/community volunteering, or trusted local supports.
  • I want lifestyle support: start with one foundation: sleep, movement, food regularity, social connection, green/blue space, smoking/vaping support, or a gradual return to work/study/volunteering.

Step 3: do a quick privacy and safety check

  • Does the resource ask for personal health information? Can you use it without sharing more than you want to?
  • Can you use it privately, without posting publicly or joining a group you do not trust?
  • Does it include crisis guidance or a clear “get help now” route?
  • Is it age-appropriate, culturally respectful, and relevant enough for Aotearoa New Zealand?
  • Does it support practical action, or does it mainly keep you reading, scrolling, comparing, or judging yourself?

Step 4: use the “one resource for one week” rule

Problem I am choosing one resource for: ________________________________
Resource I will try: ________________________________
When I will use it: ________________________________
A useful-enough result would be: ________________________________

Use one resource for one week unless it is clearly unsafe, shaming, confusing, or not relevant. More resources do not always mean more progress.

Short example

“I keep opening ten tabs about anxiety and then doing nothing. This week I will use one grounding handout after dinner on Monday, Wednesday, and Friday. Useful enough means I practise for two minutes, not that anxiety disappears.”

Common difficulties

  • “I want the perfect resource.” Choose “good enough for this week.” You can review later.
  • “I collect resources but do not use them.” Print one page or save one link only. Put the rest away.
  • “The resource makes me feel worse.” Stop, ground, and choose a gentler or more practical option. A helpful resource should not shame you.
  • “I need more than self-help.” That is not failure. Self-help works best alongside real support when risk, complexity, or distress is high.

If this does not work

If you keep searching but cannot start, choose support before more information. Ask someone you trust, a GP, counsellor, nurse, peer worker, helpline, or support person to help you choose one next step. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. If you need to talk with someone now, call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support.

Try this now: choose one problem, one resource, and one time you will use it. Make the first use less than ten minutes.

NZ crisis reminder: 111 for immediate danger. 1737 call/text for free 24/7 mental health support.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Social anxiety: stepping back into life

What this is for: fear of being judged, embarrassed, criticised, watched, rejected, or seen as awkward. Social anxiety can happen before, during, and after social situations.

When to use it: use this when the situation is uncomfortable but basically safe, and you want to rebuild confidence gradually rather than wait until anxiety disappears.

When not to rely on this alone: use professional or urgent support if anxiety is severely limiting life, if alcohol or drugs are being used to get through social situations, if bullying, discrimination, coercion, stalking, family violence, self-harm, suicidal thoughts, psychosis, mania, trauma flooding, or severe depression are also affecting safety.

Why this helps

Social anxiety is often maintained by a loop: threat predictions, body symptoms, self-focused attention, safety behaviours, avoidance, and replaying things afterwards. The aim is not to force confidence. The aim is to take small, safe steps that let your brain gather new information.

Map the loop

Part of the loopWhat to write
SituationWhat social situation feels hard?
PredictionWhat do you fear people will think, notice, or do?
BodyWhat sensations show up: heat, shaking, blank mind, nausea, tight chest, sweating?
AttentionDo you focus on yourself, other people’s reactions, escape routes, or the task?
Safety behaviourDo you rehearse, hide, over-explain, avoid eye contact, leave early, drink, scroll, or stay silent?
AfterwardsDo you replay, criticise yourself, or look for proof it went badly?

Choose one small practice step

  • Pick a safe situation: choose discomfort, not danger. Do not practise with someone who is threatening, coercive, violent, or unsafe.
  • Make it small: say hello, ask one question, stay five minutes longer, make one short call, attend the first part of a group, or send one ordinary message.
  • Drop one safety behaviour: for example, reduce over-rehearsing, look up once, ask one question without apologising, or stay present instead of checking your phone.
  • Focus outward: notice the task, the other person’s words, the room, or the conversation topic rather than monitoring yourself.
  • Review fairly: ask, “What did I learn?” rather than “Did I feel calm?”

Practice ladder

Step sizeExampleMy version
Very smallSmile, say kia ora, or make brief eye contact.
SmallAsk a shop worker one simple question.
MediumStay at a group, class, hui, or meeting for the first 10–15 minutes.
LargerSpeak once in a group or arrange a short catch-up.

Example

Situation: replying in a group chat. Prediction: “They will think I sound stupid.” Practice step: send one short, ordinary reply without rewriting it five times. Review: “No one reacted badly. Anxiety was high at first, then dropped.”

Common difficulties

  • “I still felt anxious.” Feeling anxious does not mean the step failed. Look for learning, not perfect calm.
  • “I replayed it all night.” Set a five-minute review window, write one fair learning point, then shift to grounding or sleep support.
  • “The step was too hard.” Shrink it until it feels possible enough. Repeating a tiny step is still practice.
  • “I used alcohol or substances to cope.” Make the next step safer and smaller, and consider support if this is becoming a pattern.

If this does not work

The step may be too big, too vague, or too focused on performing well. Try a smaller step, practise with a safer person, reduce only one safety behaviour at a time, or ask a GP, counsellor, nurse, peer worker, or trusted support person to help you plan the ladder.

Try this now: write one social situation you avoid and one 5-minute version of that situation you could practise safely this week.

Safety note: this is general education. Social practice is not a substitute for support when there is danger, discrimination, coercion, violence, severe depression, psychosis, mania, trauma flooding, self-harm risk, or suicidal thoughts. In New Zealand, call 111 if there is immediate danger. Call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support.

Kete handout · informed by CCI social anxiety materials · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Social anxiety experiments: predictions, safety behaviours, and stepladders

What this is for: testing social fears in a planned, gradual way instead of relying only on anxious predictions, avoidance, or replaying the situation afterwards.

When to use it: use this when a social situation is uncomfortable but basically safe, and you want to test one prediction or practise one small step.

When not to rely on this alone: use support if the practice involves genuine danger, workplace conflict, bullying, discrimination, trauma reminders, intoxication, self-harm risk, pressure to have contact with someone unsafe, or situations where safety planning is needed.

Why this helps

Social anxiety often treats predictions as facts: “I will blush,” “they will notice,” “I will sound stupid,” or “I will be rejected.” Experiments are a way to gather real-world information slowly. The aim is not to force confidence. The aim is to practise, reduce one safety behaviour a little, and review what actually happened.

Step 1: make the prediction specific

Instead of “it will go badly,” write the exact fear: “They will laugh,” “I will blush and everyone will notice,” “I will blank out,” or “they will think I am boring.” Rate belief from 0–100.

Step 2: identify safety behaviours

Safety behaviours are things you do to stop the feared outcome, such as avoiding eye contact, rehearsing every sentence, staying silent, over-apologising, checking your phone, hiding symptoms, using alcohol, or leaving early. The same behaviour can be helpful or unhelpful depending on why you use it.

Step 3: plan one small experiment

  • What will I do? Keep it small, specific, and repeatable.
  • Where and when? Choose a safe enough place and a realistic time.
  • What am I testing? Write one prediction, not every worry at once.
  • Which safety behaviour will I reduce slightly? Do not remove every support at once.
  • What evidence will I look for? Notice what people actually do, not only what anxiety says they might be thinking.
  • How will I support myself afterwards? Plan a grounding step, a kind review, or a text to someone safe.

Step 4: use a stepladder

List steps from easier to harder. Start with something challenging but doable. Repeat steps as needed. The purpose is learning and confidence, not forcing yourself into the hardest situation.

Easier step: ________ · Medium step: ________ · Harder step for later: ________

Example

Prediction: “If I ask one question in the meeting, everyone will think I am awkward.” Experiment: ask one short factual question and keep both feet on the floor while listening to the answer. Safety behaviour to reduce: do not apologise before asking. Review: “Two people looked at me, one answered normally, nobody laughed. Anxiety stayed high for a few minutes, then dropped.”

Common difficulties

  • The step is too big: shrink it until it feels uncomfortable but possible.
  • You review only the awkward parts: include neutral and helpful evidence too.
  • You use alcohol or substances to get through it: treat that as a sign to use support and choose a safer step.
  • You keep replaying afterwards: set a short review time, write one learning point, then move attention to the next task.
  • The situation was genuinely unkind or unsafe: do not turn this into self-blame. Use support, boundaries, or safety planning.

If this does not work

The experiment may be too large, too public, too focused on performing well, or too close to a trauma or bullying experience. Pause, ground, and choose a smaller or safer step. If anxiety is severely limiting work, study, relationships, leaving the house, or daily life, consider support from a GP, counsellor, psychologist, nurse, peer worker, or trusted support person.

Try this now: write one prediction, one safety behaviour, and one tiny experiment that tests the prediction without removing every support.

Safety note: if you might harm yourself or someone else, feel unable to stay safe, or are in immediate danger, call 111 in New Zealand. For free 24/7 mental health support, call or text 1737.

Kete handout · informed by CCI behavioural experiment, safety behaviour, attention, and stepladder modules · general education.

Interpersonal distress: pause, ground, navigate

What this is for: creating a safer pause when conflict, criticism, rejection fears, shame, anger, or urgent messages pull you toward reacting quickly.

When to use it: when your body feels flooded, you want to defend, chase, withdraw, attack, over-explain, apologise too quickly, or send a message immediately.

When not to rely on it alone: if there is abuse, coercion, violence, stalking, sexual pressure, monitoring, threats, or immediate danger, prioritise safety and specialist support. If you are in immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111.

Why this helps

Interpersonal distress can switch the body into threat mode. In threat mode, the mind often looks for speed, certainty, or control. A pause does not mean ignoring the problem. It gives your nervous system time to settle enough to choose a safer next move: repair, a boundary, a request, more time, or safety support.

Try this step by step

  • Notice flooding signs: racing heart, tunnel vision, urgency, replaying words, shaking, numbness, or being unable to consider another view.
  • Pause clearly if it is safe: “I want to talk about this. I need time to settle so I can respond properly.”
  • Do not rehearse the argument during the pause: use grounding, safe movement, paced breathing, cold water, a shower, or sensory soothing.
  • Separate feeling from story: What am I feeling? What story is my mind adding? What do I actually know?
  • Choose your aim: repair, clarity, boundary, request, apology, safety, support, or more time.
  • Write one sentence only: keep it brief, specific, and respectful where possible.

Repair after conflict

Repair does not mean taking all the blame, excusing harm, or rushing back into a conversation before you are safe. A repair step is a small attempt to reduce damage, clarify what matters, or return to respectful contact where that is appropriate.

Repair stepPlain-language prompt
Name your part“The part I can own is…”
Name the impact“I can see this may have affected you by…”
Ask, do not demand“Would it help if I…?” or “What would make the next conversation safer?”
Set a boundary if needed“I want to repair this, and I also need us not to yell / threaten / message repeatedly.”
Choose one next actionApologise, clarify, pause, make a plan, seek support, or come back later.

Short example

A person receives a message that feels rejecting. Their first urge is to send five paragraphs. They pause for 20 minutes, ground with a cold drink, and write: “I felt hurt reading that. I want to understand what you meant, but I need to talk about it later when I am calmer.” Later, when settled, they add: “I can see my last message came across harshly. I am sorry for that. Can we restart this tomorrow?”

Common difficulties

  • “If I pause, they will leave.” Try a brief holding message rather than a full conversation: “I am not ignoring this. I need time to respond properly.”
  • “I keep replaying the argument.” Move attention to the room, body, or a practical task for a set time. Rehearsing often keeps the threat system switched on.
  • “I cannot tell what is true.” Write facts in one column and interpretations in another. You do not have to solve it immediately.
  • “I apologise for everything.” Slow down. Try owning one specific part without accepting blame for someone else’s behaviour.
  • “Repair would put me at risk.” Do not use ordinary repair tools where there is intimidation, control, violence, monitoring, or fear. Use safety planning and specialist support instead.

If this does not work

If the conversation keeps escalating, if you feel unsafe, or if there are urges to harm yourself or someone else, move away from the conversation and contact support. Call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support in New Zealand, contact a trusted person, or call 111 if there is immediate danger.

Try this now: before replying to a difficult message, write four words: feeling, story, aim, sentence. Then draft one short sentence and wait before sending it. If repair is appropriate, add one specific repair sentence later, after your body has settled.

Kete handout · general education.

Social connection: one small safe step

What this is for: loneliness, isolation, low mood, grief, withdrawal, social anxiety, or feeling cut off from people, whānau, culture, work, study, or community.

When to use it: when you want more safe contact but the idea of reaching out feels awkward, risky, pointless, or too big.

When not to rely on this alone: if a relationship is unsafe, controlling, monitored, sexually harmful, violent, or threatening, prioritise specialist support, privacy, and safety planning rather than ordinary relationship repair. If you feel unable to stay safe, call 111 in New Zealand or call/text 1737 for support.

Why this helps

Loneliness is not a personal failure. It can affect mental and physical wellbeing, and it often makes contact feel harder. Small, repeated contact can rebuild belonging and make it easier to ask for support when distress is high. The aim is not to become instantly social; it is to create one safe thread of connection.

Choose the right size of connection

Connection sizeExamplesWhen this may fit
Nearby but not talkingSit in a library, café, park, class, marae, church, community space, or shared work area.When direct conversation feels too much.
Low-pressure contactSend an emoji, reply with one sentence, wave, say kia ora, or react to a message.When you want contact without a long exchange.
Shared activityWalk with someone, attend a group, volunteer, do a class, cook, garden, game, or watch something together.When talking face-to-face feels intense.
Direct supportAsk someone to check in, come with you, sit nearby, help with a task, or listen for ten minutes.When distress is higher and you need support, not just company.

Try this step by step

  • Name what is missing: one close person, whānau, shared activity, culture, faith, work/study, neighbourhood, peer support, or simply being around people.
  • Choose the safest option: a trusted person, whānau member, group, library, peer support space, marae, faith community, club, class, volunteering option, or low-pressure public place.
  • Make it tiny: send one message, reply to one text, sit near people for ten minutes, say hello to one person, or look up one local group.
  • Reduce the awkwardness barrier: ask someone to go with you, phone first, use a script, arrive for a short time, choose an activity with a shared focus, or plan an exit time.
  • Repeat before judging it: one contact attempt may feel flat. Try the same-size step a few times before deciding it is useless.
  • Review gently: ask what helped, what felt hard, and what the next smallest repeatable step could be.
Script: “Kia ora, I have been a bit quiet lately. No pressure, but it would be good to catch up sometime.”
Example: A person feels too low to meet a friend for coffee. They send one message instead: “I am a bit flat today. Could you send me a quick photo of what you are up to?” Later in the week, they sit in the library for twenty minutes so they are around people without needing to explain anything.

Common difficulties

  • “I do not want to burden people.” Ask for something small and specific: a short text, a ten-minute walk, help with one task, or sitting together quietly.
  • “Everyone already has people.” Look for structured spaces where new contact is expected: classes, volunteering, peer groups, community events, libraries, faith/culture groups, or interest groups.
  • “I tried once and felt worse.” Review the size and safety of the step. It may have been too intense, the wrong person, or the wrong setting.
  • “Social anxiety gets in the way.” Use a stepladder or behavioural experiment. Practise smaller contact before jumping into the hardest situation.
  • “Whānau contact is complicated.” Connection does not have to mean the most painful relationship. Choose safer whānau, friends, peers, community, cultural, or professional supports.

If this does not work

If isolation is worsening, daily life is becoming hard to manage, or you are using alcohol, drugs, gambling, self-harm, or risky situations to cope with loneliness, bring in support rather than trying to solve it alone. Contact a GP, counsellor, peer support, community service, trusted person, or call/text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support in New Zealand. If there is immediate danger, call 111.

Try this now: choose one person, place, or group. Write one contact step that takes less than ten minutes and one smaller backup step if that feels too hard.

Kete handout · informed by Health NZ loneliness and social isolation guidance · general education.

Self-compassion and the inner critic

What this is for: responding to harsh self-talk, shame, guilt, perfectionism, or “I should be over this” thoughts without pretending everything is fine.

When to use it: when the way you speak to yourself is making it harder to repair, rest, ask for help, or take one useful step.

When not to rely on it alone: if self-hatred is linked with self-harm urges, suicidal thoughts, trauma flooding, unsafe relationships, or feeling unable to stay safe, use support as well. In New Zealand, call 111 if there is immediate danger, or call/text 1737 for free 24/7 support.

Why this helps

A harsh inner critic can feel like it is trying to protect you from mistakes, rejection, or failure. But attack often increases threat and makes problem-solving harder. Self-compassion is not letting yourself off the hook; it is a steadier way to face what is hard and choose the next safe step.

Try this step by step

  • Write the critic’s exact words: use the real sentence, such as “I always mess things up.”
  • Name the tone: harsh, frightened, impatient, shaming, perfectionistic, or protective.
  • Notice the function: ask, “What is this critic trying to prevent or control?”
  • Shift to a fair-coach voice: kind enough to reduce threat, honest enough to support action.
  • Use one sentence: “This is hard, and attacking myself will not help. What is one safe next step?”
  • Pair it with action: rest, repair, grounding, food, shower, asking for help, apologising, booking an appointment, or one small task.

Example

Critic: “I’m useless. I ruined everything.”
Fair-coach response: “I am upset and I made a mistake. Attacking myself will not fix it. I can send one repair message, take a break, then review what to do next.”

Common difficulties

  • “It feels fake.” Start with respectful neutrality: “I am a person having a hard moment.”
  • “I do not deserve kindness.” Try fairness instead of kindness: “What response would help me act safely and responsibly?”
  • “I need to punish myself or I will not change.” Ask whether punishment has helped you learn, repair, or stay safe in the past.

If this does not work

Use grounding first if shame is flooding your body. Talk it through with a trusted person if you keep looping. If the critic is linked with trauma, abuse, self-harm, or not wanting to be alive, this handout is not enough on its own; use professional, whānau, peer, GP, nurse, counsellor, or crisis support.

Try this now: rewrite one self-critical sentence as if you were speaking to someone you care about. If kindness feels too hard, make it fair, brief, and practical.

Kete handout · general education · review before formal clinical use.

Sensory safety and neurodivergent distress

What this is for: planning for overload, shutdown, meltdown, ADHD overwhelm, autistic distress, rejection-sensitive spirals, or times when words are hard.

When to use it: before crisis, when you notice sensory, sleep, social, demand, transition, notification, or body-warning signs. It can also help after an overload episode, when you want to understand what made things harder and what might make next time safer.

When not to rely on it alone: if there is intent, planning, inability to stay safe, dangerous impulsivity, command voices, escalating paranoia, family violence, unsafe housing, intoxication, withdrawal risk, or immediate danger, call 111 in New Zealand or use crisis support now. This handout is not a substitute for assessment, disability support, whānau support, workplace/school adjustments, or urgent mental health care when risk is high.

Why this helps

Overload is easier to prevent than to untangle at full intensity. A written plan can reduce the need to explain yourself while distressed. It also gives support people clear, respectful ways to help without crowding, arguing, questioning, or adding more demands.

Early signs

  • Noise, light, touch, smell, crowds, conversation, or notifications start feeling sharper or harder to filter.
  • Words become harder, decisions feel too much, or small demands feel suddenly impossible.
  • You notice pacing, rocking, shutdown, withdrawal, impulsive messages, irritability, detachment, or sudden hopelessness.
  • You become more sensitive to rejection, criticism, changes of plan, waiting, hunger, pain, tiredness, or unclear expectations.

Map the load

AreaWhat adds load?What lowers load?
SensoryNoise, light, smells, texture, crowdsDim light, quiet space, headphones, sunglasses, softer clothing
DemandToo many tasks, questions, choices, messagesOne step, fewer words, written instructions, postponing non-urgent decisions
BodyHunger, pain, heat, tiredness, medication side effects, illnessDrink, food, bathroom, warmth/coolness, rest, GP or clinical advice if needed
SocialConflict, pressure to talk, being watched, sudden changesLow-demand contact, text instead of speech, clear exit plan, trusted person

Downshift plan

  1. Reduce input: lower light, reduce noise, move away from crowds, use headphones, sunglasses, soft clothing, or a quieter space.
  2. Reduce demands: postpone non-urgent decisions, messages, chores, explanations, appointments, or app notifications until the overload drops.
  3. Use predictable steps: follow a three-step plan: safe place, body support, one contact. Keep it written somewhere easy to find.
  4. Use regulation supports: movement, pressure, warmth/coolness, weighted items, fidgets, music, silence, or a familiar object if those help you.
  5. Increase safety: move away from means of harm, substances, driving, conflict, unsafe websites, public arguments, or impulsive spending/messages if control is dropping.
  6. Restart gently: choose one drink, food step, rest step, message, or practical task. Do not restart everything at once.
Example: “When the supermarket is loud and I start snapping or going blank, I will leave the aisle, put headphones on, text ‘overloaded — need quiet, not questions,’ sit in the car or outside for five minutes, drink water, then decide whether to finish with a shorter list or go home.”

Overload text template

I am overloaded and words are hard. Please do not ask lots of questions. I need: [quiet / space / a lift / help leaving / food / water / someone nearby / no advice right now]. I will message again when I can.

Common difficulties

  • “People think I am being rude.” Use a pre-written text or card when you are calm: “When I go quiet, I am overloaded, not ignoring you.”
  • “I only notice when it is already too late.” Track one early body sign this week, such as jaw tension, heat, noise sensitivity, fast scrolling, or wanting to flee.
  • “My plan has too many steps.” Make it smaller: leave, reduce sound/light, contact one person.
  • “Support people crowd me.” Write what helps and what does not help. For example: “Please use fewer words, offer choices, and avoid touching me unless I ask.”
App and reminder note: digital tools can help with routines and organisation, but too many reminders, colours, notifications, or goals can increase overload. Adjust or pause tools that create pressure.
If this does not work: reduce demands further and involve support earlier. If overload is linked with self-harm urges, unsafe conflict, voices telling you to act, escalating paranoia, substance withdrawal, violence, or inability to stay safe, use support now rather than trying to self-manage. In New Zealand, call 111 for immediate danger or call/text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support.

Try this now: write one overload text you could send when speaking is hard, then choose one low-input place you can move to if distress rises.

Kete handout · informed by Healthify sensory-processing and ADHD app-safety guidance · general education.

Choice point: moving toward what matters

What this is for: choosing a small values-based action when thoughts, feelings, urges, or avoidance are pulling strongly.

When to use it: when one option gives short-term relief but makes life smaller, and another option is harder but closer to safety, care, connection, health, repair, or the kind of person you want to be.

When not to rely on it alone: values-based action is not forcing yourself through danger, exhaustion, trauma flooding, or unsafe relationships. Sometimes the toward move is rest, contacting support, setting a boundary, taking medication as prescribed, eating something, or leaving a risky situation.

Why this helps

Distress often narrows attention toward the fastest way to reduce discomfort. A choice point does not remove the discomfort. It helps you notice the fork in the road, name the pull of avoidance or impulse, and choose one small action that keeps life from getting smaller.

Mini values card sort

Circle or mark the words that still matter to you today. You do not have to feel inspired by them. Choose words that point to the kind of life, relationship, recovery, or next step you want to move toward.

Care and connectionStability and healthMeaning and identityAction and repair
kindness, whānau, friendship, honesty, belonging, manaakitangasafety, rest, food, sleep, medication as prescribed, routine, sobriety, movementculture, whakapapa, faith, learning, creativity, nature, dignity, purposecourage, responsibility, boundaries, repair, asking for help, finishing one small task

Choose one word only. Then ask: “What is the smallest safe action that would point in this direction today?”

Steps

  • Name the moment: write the situation in plain words. “I feel criticised,” “I want to cancel,” “I want to send a sharp message,” or “I am avoiding the form.”
  • Choose one value word: use the mini card sort above or pick a plain word such as safety, kindness, honesty, whānau, health, learning, responsibility, rest, repair, or dignity.
  • Name the away move: what would reduce discomfort now but may pull you further from safety, connection, health, repair, or responsibility?
  • Name the toward move: what would move one step toward the value word, even if it feels awkward, slow, or incomplete?
  • Make it tiny: shrink the toward move to less than five minutes. Smaller is usually more usable when you are distressed.
  • Do and review: try the tiny step, then ask: “Did this move me slightly toward or slightly away?” Do not grade yourself harshly.

Example

Moment: “I feel overwhelmed and want to avoid a hard message.” Value word: responsibility. Away move: ignore it all day, then feel worse. Toward move: write a two-sentence draft and leave it unsent for ten minutes. Tiny step: open the message and write only the first sentence.

Common difficulties

  • “I do not know my values.” Use simpler words: safety, kindness, honesty, whānau, health, learning, responsibility, rest, repair, or dignity.
  • “The toward move feels too hard.” Make it smaller. A toward move can be opening the document, putting shoes by the door, drinking water, or texting “Can I talk later?”
  • “I chose the away move.” Notice it without attacking yourself. The next choice point starts now, not tomorrow.
  • “This feels like pressure.” The aim is not to force productivity. Rest, pausing, and support can be toward moves.

Try this now: choose one value word from the table, then draw a fork in the road. Label one side “away” and one side “toward”. Put one tiny safe action on the toward side that takes less than five minutes.

If this does not work: use a stabilising skill first, such as grounding, paced breathing, eating, resting, reducing stimulation, or contacting a safe person. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. If you feel unsafe with yourself, overwhelmed by urges, or unable to stay safe, call or text 1737 or contact local crisis, GP, whānau, peer, counsellor, nurse, or emergency support.

Kete handout · general education · values-based action adapted into plain-language self-help wording.

Anxiety map: body, thoughts, feelings and behaviour

What this is for: understanding one anxious moment so you can choose a useful first response instead of fighting the whole problem at once.

When to use it: when worry, panic sensations, avoidance, checking, reassurance-seeking, irritability, fear of fear, or overthinking keeps repeating.

When not to rely on it alone: do not use mapping as a substitute for medical, crisis, family violence, substance-use, or professional support when symptoms are new, severe, medically concerning, linked with intoxication or withdrawal, or safety feels uncertain.

Why this helps

Anxiety can make the whole situation feel like one urgent threat. A map separates the loop into parts: the situation, body alarm, thoughts or predictions, feelings, actions, and what happened afterwards. Once the loop is clearer, you can start with one part: grounding the body, checking a thought, reducing avoidance, solving a real problem, or contacting support.

Step-by-step

  1. Choose one recent moment. Pick a specific situation rather than “my whole life feels anxious.”
  2. Name the trigger. Where were you, who was there, and what had just happened?
  3. Map the body alarm. Notice breathing, heart rate, stomach, muscles, heat, dizziness, shaking, numbness, or restlessness.
  4. Write the mind’s prediction. For example: “I will faint,” “they are angry with me,” “I cannot cope,” or “something bad will happen.”
  5. Notice the action urge. Avoid, escape, check, ask for reassurance, freeze, argue, drink, scroll, withdraw, or push through?
  6. Choose one first response. Body alarm usually needs grounding or breathing. Avoidance may need a tiny step. A real practical problem may need problem-solving or support.
SituationThoughts / predictionsBody sensationsActions / urgesPossible first response
Where was I? Who was there? What was happening?What did my mind say might happen?Heart, breathing, stomach, muscles, dizziness, heat, shaking, numbness?Avoid, escape, check, ask reassurance, freeze, snap, drink, withdraw?Grounding, small step, problem-solving, support, or urgent help?

Short example

A person notices anxiety before making a phone call. Their body feels hot and shaky. The prediction is, “I will mess it up.” The urge is to avoid the call and keep replaying it. Their first response is to write the first sentence of the call, put their feet on the floor, and make a two-minute call rather than waiting to feel calm.

Common difficulties

  • The map gets too big: use one moment only, or fill in just one column.
  • The body sensations feel frightening: switch to external grounding and seek medical help for new, severe, or unusual symptoms.
  • The thought feels completely true: you do not have to argue with it. First ask, “What action does this thought pull me toward?”
  • You keep avoiding: make the first step much smaller, or ask someone safe to sit nearby while you begin.

If this does not work

Pause the map and use a stabilising skill if anxiety rises too high. If anxiety is linked with unsafe relationships, stalking, threats, intoxication, withdrawal, self-harm urges, command voices, severe paranoia, or not being able to stay safe, use support rather than trying to map it alone.

Safety note: Anxiety symptoms are often uncomfortable alarm responses, but do not ignore new chest pain, fainting, severe breathlessness, neurological symptoms, intoxication, withdrawal, or immediate safety concerns. In New Zealand, call 111 if there is immediate danger. Call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support.

Kete handout · CBT-informed anxiety mapping · general education, not a diagnosis or crisis service.

ABCDE thought check

What this is for: slowing down a strong emotional reaction and testing the thought, story, or belief that is driving it.

When to use it: after a specific event where your reaction feels bigger than the facts you can clearly see, or when you are stuck replaying what something means.

When not to rely on it alone: do not use it as the first step during crisis-level distress, trauma flooding, intoxication, command voices, unsafe relationships, or immediate danger. Stabilise first and use support if safety is uncertain.

Why this helps

Strong emotions often arrive with a fast meaning: “I am rejected,” “I have failed,” “something bad will happen,” or “I have to fix this now.” The ABCDE check does not ask you to pretend everything is fine. It helps separate the event from the meaning your mind added, so the next step can be more grounded.

The five steps

AActivating event: what happened? Describe it like a camera. Keep to facts: who, what, where, when.
BBelief: what did your mind say it meant? Include the exact sentence if you can.
CConsequences: what feelings, body sensations, actions, or urges followed?
DDispute gently: what evidence fits, what does not fit, what else could be true, and what would you say to a friend?
EEffective new belief: what balanced statement helps you take the next useful step?

Step-by-step version

  • Start small: choose one recent moment, not your whole life story.
  • Write the event first: if the sentence includes a judgement, move that judgement to B.
  • Name the feeling and urge: shame, fear, anger, sadness, tight chest, urge to text, avoid, argue, check, drink, or give up.
  • Look for a kinder test: ask, “What do I know for sure? What am I guessing? Is there another possible explanation?”
  • Choose one next action: wait, ask clearly, ground, rest, apologise, set a boundary, solve one practical problem, or contact support.

Short example

A: My friend did not reply today. B: “They are annoyed with me.” C: Shame, tight chest, checking the phone, urge to send repeated messages. D: They may be busy; I do not know yet; they have replied late before. E: “I feel anxious, but I can wait, do one grounding action, and decide later whether to send one calm message.”

Common difficulties

  • “The belief feels completely true.” Start with “this is one possible story,” rather than trying to force yourself to stop believing it.
  • “I keep finding more evidence against myself.” Add the friend test: “What would I say to someone I cared about in the same situation?”
  • “I am too activated to think clearly.” Use grounding, breathing, cold water, movement, or a support contact first. Come back to the thought check later.
  • “The problem is real.” A balanced thought can still include a real problem. The aim is a workable next step, not positive thinking.

Try this now: complete one ABCDE line for a small recent event. Keep it practical, not perfect. One sentence per letter is enough.

If this does not work: use fewer words. Write only A, B, and one safer next step. If the thought is linked with self-harm urges, command voices, escalating paranoia, family violence, or feeling unable to stay safe, use support rather than trying to think your way through it alone. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. For free 24/7 mental health support, call or text 1737.

Kete handout · informed by the uploaded ABC model and anxiety CBT material · general education.

Problem-solving: one real problem, one next action

What this is for: use this when there is a practical problem you can do something about, even if the whole situation cannot be fixed today.

When to use it: when worry has turned into looping, avoidance, repeated reassurance-seeking, or trying to solve everything at once.

When not to rely on this alone: if you are unsafe, overwhelmed, intoxicated, withdrawing, dealing with family violence or sexual harm, or at risk of harming yourself or someone else, use safety steps and support first. Problem-solving works better after the first wave has settled.

Why this helps

Worry and problem-solving can feel similar, but they are different. Worry often loops around “what if” questions. Problem-solving slows things down, defines one current issue, and turns it into one realistic action. The aim is not to fix your whole life in one sitting. The aim is to make the next step clearer.

Step 1: is this a problem, a worry, or a safety issue?

QuestionIf yes, try…
Is this happening now or soon?Problem-solving may help.
Can I influence one small part of it?Choose the part you can act on.
Is this mostly a future “what if”?Use worry postponement, grounding, or defusion first.
Is someone unsafe, threatening, coercive, or out of control?Use safety planning and support before ordinary problem-solving.
Am I too distressed to think clearly?Ground first, solve later.

Step 2: write the problem clearly

The problem I can act on is: ________________________________

Keep it specific. “Everything is falling apart” becomes “I need food for the next two days,” “I need to reply to one message,” or “I need to ask about a bill before Friday.”

Step 3: shrink the problem to one piece

Too bigSmaller and more workable
My money is a mess.I need to check the due date of one bill.
I cannot cope with work.I need to write down the one task due next.
This relationship is impossible.I need to decide whether to send one calm message or wait.
The house is overwhelming.I need to clear one surface or choose one load of washing.

Step 4: list options without judging them yet

  • Option 1: ________________________________
  • Option 2: ________________________________
  • Option 3: ________________________________
  • Someone who could help me think: ________________________________

Step 5: choose the smallest useful step

OptionProsCons / barriersFirst tiny step

Step 6: make a small plan

I will do: ____________________ When: __________ Where/how: __________
If this is too hard, I will make it smaller by: ____________________
If I need help, I can contact: ____________________

Step 7: review without blame

  • What happened?
  • What helped even a little?
  • What got in the way?
  • Do I repeat, adjust, ask for help, or choose another option?

Short example

A person feels stuck because they have several overdue messages and one bill they are avoiding. They choose one problem only: “I need to check whether the power bill is overdue.” Their first tiny step is to open the bill for two minutes and write down the due date. If it is overdue, the next step is to ask for a payment plan or support rather than trying to solve every money worry at once.

Common difficulties

  • “There are too many problems.” Choose the one that affects safety, basic needs, deadlines, or other people first. Put the rest on a later list.
  • “I keep turning it into self-criticism.” Rename the task in neutral language: “make one phone call,” not “fix my failure.”
  • “Every option feels bad.” Look for the least harmful next step, not the perfect step. Ask someone safe to help compare options.
  • “I freeze when I try to start.” Make the first step physical and tiny: open the email, put shoes by the door, write the first sentence, or place the document on the table.
  • “The problem involves another person.” Focus on your next safe action: pause, write down what you need, choose a calm message, ask for support, or wait if contact would make things worse.

Try this now

Write one problem as a clear sentence beginning: “The problem I can act on is…” Then choose a step that takes less than ten minutes. Stop after that step and review.

If this does not work: make the task smaller or switch to grounding first. If the problem is about danger, family violence, coercion, unsafe housing, severe withdrawal, command voices, escalating paranoia, or feeling unable to stay safe, do not try to problem-solve alone. Use professional, whānau, peer, GP, nurse, counsellor, crisis, or emergency support. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. For free 24/7 mental health support, call or text 1737.

Kete handout · informed by Healthify life-skills resources and CCI problem-solving materials · general education · review before formal service use.

Activity menu for low mood

What this is for: choosing one small action when mood, energy, or motivation is low.

When to use it: when you are waiting to feel motivated before acting, or when your day has become smaller, flatter, or more withdrawn.

When not to rely on it alone: if depression is severe, self-care has collapsed, you feel unsafe, or risk is increasing, use this alongside professional, whānau, peer, GP, nurse, counsellor, or crisis support.

Why this helps

Low mood often tells people to wait until they feel better before doing anything. Sometimes the order has to be reversed: a very small action can create a little movement, contact, routine, or evidence that the day is not completely stuck. The aim is not to force happiness. The aim is to make one small opening.

Step 1: choose one category

Pleasure: music, podcast, movie, sunset, coffee, picnic, new food, beach, photos.
Mastery: journal, cook, bake, rearrange a room, garden, puzzle, craft, library, small admin task.
Connection: text a safe person, call a friend, visit someone briefly, write a letter, volunteer, see neighbours, care for a pet.
Movement / outside: walk to the letterbox, stretch, stand in sunlight, bike ride, swim, park, nature trail, gentle sport if realistic.

Step 2: make it smaller than your mood says

  • Pick one action that could take 2–10 minutes.
  • Choose when and where you will do it.
  • Remove one barrier: put shoes by the door, choose the song, open the curtains, place the cup near the kettle, or write the text message first.
  • Rate mood, energy, or stuckness before and after from 0–10.
  • Review without criticism: did it shift anything even slightly?

Step 3: use the “starter version” if the full activity is too much

Activity ideaStarter version
Go for a walkStand outside for one minute or walk to the letterbox.
Clean the roomPut five items away or clear one small surface.
Contact someoneSend: “Rough day. No need to fix it. Can you send a quick hello?”
CookMake toast, heat soup, or put one easy food option where you can see it.

Short example

A person has spent most of the day in bed and feels too flat to “exercise”. They choose the movement category but shrink it to standing outside with a drink for two minutes. Their mood does not change much, but their stuckness drops from 8/10 to 6/10, so they repeat the same small step after dinner.

Common difficulties

  • “It feels pointless.” Treat it as an experiment, not proof that everything is fixed.
  • “I cannot choose.” Pick the easiest starter version, not the best one.
  • “I used to do much more.” Start from today’s capacity, not yesterday’s standards.
  • “I feel guilty resting.” Rest can be part of the plan. Balance one gentle activity with one genuine recovery step.

Try this now: choose one activity from the menu and shrink it until it feels doable today. Write the starter version here: ____________________

If this does not work: the task may still be too big, or the problem may need more support than a self-help activity can provide. Make the action smaller, ask someone to do the first step with you, or contact a trusted person or health professional. If you might hurt yourself, cannot stay safe, or someone else is in immediate danger, call 111 in New Zealand. For free 24/7 mental health support, call or text 1737.

Kete handout · behavioural activation style activity planning · general education · use alongside support when mood is severe, worsening, or unsafe.

Busy places / agoraphobia step ladder

What this is for: planning gradual practice for fear of busy places, leaving home, shops, queues, buses, lifts, cinemas, traffic, or places where escape feels difficult.

When to use it: when avoidance, constant escape planning, needing safety signals, or relying on other people to manage places has started shrinking life.

When not to rely on it alone: if fear is linked with recent trauma, violence, stalking, unsafe housing, medical risk, severe panic, substance withdrawal, psychosis, high self-harm risk, or feeling unable to stay safe, get support and prioritise safety before exposure practice.

Why this helps

Avoidance can give short-term relief, but it can also teach the nervous system that the place is dangerous. A step ladder helps you practise in small, planned doses so your body can learn: “This is uncomfortable, but I can move through it safely enough.”

Step 1: map the fear

  • Place or situation I avoid: for example, supermarket, bus, queue, lift, appointment, traffic, school gate, or busy mall.
  • What I fear might happen: panic, embarrassment, being trapped, fainting, losing control, needing a toilet, being judged, or not being able to get home.
  • Body sensations I fear: racing heart, dizziness, breathlessness, nausea, sweating, shaking, heat, numbness, or unreality.
  • Safety signals I rely on: a person, exit, toilet, car, phone, medication, water bottle, sunglasses, headphones, checking symptoms, or an escape route.

Step 2: build the ladder

StepSituationAnxiety 0–10Support plan
1Very small version
2Slightly harder
3Medium
4Harder

Step 3: choose one practice rule

  • Start lower on the ladder than you think you “should”. Too hard usually teaches escape; manageable practice teaches confidence.
  • Stay long enough to learn something if it is safe to do so. The aim is not zero anxiety; the aim is “I can handle this wave”.
  • Repeat the same step several times before moving up. Repetition matters more than bravery.
  • Reduce only one safety behaviour at a time. For example, keep the support person but stop checking the exit every minute.
  • Plan recovery afterwards: water, food, quiet time, a walk, a text to someone safe, or a simple note about what you learned.

Short example

Example: A person avoids the supermarket because they fear panic in the queue. Step 1 is standing outside the shop for two minutes. Step 2 is walking in and buying one item with a support person nearby. Step 3 is buying one item alone at a quiet time. Step 4 is using a busier checkout while practising slow breathing and staying until the purchase is finished.

Common difficulties

  • “I left early, so I failed.” Not necessarily. Write down what you did manage and repeat a smaller step next time.
  • “My anxiety went up.” That can happen during exposure. The key question is whether the situation was safe enough and planned enough.
  • “I need all my safety behaviours.” Keep what is genuinely protective. Experiment with reducing only the safety behaviours that keep fear stuck.
  • “I feel ashamed that this is hard.” This is nervous-system learning, not a character flaw. Smaller and repeated is better than harsh and unsustainable.

If this does not work

  • Make the step smaller, shorter, closer to home, quieter, or more supported.
  • If panic becomes overwhelming, pause exposure practice and use grounding, breathing, or a support person.
  • If the fear is connected with trauma, family violence, stalking, unsafe housing, medical symptoms, alcohol or drug withdrawal, or not feeling safe, get tailored support before continuing.
  • If there is immediate danger, call 111 in New Zealand. If distress is high or you are unsure what to do next, call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support.

Try this now: write one easier step that is challenging but not overwhelming. Rate it 0–10. If it is above 6, make it smaller.

Safety note: gradual exposure is not about forcing yourself into unsafe situations. It is about testing fear predictions in planned, supported, manageable steps. You can go slowly.

Kete handout · informed by uploaded agoraphobia booklet and anxiety-avoidance material · general education.

Alcohol and mood check

What this is for: noticing whether alcohol is affecting mood, anxiety, sleep, relationships, safety, money, work, medication routines, voices/paranoia, impulsive decisions, or relapse warning signs.

When to use it: if drinking has increased, other people are worried, alcohol is used to cope, anxiety or depression feels worse the next day, sleep is disrupted, or alcohol is making conflict, spending, medication routines, suspiciousness, or safety harder to manage.

When not to rely on it alone: if you may be dependent, have withdrawal symptoms, drink heavily most days, mix alcohol with other drugs or medication, are pregnant, feel unsafe, have self-harm thoughts, or have psychosis/mania warning signs. Get professional support before making major changes.

First check: is this safe to change on my own?

Do not suddenly stop heavy daily drinking without medical advice. Alcohol withdrawal can be dangerous. Seek urgent medical help if withdrawal includes confusion, seizures, hallucinations, severe shaking, chest pain, collapse, severe vomiting, or you cannot stay safe.

For non-urgent alcohol or drug support in New Zealand, the Alcohol Drug Helpline is available 24/7 on 0800 787 797, free text 8681, or webchat.

One-week pattern check

QuestionMy notes
When do I usually drink? Time, place, people, payday, stress, loneliness, pain, boredom, trauma cues, or habit cues.
What feeling am I trying to change? Anxiety, low mood, shame, anger, sleep pressure, social discomfort, voices, memories, or emptiness.
What happens during drinking? Amount, pace, mixing with drugs/medication, spending, arguments, messages, sex, driving, gambling, or risk-taking.
What happens afterwards? Sleep, anxiety rebound, mood, voices, suspiciousness, conflict, money, work, parenting, medication routines, or safety.
What makes it harder to stop or slow down? Withdrawal, cravings, social pressure, shame, secrecy, access, or believing “I have already blown it.”
What would be one lower-risk step this week?

Choose one lower-risk experiment

  • Track without judging: note drinks, mood before, mood after, sleep, anxiety next day, and any safety concerns for one week.
  • Add friction: buy less, avoid drinking alone if that is risky for you, delay the first drink, slow the pace, plan an earlier finish, or keep less alcohol at home.
  • Reduce next-day harm: eat before drinking, alternate with water or non-alcoholic options, plan transport, and avoid driving.
  • Protect relationships and safety: avoid major decisions, conflict conversations, risky messages, spending decisions, sex you are unsure about, or unsafe situations while intoxicated.
  • Plan the high-risk time: choose what you will do during the hour you are most likely to start or keep drinking: shower, food, walk, text someone, meeting, helpline, bed routine, or leaving earlier.
  • Bring in support: talk with a GP, pharmacist, prescriber, alcohol and drug service, or trusted support person if medication, withdrawal, pregnancy, psychosis, mania, trauma, family violence, or self-harm risk is relevant.

Alcohol, drugs, and relapse warning signs

Alcohol and other drugs can lower distress in the short term but may worsen sleep, anxiety rebound, mood swings, judgement, medication routines, conflict, and relapse warning signs. Cannabis and stimulant-type drugs can be especially risky for some people who experience voices, paranoia, or psychosis. If symptoms increase after use, treat that as useful information and get support early.

Short example

Example: A person notices they drink most after late shifts. It helps them feel switched off for two hours, but the next day they feel anxious, suspicious, ashamed, and short-tempered. Their experiment is to eat before leaving work, buy no alcohol on the way home, text a support person, and call the Alcohol Drug Helpline if the urge is still high after 20 minutes.

Common difficulties

  • “I only drink because things are hard.” That may be true. The question is whether alcohol is helping overall or creating extra problems the next day.
  • “I have already ruined the week.” One heavy episode does not mean the week is lost. The next safer step still counts.
  • “I feel ashamed telling anyone.” Shame is common and can keep the pattern hidden. A confidential helpline, GP, or alcohol and drug service can be a smaller first step.
  • “I get shaky or sick when I stop.” Treat this as a medical safety issue. Do not detox alone.
  • “Alcohol is linked with violence or unsafe sex.” Prioritise safety planning and specialist support, not just drinking goals.

If this does not work

If you cannot cut down, keep drinking more than intended, feel withdrawal, hide drinking, mix substances, spend money needed for essentials, or risk is rising, this is not a willpower failure. It is a sign to use extra support. Start with a GP, alcohol and drug service, Alcohol Drug Helpline, mental health team, or trusted support person.

Try this now: write one line: “When I drink, I am usually trying to change _____. The next-day cost is usually _____. One safer step this week is _____.”

Urgent support: if alcohol or drug use is linked with immediate danger, overdose, violence, self-harm, withdrawal, hallucinations, severe confusion, chest pain, seizures, collapse, or inability to stay safe, call 111 in New Zealand or seek urgent medical help.

Kete handout · informed by Healthify NZ alcohol/addiction information, Health NZ alcohol and drug addiction support guidance, and Alcohol Drug Helpline NZ · general education.

Unsafe relationships: getting support safely

What this is for: use this when a partner, ex-partner, family member, flatmate, carer, or another person makes you feel frightened, controlled, threatened, pressured, monitored, sexually unsafe, or unable to get help freely.

When not to rely on this alone: if there is immediate danger, call 111 in New Zealand. If using this handout, searching online, printing it, or calling someone could make things less safe, close it and use a safer phone, device, webchat, trusted person, or local service if possible.

Why this helps

When another person may harm or control you, the safest next step is often not a direct conversation. A short support plan can help you contact the right service, protect privacy, and think through safety before acting.

Signs this may be unsafe

  • You feel afraid of their reaction or feel you have to change your behaviour to stay safe.
  • They threaten, hit, hurt, humiliate, isolate, stalk, monitor, pressure sexually, or control money, transport, medication, phone access, children, pets, housing, or whānau contact.
  • You feel unable to say no, leave, sleep safely, use your phone freely, or get help without consequences.
  • They check your device, accounts, location, messages, bank activity, social media, or who you talk to.

Try this step by step

  • Check immediate safety: if danger is happening now, call 111 or move toward safer people, a public place, neighbour, or safer room if possible.
  • Protect privacy first: use a safer device or phone if you are being monitored. Consider webchat if calling is unsafe. Be careful with printed handouts, browser history, shared accounts, and notifications.
  • Contact specialist support: you can ask for advice even if you are not ready to leave, do not have proof, or are unsure whether it “counts”.
  • Prepare a simple script: “I am worried about my safety and need help making a plan.”
  • Plan one small next step: safe contact, code word, essential items, safer transport, support appointment, or advice about children, pets, money, housing, or documents.
  • Avoid sudden confrontation if risk could rise: plan with specialist support where possible, especially if the person monitors you, has threatened harm, or reacts dangerously to independence.

Useful NZ supports

  • Are You OK: family violence information, safer-site guidance, and help finding support. Phone 0800 456 450.
  • Women’s Refuge: crisis line 0800 REFUGE / 0800 733 843. Call 111 if urgent.
  • Shine: free 24/7 family violence helpline and webchat. Phone 0508 744 633.
  • Safe to Talk — Kōrero mai, ka ora: free 24/7 confidential sexual harm support. Call 0800 044 334, text 4334, email, or webchat.

Try this now: if safe, write or memorise one safer contact method, one safer device/place, and one sentence you could use without explaining everything.

Kete handout · general education · informed by NZ family violence and sexual harm support sources · not a substitute for specialist family violence, sexual harm, legal, medical, or emergency support.

Eating, body distress, and rigid food rules

What this is for: noticing when food, body, exercise, weight, “clean eating”, dieting, or wellness rules are starting to shrink life, increase shame, or affect safety. This can include restriction, bingeing, purging, compulsive exercise, body checking, secrecy, orthorexia-style rigidity, or feeling unable to eat flexibly.

Do not wait until it feels “serious enough”. Eating disorders and disordered eating can affect the heart, brain, mood, hormones, sleep, concentration, relationships, and safety. If eating, purging, restriction, rapid physical change, faintness, chest pain, pregnancy, diabetes, self-harm risk, or a young person is involved, speak with a GP or specialist service early.

When to use this

  • Food rules feel stricter, more frightening, or harder to break.
  • You feel shame, panic, disgust, or guilt around eating or your body.
  • You are checking, comparing, hiding, compensating, or bargaining with yourself.
  • “Healthy” eating, exercise, or wellness content is making life smaller rather than freer.
  • You are worried about someone else and do not know how to start a supportive conversation.

First safety check

NoticeSafer next step
Fainting, chest pain, severe weakness, confusion, vomiting, blood, dehydration, pregnancy concerns, diabetes concerns, or rapid physical changeSeek medical advice urgently through a GP, urgent care, Healthline, or 111 if there may be immediate danger.
Restriction, purging, bingeing, compulsive exercise, laxative/diuretic use, or feeling unable to stopTell a GP, counsellor, eating-disorder service, or trusted support person. Early support matters.
Self-harm urges, suicidal thoughts, unsafe shame, command voices, or feeling unable to stay safeUse urgent support. In New Zealand call 111 for immediate danger or call/text 1737 for mental health support.
A child or young person is affectedSeek help early. A GP can check physical safety and refer to specialist eating-disorder support where appropriate.

Map the loop without numbers or detail

Part of the loopPlain-language exampleOne safer response
TriggerMirror, photo, social media, comment, stress, conflict, eating with othersReduce checking/comparing; move toward a person or place that is less shaming.
Rule or urge“I need to fix this”, “I must be clean”, “I have ruined it”, “I should compensate”Name it as a rule or urge, not an instruction. Delay action and contact support.
BehaviourRestriction, bingeing, purging, over-exercising, hiding, checking, avoiding meals or peopleChoose one harm-reduction step and one support step rather than adding another rule.
CostMore shame, secrecy, fear, tiredness, body distress, conflict, reduced concentrationWrite the cost in kind language: “This is hurting me; I deserve help.”

One non-shaming next step

  1. Pause the rule: “This is a food/body rule showing up. I do not have to obey it immediately.”
  2. Lower exposure: step away from triggering content, mirrors, comparison, diet talk, or body-checking loops.
  3. Choose support over secrecy: message one trusted person or book a GP appointment.
  4. Protect the body: if there are physical symptoms or purging/restriction, seek medical advice rather than trying to manage it alone.
  5. Use neutral language: speak about distress, safety, and support rather than weight, numbers, blame, or willpower.
Example: A person notices “wellness” rules have become rigid. They feel panicky if food is not exact, avoid eating with whānau, and check their body often. Their next step is to pause body checking for one hour, message a trusted friend, and book a GP appointment to talk about eating and anxiety without using numbers.

For whānau, friends, or partners

  • Focus on concern, care, and support rather than appearance, weight, blame, or arguments about food.
  • Say what you have noticed: “You seem distressed around food and I care about you. Can we get support together?”
  • Do not become the food police. Encourage professional help, medical review, and steady support.
  • If safety is uncertain, escalate support early rather than waiting for the person to agree everything is serious.

Common difficulties

  • “But I am not thin / sick / bad enough.” You do not need to meet a stereotype to deserve help.
  • “It is just healthy eating.” If it is driven by fear, shame, rigidity, secrecy, or life getting smaller, it is worth getting support.
  • “I do not want people to control me.” Ask for support that protects choice, dignity, and safety. You can ask what treatment involves before starting.
  • “The internet says this is wellness.” Be cautious with shame-based, extreme, miracle, detox, body-transformation, or purity-based advice.

If this does not work

If food, body, exercise, purging, bingeing, restriction, or shame keeps taking over, involve support. Start with a GP, counsellor, eating-disorder service, school/tertiary health service, or trusted support person. EDANZ can support carers and whānau, and Healthify has NZ eating-disorder information. Use urgent help if physical safety or self-harm risk is present.

My warning sign: _____. One person I can tell: _____. One professional/support step: _____. One thing I will stop checking/comparing for today: _____.

Kete handout · informed by Healthify NZ, EDANZ, KidsHealth NZ, Mental Health Foundation NZ, and Health NZ eating-disorder service information · general education.

Gambling harm: pause, protect, get support

What this is for: use this when gambling is affecting money, mood, sleep, relationships, work, secrecy, shame, safety, or your sense of control.

When not to rely on this alone: use urgent support if gambling is linked with self-harm thoughts, threats, debt pressure, family violence, illegal activity, or feeling unable to stay safe. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111.

Why this helps

Gambling harm often runs on urgency: chase the loss, keep it secret, try one more time, fix it before anyone knows. A pause plan slows the loop, adds friction between the urge and the next bet, and makes it easier to bring in support before the harm gets bigger.

Map the gambling loop

Part of the loopWhat to noticeOne possible friction step
TriggerPayday, boredom, stress, loneliness, alcohol, sport, conflict, shame, or being alone with a phone.Plan the risky time before it arrives.
AccessApp, website, venue, cash, card, account, saved password, advertising, or betting messages.Log out, block access, avoid the venue, leave cards behind if safe, or ask someone trusted to hold money short term.
Thought“I can win it back,” “one more,” “I deserve this,” “I have already blown it,” or “I need to fix this before anyone knows.”Name it as a gambling thought, not an instruction.
After-effectRelief, shame, secrecy, panic, debt pressure, relationship strain, poor sleep, or more urges.Contact support before trying to repair everything alone.

Try this step by step

  • Name the urge: “There is an urge to gamble or chase losses.” This is a signal to pause, not proof you have to act.
  • Delay: set a 20-minute timer before any next bet. During the timer, move your body, change rooms, or step outside if safe.
  • Change location or access: leave the venue, put the phone in another room, log out, block the site or app, avoid carrying extra cash, or move cards away if safe and practical.
  • Tell one person or service: send a short message such as, “I am having a gambling urge and need a pause.” You do not need to explain everything at once.
  • Write the real cost: money, sleep, mood, secrecy, relationships, work, safety, and the next thing that could get worse if the loop keeps going.
  • Choose one recovery action: food, shower, walk, sleep routine, support call, budgeting support, banking limits, blocking tools, venue exclusion, or specialist gambling support.

Example

A person wants to chase losses online after a stressful evening. They say, “This is the chase-loss urge,” set a 20-minute timer, leave their phone charging in another room, text a friend, and call a support line before deciding what to do next. The aim is not to feel calm straight away. The aim is to avoid making the next unsafe step automatic.

Common difficulties

  • “I have to win it back first.” Chasing losses usually keeps the loop alive. Pause first, then bring in support for the money problem.
  • “I am too ashamed to tell anyone.” Shame is common with gambling harm and can make secrecy stronger. Confidential specialist support is a valid first step.
  • “A 20-minute pause will not fix the debt.” The pause is not meant to fix everything. It is meant to stop the next bet and create room for safer help.
  • “I keep finding ways around blocks.” Add more than one barrier: people, money access, app/site blocks, venue changes, banking limits, and specialist support.

If this does not work

If gambling is linked with debt pressure, threats, illegal activity, family violence, relationship danger, unsafe shame, suicidal thoughts, or feeling unable to stop, use specialist and urgent support rather than relying on a worksheet. Gambling Helpline NZ offers free 24/7 support on 0800 654 655 or text 8006. For mental health distress in New Zealand, call or text 1737. If there is immediate danger, call 111.

Try this now: write one trigger, one access point to gambling, one person or service you could contact, and one friction step you can put between you and the next bet.

Kete handout · general education · review alongside specialist gambling, financial, legal, family violence, or crisis support where needed.

Addiction patterns, cravings, and supporting someone

What this is for: use this when alcohol, drugs, gambling, gaming, pornography, shopping, social media, or another pattern is starting to affect mood, money, sleep, relationships, safety, work, study, parenting, culture, or a sense of control.

When not to rely on this alone: get specialist or medical support if alcohol or drug withdrawal may be possible, if there is overdose risk, severe intoxication, unsafe driving, violence, debt threats, illegal activity, psychosis, mania, pregnancy, unsafe caregiving, self-harm thoughts, or feeling unable to stay safe. In New Zealand call 111 for immediate danger.

Why this helps

Addiction and compulsive patterns are not solved by shame. They often run on urgency, secrecy, relief, pain, habit, access, and the hope that one more time will settle things. A useful first step is to slow the loop, reduce easy access, and bring in support before the next high-risk moment.

Map the loop without blame

Part of the loopWhat to noticeOne safer response
TriggerStress, loneliness, boredom, pain, payday, conflict, shame, tiredness, social pressure, or being online late.Name the trigger and delay acting for 10 minutes.
AccessPhone, app, dealer/contact, venue, cash, card, saved password, private browser, alcohol at home, or being alone.Add friction: move location, give the card to a trusted person, block an app, avoid the route, or contact support.
Thought“I need this,” “I have already failed,” “No one can know,” “I will stop tomorrow,” or “This is the only thing that helps.”Treat it as an urge-thought, not an instruction.
After-effectRelief, shame, secrecy, panic, debt, conflict, withdrawal, low mood, poor sleep, or wanting to hide.Write the next repair step instead of replaying the mistake.

Harm-reduction next steps

  • Pause: set a timer for 10 minutes. During the timer, do not bargain with the urge; change one condition around it.
  • Reduce access: move away from the cue, device, cash, alcohol, person, venue, saved account, or private space if you can do this safely.
  • Make it less risky: avoid driving, mixing substances, using alone, borrowing money, sending messages you may regret, or caring for children while impaired.
  • Tell one safe person or service: “I am having a strong urge and need a pause. Can you stay in contact for 20 minutes?”
  • Plan for lapse: a lapse is information, not proof that recovery is impossible. Ask: what was the trigger, what made access easy, and what support is needed before next time?

If you are supporting someone else

  • Use calm, specific concern: “I care about you. I am worried about what this is doing to your sleep, money, safety, and stress.”
  • Support change without becoming the whole safety net. You can offer transport, information, listening, or help contacting a service, while still keeping boundaries.
  • Avoid arguments when someone is intoxicated, highly distressed, or unsafe. Prioritise immediate safety.
  • If there are children, violence, threats, debt pressure, unsafe driving, overdose risk, or self-harm concerns, bring in specialist or urgent support.

Example

A person notices that after conflict they stay up late, drink, scroll, and almost gamble online. Their plan is: eat first, put the bank card in another room, text a friend “I need a 20-minute pause,” use webchat or a helpline if the urge stays high, and review the trigger the next morning without blaming themselves.

Common difficulties

  • “I should be able to stop by myself.” Support is not failure. Addiction patterns are easier to change when access, stress, secrecy, and shame are addressed together.
  • “I do not want to make it worse by telling someone.” Choose one safe person or confidential service. You do not have to tell everyone.
  • “They will not accept help.” Supporters can still get help for themselves, set boundaries, and plan for safety.
  • “I lapsed, so there is no point.” A lapse can show where the plan needs more support, more friction, or earlier action.

If this does not work

If risk is rising, do not rely on a worksheet. Contact a GP, addiction service, counsellor, peer support, Alcohol Drug Helpline, Gambling Helpline, or another specialist service. If alcohol or drug withdrawal may be involved, seek medical advice before cutting down quickly. If there is immediate danger, call 111. For mental health distress in New Zealand, call or text 1737.

Try this now: write one trigger, one access point, one support person or service, and one barrier you can place between you and the next high-risk moment.

Kete handout · general education · use alongside medical, addiction, whānau, peer, legal, financial, family violence, or crisis support where needed.

Medication questions: prepare for a safer conversation

What this is for: preparing for a conversation with a GP, psychiatrist, prescriber, pharmacist, or mental health team about mental health medication, side effects, missed doses, stopping worries, medicine changes, or possible mood and behaviour changes.

When not to rely on this alone: get urgent help for overdose, severe allergic reaction, breathing difficulty, chest pain, severe confusion, severe agitation, fainting, possible lithium toxicity, sudden severe mood or behaviour change, or immediate risk to yourself or someone else. Do not stop, restart, double, reduce, or change prescribed medication without professional advice.

Why this helps

Medication conversations can be hard to organise when you are distressed, embarrassed, tired, or unsure what matters. A short written list helps you give clear information and ask safer questions. It also helps your clinician or pharmacist understand timing, benefits, side effects, missed doses, interactions, and what else has changed.

Before the appointment

  • Current medicines: list the name, dose if known, when you take it, and what you understand it is for. Include over-the-counter medicines, supplements, herbal products, alcohol, nicotine, caffeine, or other substances if relevant.
  • What has helped: mood, sleep, anxiety, voices, paranoia, energy, irritability, concentration, pain, side effects, or daily functioning.
  • What worries you: side effects, weight, appetite, movement symptoms, sexual problems, sedation, emotional flattening, sleep, restlessness, driving, missed doses, cost, stigma, pregnancy, breastfeeding, or interactions.
  • Timeline: write down when the medicine started, stopped, or changed; when the problem began; missed doses; and what else changed around the same time.
  • Pattern notes: note whether the issue started after a new medicine, dose change, stopping a medicine, missed doses, smoking/vaping change, alcohol or drug change, illness, stress, or sleep loss.

Four safety checks to include

CheckWhy it mattersQuestion to ask
Stopping or missing dosesSome medicines can cause withdrawal effects, return of symptoms, or relapse if stopped suddenly.“What should I do if I miss a dose, and how should this medicine be reduced if we ever decide to stop?”
Pregnancy or breastfeedingBenefits and risks can change. Untreated mental distress can also carry risk, so decisions need individual advice.“What is safest for me and my pēpi if I am pregnant, planning pregnancy, or breastfeeding?”
Smoking, vaping, caffeine, alcohol, or drugsChanges in these can affect sleep, anxiety, side effects, relapse risk, and sometimes medicine levels.“Could smoking/vaping changes, caffeine, alcohol, or other drugs affect this medicine or how I feel?”
Mood, behaviour, sleep, or thinking changesSome medicines, including non-mental-health medicines, can affect mood, behaviour, sleep, agitation, or confusion.“Could this new symptom be a medicine effect, interaction, or something else that needs review?”

Questions you can ask

  • What benefits should I expect, and how long might they take?
  • Which side effects are common, which should be reviewed, and which need urgent help?
  • What should I do if I miss a dose? Should I ever take an extra dose?
