  • If I want to stop or change this medicine, what is the safest plan and who should I contact?
  • What are my options if this medicine is not helping or side effects are hard to manage?
  • Could this medicine, dose change, missed dose, or stopping affect mood, sleep, agitation, confusion, behaviour, or thoughts?
  • Are there driving, alcohol, pregnancy, breastfeeding, smoking/vaping, caffeine, herbal-product, supplement, or interaction issues I should know about?
  • Where can I find reliable NZ medicine information that is readable for consumers?
  • Should this side effect be reported to Medsafe/CARM, and can you help me decide how?

Simple appointment note

Write this downYour notes
The main thing I need advice about
When it started or changed
Medicines, doses, missed doses, alcohol/drug/caffeine changes, or other changes around that time
What I am worried might happen if nothing changes
What I want to understand before I decide anything

Short example

“Since the dose changed two weeks ago, I have been sleeping longer, finding mornings harder, and feeling restless at night. I have missed two doses because I felt unsure. I want to know whether this could be a side effect, what is safe to do next, and what to do if I miss another dose.”

Common difficulties

  • “I feel embarrassed.” Side effects, sexual problems, missed doses, alcohol or drug use, and cost worries are common things to discuss. You can write them down and hand the note over if saying it feels hard.
  • “I am worried I will be judged.” Try starting with: “I need practical advice, not a telling-off.” A support person or advocate can help you stay with the question.
  • “I want to stop today.” Treat that as an important safety signal to seek prompt advice. Ask what can be changed safely rather than making a sudden change alone.
  • “I cannot remember the names.” Bring the packets, a pharmacy printout, a phone photo, or a list from My Health Record if you have access.

If something feels wrong

Do not wait until the next routine appointment if symptoms feel severe, sudden, frightening, or unsafe. Contact a pharmacist, GP, prescriber, mental health team, Healthline, after-hours service, or emergency service depending on urgency. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111.

Do not change the dose alone: if a medicine feels unhelpful, embarrassing, expensive, sedating, or hard to take, use that as a reason to seek advice rather than stopping suddenly. This is especially important with antipsychotics, antidepressants, benzodiazepines, mood stabilisers, sleeping tablets, and medicines used during pregnancy or breastfeeding.

If the conversation feels difficult

You can bring a support person, whānau member, advocate, written notes, or a medicines list. You do not have to remember everything in the appointment. Ask for the plan to be written down, including who to contact if side effects worsen, a dose is missed, or you feel unsafe.

Try this now: write one sentence: “The main thing I need advice about is…” Then write when it started, what changed around that time, and the safest person or service to contact if it gets worse before the appointment.

NZ information: Healthify has plain-English information about medicines, side effects, missed-dose questions, and medicines that can affect mood. Medsafe provides Consumer Medicine Information and data sheets; data sheets can be technical and are often best discussed with a clinician or pharmacist. Use these sources for preparation, not as a substitute for individual advice.

Kete handout · informed by Healthify and Medsafe NZ medicine information · general education · not prescribing advice.

Therapy approaches, medication questions, and natural therapies

What this is for: understanding common therapy approaches in plain language, preparing questions before starting therapy, and making safer choices about medication, side effects, supplements, herbs, rongoā, and wellness products.

When to use it: use this when you are choosing a counsellor, therapist, psychologist, GP, psychiatrist, peer worker, or support service, or when someone has suggested a type of therapy and you want to know what it might involve.

When not to rely on this alone: use urgent or professional support if there is immediate danger, self-harm risk, harm-to-others risk, command voices, escalating paranoia, mania or very little sleep with high energy, severe eating-disorder risk, withdrawal risk, family violence, sexual harm, or inability to stay safe.

Fit matters

Therapy is not one-size-fits-all. A good fit includes feeling respected, having clear goals, being able to ask questions, and having enough safety and pacing. It is okay to ask what approach a therapist uses, what training they have, how they handle risk, and what happens if the approach does not feel helpful.

Common therapy approaches

ApproachPlain-language ideaWhat a session might involveExtra care
Counselling / supportive therapyTalking through what is happening with a trained person who listens, reflects, supports, and helps you make sense of next steps.Conversation, emotional support, problem exploration, coping ideas, and support planning.Ask about crisis plans, confidentiality, cultural safety, and how progress will be reviewed.
CBTLooks at links between thoughts, feelings, body sensations, and actions.Mapping patterns, testing thoughts, small behaviour experiments, and practice between sessions.Needs gentle pacing if thoughts are linked with trauma, shame, psychosis, or unsafe relationships.
ACTHelps people unhook from difficult thoughts and move toward values, even when feelings are present.Values work, defusion, acceptance skills, mindfulness, and small committed actions.Values work should not be used to push through danger, exhaustion, coercion, or trauma flooding.
DBT skillsPractical skills for intense emotions, relationship stress, distress tolerance, and staying safe.Skills practice, diary cards or tracking, crisis planning, and coaching-style review.Often most useful when risk, self-harm urges, or emotional intensity need clear structure.
ERPExposure and response prevention helps people face feared thoughts or situations while reducing compulsive responses.Carefully planned exposure steps, resisting rituals or reassurance, and review of learning.Should be guided by someone trained in OCD/ERP. Do not use sudden or forced exposure for trauma or unsafe situations.
Schema therapyExplores long-standing patterns, unmet needs, protective parts, and relationship templates.Pattern mapping, imagery or chair work, emotion work, and practising new responses.Needs careful pacing when trauma, dissociation, or unsafe relationships are involved.
Emotionally focused therapyOften used with couples/families to understand emotional patterns and attachment needs.Slowing conflict cycles, naming feelings/needs, and practising safer connection.Not a substitute for family violence or coercive-control support. Safety comes before relationship repair.
Family therapyLooks at how a family or whānau system affects stress, communication, roles, and support.Sessions with more than one person, communication work, boundary work, and shared planning.Use extra care if anyone is being threatened, controlled, silenced, or unsafe.
Solution-focused therapyFocuses on strengths, exceptions, preferred futures, and small next steps.Goal questions, scaling questions, noticing what already helps, and planning one step.Should not minimise trauma, poverty, racism, grief, illness, or safety issues.
Mentalisation-based therapyHelps people pause and wonder about their own mind and other people’s minds, especially when relationships feel intense.Exploring misunderstandings, emotions, assumptions, and relationship moments slowly.Needs skilled support if risk, trauma, dissociation, or self-harm urges are present.

Questions to ask before starting

  • What approach do you use, and what might sessions look like?
  • What problems do you usually help with?
  • How do you adapt therapy for trauma, neurodivergence, culture, disability, faith, sexuality, gender, or whānau needs?
  • What happens if I become unsafe, overwhelmed, or need more support between sessions?
  • How will we know whether this is helping?
  • What are the fees, cancellation rules, wait times, confidentiality limits, and complaint routes?

Cultural safety and fit

It is reasonable to ask whether a therapist, counsellor, prescriber, or service can work in a way that respects your culture, whānau, language, faith, disability, sexuality, gender, migration story, or lived experience. Cultural safety is not only about being polite; it is about whether the support feels respectful, understandable, and useful to you.

  • Ask whether whānau, family, an interpreter, cultural support person, peer worker, or advocate can be involved.
  • Ask how the service works with kaupapa Māori, Pacific, rainbow, migrant/refugee, disability, neurodivergent, or faith-related needs if that matters to you.
  • If the fit feels wrong, you can ask to review the plan, change pace, request a different worker, or seek another service.

Medication, side effects, and prescriber conversations

Kete cannot tell anyone what medication to take, start, stop, increase, reduce, or change. If medication or side effects are part of the picture, talk with a GP, psychiatrist, pharmacist, nurse practitioner, prescriber, or mental health team. Bring a current medicines list, including prescribed medicines, over-the-counter medicines, supplements, herbal products, alcohol/drug use, and any recent changes.

Medication safety: do not stop, restart, double, reduce, or change prescribed medication suddenly without medical advice, unless a clinician has already given you a clear plan. Seek urgent medical help for severe allergic reaction, overdose, severe confusion, chest pain, breathing difficulty, seizure, severe agitation, or immediate danger.

Natural therapies, supplements, herbs, and wellness products

  • Natural does not always mean safe. Supplements, herbs, vitamins, rongoā, and “wellness” products can have side effects or interact with medicines.
  • Check interactions first. Ask a pharmacist, GP, prescriber, or qualified practitioner before combining products, especially during pregnancy, with long-term conditions, before surgery, or when taking psychiatric, heart, blood-thinning, seizure, hormone, or immune medicines.
  • Watch miracle-cure claims. Be careful with products that promise a cure, shame medication, pressure urgent buying, discourage professional care, or rely mainly on testimonials.
  • Respect culture and safety together. Cultural, spiritual, rongoā, and natural supports can matter; it is still wise to discuss safety and interactions with someone qualified and respectful.

Short example

Situation: “My GP suggested CBT, my friend says ACT helped them, and I am also thinking about a herbal supplement.” Next step: write three therapy questions, book a GP/pharmacist conversation about interactions, and choose one approach to try for a set review period.

If this does not work

If therapy does not feel helpful, you can ask to review goals, change pace, ask for a different approach, bring in cultural/whānau support, seek a second opinion, or change therapist. If you feel dismissed, unsafe, pressured, blamed, or worse after sessions, discuss this if safe, or seek support from another trusted person or service.

Try this now: choose one approach from the table and write one question you would ask a therapist before starting.

NZ support reminder: if there is immediate danger, call 111. For free 24/7 mental health support in Aotearoa New Zealand, call or text 1737.

Kete handout · general education · therapy and treatment choices · review before formal clinical use.

Burnout, compassion fatigue, and overload

What this is for: when work, caregiving, whānau responsibility, study, service roles, or repeated emotional demand have been building for too long and you feel exhausted, flat, numb, irritable, cynical, guilty, or unable to keep giving.

When to use it: use this when you need to sort what can be reduced, what needs support, and what recovery could realistically look like this week.

When not to rely on this alone: get extra support if you feel unsafe, are thinking about self-harm, cannot sleep or function, are using alcohol or substances to get through, feel trapped in unsafe work or family violence, or are caring for someone when you no longer feel able to keep yourself or them safe. In New Zealand, call 111 for immediate danger or call/text 1737 for 24/7 mental health support.

Why this helps

Burnout is not laziness or weakness. It often means the demand has stayed higher than the available recovery, control, support, fairness, meaning, or safety. Helpful change usually needs both personal recovery steps and load changes around the person.

Sort the load first

What might be happening?What may help first?
Work burnout: pressure, overload, conflict, low control, unsafe workload, or blurred boundariesChoose one boundary or workload conversation. Record what is unsafe or unsustainable. Use workplace support, union, EAP, manager, HR, GP, or WorkSafe information if needed.
Caregiver burnout: caring without enough breaks, sleep, respite, money, or backupAsk for one practical support step. Check carer supports, respite options, family/whānau sharing, and whether a GP or service coordinator can help.
Compassion fatigue: less empathy, numbness, irritability, dread, or feeling hardened by repeated distressReduce emotional load where possible. Add decompression after demanding contact. Talk with supervision, peer support, or a trusted colleague.
Moral distress: being unable to provide the care, safety, time, or fairness you believe is neededName the mismatch. Seek support, supervision, advocacy, documentation, or role/workload review rather than carrying it as personal failure.
Shutdown or numbness: blankness, disconnection, avoidance, or “I do not care anymore” feelingsStart with body basics and low-demand contact. Use grounding, sleep protection, food, water, and one safe person before major decisions.

Try this step by step

  • Name the pattern: “This looks like overload, not a personal defect.”
  • Pick one load to reduce: task, expectation, shift, message channel, extra role, unpaid labour, emotional conversation, or unnecessary standard.
  • Pick one recovery block: sleep opportunity, quiet time, food, movement, sunlight, decompression after work, or time without being needed.
  • Ask for one concrete support: “Can you take dinner on Tuesday?” “Can we review my caseload?” “Can someone cover school pickup?”
  • Separate urgent from important: write three columns: must happen today, can wait, can be shared or dropped.
  • Plan one boundary sentence: “I cannot take that on this week,” or “I can do X, but not Y.”
  • Review after a few days: notice sleep, irritability, numbness, dread, concentration, and whether the load actually changed.

Short example

A caregiver notices they are snapping at everyone and feeling numb. They write down the pressure points: broken sleep, no respite, constant appointments, and no time alone. They ask one sibling to take a weekly appointment, call a carer support service, and block 20 minutes after dinner with no phone or requests.

Common difficulties

  • “Other people have it worse.” Comparing pain does not create recovery. Your load still needs attention.
  • “I cannot reduce anything.” Start by documenting what is unsustainable. Some changes require support, advocacy, workplace review, or service help.
  • “Rest makes me feel guilty.” Recovery is part of staying able to care, work, parent, and make decisions. It is not a reward for reaching the end of the list.
  • “I feel numb and detached.” Numbness can be a threat-system response. Use low-demand grounding and talk with someone rather than forcing yourself to feel more.

If this does not work

If the load cannot be reduced, escalate support. Talk with a GP, counsellor, supervisor, manager, union, trusted colleague, whānau member, carer support service, or workplace wellbeing/EAP service. If burnout overlaps with depression, trauma, anxiety, unsafe work, family violence, or substance use, treat that as a support need rather than a personal failure.

Seek urgent help now: if you may harm yourself or someone else, if you cannot keep a dependent person safe, if exhaustion is causing dangerous mistakes, if you feel trapped and unsafe, or if you are thinking you cannot go on. Call 111 for immediate danger or call/text 1737 for urgent mental health support.

Try this now: write one sentence: “The load I most need help reducing is ______.” Then choose one person or service to tell.

Kete handout · informed by Healthify NZ, WorkSafe NZ, Carers NZ, and Healthify carer wellbeing resources · general education.

Pregnancy, birth, new baby, and matrescence

What this is for: the emotional and identity shift of pregnancy, birth, becoming a parent, caring for a baby, or supporting someone in the perinatal period. This can include matrescence, antenatal anxiety or low mood, postnatal anxiety or depression, birth trauma, intrusive thoughts, sleep deprivation, overwhelm, relationship strain, and loss of confidence.

When to use it: use this when the changes around pregnancy, birth, or early parenting feel bigger than expected and you need one safe next step rather than a perfect plan.

When not to rely on this alone: get urgent help if you might harm yourself, your baby, or someone else; if you feel unable to keep yourself or baby safe; if you are not sleeping for long periods and feel unusually energised, agitated, confused, or out of control; if you are hearing or seeing things others do not; or if intrusive thoughts feel like instructions. In New Zealand, call 111 if there is immediate danger, or call/text 1737 for 24/7 mental health support.

Why this helps

Matrescence is the transition into parenthood. It can affect identity, body, sleep, relationships, work, culture, sexuality, confidence, and the sense of who you are. Distress in this period is not a character flaw. Naming the load can reduce shame and make it easier to ask for the right kind of support.

First sort the load

What is happening?One useful next step
Sleep deprivation, feeding stress, crying, or practical overloadAsk one person for one concrete task: a meal, washing, a lift, a short rest block, or help with an appointment.
Anxiety, checking, panic, or feeling unable to switch offUse grounding first, then tell a GP, midwife, Plunket nurse, counsellor, or trusted support person what is happening.
Low mood, numbness, guilt, shame, or feeling disconnectedReduce isolation. Book a support conversation and use one tiny daily anchor: shower, food, daylight, or a short walk if safe.
Birth trauma, frightening memories, or body alarmUse eyes-open grounding. Avoid forcing detailed retelling before you feel supported. Ask for trauma-informed help.
Intrusive thoughts that feel unwanted and frighteningDo not manage this alone. Tell a safe professional or support person. If the thoughts feel like commands or safety feels uncertain, seek urgent help.

Try this step by step

  • Name the season: “This is a major transition. I do not have to be coping perfectly.”
  • Check immediate safety: ask, “Can I keep myself and baby safe for the next hour?” If not, contact urgent help now.
  • Choose one support person: partner, whānau, friend, GP, midwife/LMC, Plunket nurse, counsellor, peer support, or crisis support.
  • Ask for one specific thing: “Can you hold the baby while I sleep for 30 minutes?” or “Can you come to the GP appointment with me?”
  • Use a tiny stabiliser: food, water, medication as prescribed, daylight, shower, grounding object, or sitting near another adult.
  • Write the pattern down: sleep, mood, intrusive thoughts, panic, feeding stress, pain, medication concerns, and what helps even slightly.
  • Book support early: do not wait until things are unbearable. Perinatal anxiety and depression are common and treatable with the right support.

Short example

A new parent feels ashamed because they are crying most evenings and having unwanted scary thoughts. They tell their partner, book a GP appointment, call PlunketLine about baby sleep and feeding worries, and ask a friend to bring dinner twice this week. They use grounding when thoughts spike and agree to call urgent support if safety feels uncertain.

For partners, whānau, and friends

  • Listen without arguing, minimising, or saying “you should be grateful.”
  • Offer concrete help rather than vague help: food, dishes, transport, older-child care, appointment support, or a rest block.
  • Watch for worsening mood, panic, isolation, frightening thoughts, confusion, not sleeping, or comments about not being safe.
  • If risk feels high, stay nearby and involve professional or urgent support. Do not leave someone alone with severe risk.

Common difficulties

  • “I should be coping.” Parenting distress is not failure. It is information that more support is needed.
  • “I am scared they will judge me.” You can start with plain facts: sleep, mood, thoughts, feeding, pain, panic, and what feels unsafe.
  • “The thoughts are horrifying.” Unwanted intrusive thoughts can happen in the perinatal period, but they deserve support, especially if they feel compelling, command-like, or linked with safety concerns.
  • “Everyone keeps focusing on the baby.” The parent’s wellbeing matters too. Ask directly for help for you, not only advice about the baby.

If this does not work

Escalate support rather than blaming yourself. Contact a GP, midwife/LMC, Plunket nurse, Well Child/Tamariki Ora provider, counsellor, local maternal mental health service, or 1737. If there is immediate danger, call 111.

Seek urgent support now: if you may harm yourself, your baby, or someone else; if you feel unable to stay safe; if you are extremely sleep deprived and feel unusually energised, confused, paranoid, or out of control; if voices or thoughts are telling you to act; or if family violence or coercion is present.

Try this now: write one sentence to send: “I am not coping as well as I look. I need help with ______ today, and I need support to book or attend an appointment.”

Kete handout · informed by Healthify NZ, PADA, Mental Health Foundation NZ, PlunketLine, and Mothers Helpers resources · general education.

Sudden loss, serious illness, and supporting whānau

What this is for: the first days or weeks after a death, traumatic loss, serious diagnosis, prognosis change, or when someone close to you is very unwell.

When not to rely on this alone: use urgent support if you might harm yourself or someone else, children or dependents are unsafe, grief is mixed with violence or coercion, or shock is so strong that you cannot stay oriented or make basic safety decisions.

Why this helps

Grief and serious illness can make ordinary tasks feel impossible. A small written plan can reduce decision-load, protect basic needs, and make it easier for other people to help without asking you to explain everything repeatedly.

Start with the next hour, not the whole future

NeedOne small next stepWho could help?
BodyDrink water, eat something plain, take regular medication as prescribed, or sit somewhere safer.
InformationWrite down the next appointment, phone call, document, or person to contact.
Whānau / familyChoose one person to update others so you do not have to repeat the story.
Children / rangatahiUse simple honest language, keep routines where possible, and involve a trusted adult.
RestLower non-urgent expectations for today. Choose one task that can wait.

If the loss was sudden or traumatic

  • Shock, numbness, anger, guilt, images, repeated checking, and feeling unreal can happen after traumatic news.
  • Do not force yourself to talk through details before you are ready. Stabilise first: water, warmth, one safe person, and one clear next step.
  • If media, social media, or repeated messages are overwhelming, ask someone trusted to help manage updates, phones, or visitors for a while.

If someone has a serious prognosis or is very unwell

  • Ask the health team: “What do we need to know for today?” and “Who do we call if things change?”
  • Write questions down before appointments. It is normal to forget things under stress.
  • Share the caring load where possible: transport, meals, childcare, cleaning, paperwork, visits, and quiet company all count.

Short example

Example: After sudden bad news, a person feels numb and keeps rereading messages. They ask one sibling to update whānau, drink water, write the next appointment time on paper, put their phone on silent for 30 minutes, and text one trusted friend: “I do not need advice. Can you sit with me or check in tonight?”

Common difficulties

  • “I should be coping better.” Grief is not a performance. Start with basic safety and practical support.
  • “Everyone needs something from me.” Use one spokesperson or one group message where possible.
  • “I feel guilty resting.” Rest is part of staying able to keep going.
  • “I do not know what to say to children.” Use simple, truthful words and repeat them gently. Children often need small pieces of information more than once.

If this does not work

If grief or shock is becoming unsafe, if you cannot care for dependents, if alcohol or drugs are increasing risk, or if you are having thoughts of suicide or harming someone, use urgent support. In New Zealand call 111 for immediate danger. Call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support. For traumatic events or sudden death, Victim Support can provide practical and emotional support. For serious illness or end-of-life care, ask the health team, GP, hospice service, social worker, or counsellor what support is available for the person and whānau.

NZ support starting points: 1737, Healthify grief/loss information, Manaaki Tāngata Victim Support, local hospice bereavement support, GP/health team, school support staff for children, and trusted whānau/community supports.

Trauma, triggers, and post-traumatic stress

Use this when reminders, flashbacks, nightmares, body alarm, shutdown, numbness, avoidance, or feeling unsafe in the present are showing up after frightening, overwhelming, or harmful experiences.

Start with safety and stabilisation. You do not need to tell the story, analyse the trauma, forgive anyone, or push through details before you are ready. If there is immediate danger, call 111 in New Zealand. If you are unsafe with yourself or someone else, use urgent support now.

What this is for

Trauma responses are the mind and body trying to protect you after something has been too much, too fast, too unsafe, or too alone. Triggers can make the nervous system react as if the danger is happening now, even when part of you knows it is not.

What this might feel like

ResponseWhat it can look likeOne safer first step
Body alarmRacing heart, tight chest, jumpiness, shaking, nausea, startle response.Name the room, press feet into the floor, lengthen the out-breath.
Flashback or memory rushImages, sounds, smells, body sensations, or emotions that feel like the past is present.Say: “This is a memory. I am in [place]. It is [year].” Look for three signs of now.
Shutdown or numbnessBlankness, fog, heaviness, unable to speak, feeling far away.Use low-pressure grounding: warmth, texture, light, a drink of water, or naming objects.
AvoidanceStaying away from places, people, reminders, messages, appointments, or feelings.Do not force exposure. Choose one tiny stabilising step and support.
HypervigilanceScanning, checking, feeling watched, difficulty relaxing, always preparing.Check for real current danger once, then shift to a safe action rather than repeated checking.

Stabilise first

  1. Check current safety. Ask: “Am I in danger right now, or is my body reacting to a reminder?” If there is danger, prioritise leaving, support, or emergency help.
  2. Orient to now. Name where you are, the date or year, your age now, and one thing that is different from the traumatic time.
  3. Use the senses. Look for colours, feel your feet, hold a textured object, notice temperature, or listen for ordinary sounds.
  4. Reduce threat cues. Lower noise, move to light, open a door, sit with your back to a wall, change rooms, or contact a safe person if that helps.
  5. Choose the next 10 minutes only. Food, water, shower, blanket, breathing, safe company, or putting off big decisions all count.

Trauma-informed pacing

  • Go smaller than you think you “should”. A tiny safe step is better than flooding yourself.
  • Do not use exposure, memory work, or detailed writing when you are outside your window of tolerance.
  • If grounding into the body feels unsafe, use external grounding: colours, objects, sounds, words on a page, a pet, a wall, or a view outside.
  • If talking about details makes things worse, ask for support with stabilisation first.

Grief and trauma can overlap

After a traumatic loss, accident, violence, sudden death, frightening medical event, or unsafe relationship, grief and trauma may both be present. One part of you may be mourning while another part is still bracing for danger. Work with both gently: safety first, then connection, then meaning when or if it becomes possible.

Post-traumatic growth, carefully

Some people later notice new strength, clearer priorities, deeper relationships, culture, spirituality, advocacy, or meaning after trauma. This is possible, but it is not required. Growth should never be used to minimise harm, rush grief, excuse abuse, or pressure someone to be grateful.

Short example

Example: A person hears a sound that reminds them of a past assault. Their body freezes and they feel 19 again. They say aloud, “This is a memory. I am 40. I am in my lounge in 2026.” They turn on a light, hold a cold drink, text a trusted person, and decide not to read old messages tonight.

Common difficulties

  • “Grounding does not work.” Try a smaller, more external version. Do not force body-based grounding if the body feels unsafe.
  • “I feel ashamed that this still affects me.” Ongoing reactions do not mean weakness. They mean the nervous system learned danger.
  • “People want details.” You can say, “I am not ready to talk about details. I need support with now.”
  • “I keep checking.” One safety check may be useful. Repeated checking can keep the alarm switched on. Add support if checking feels hard to stop.

If this does not work

Use more support if trauma reminders are frequent, getting worse, affecting sleep or work, causing unsafe avoidance, linked with self-harm urges, or making it hard to care for yourself or dependents. Seek specialist support after sexual harm, family violence, childhood abuse, torture, traumatic loss, serious assault, accident, or any experience where you feel unsafe processing it alone.

If there is immediate danger, call 111. If you might harm yourself or someone else, if voices or thoughts feel commanding, if paranoia is escalating, if alcohol or drugs are increasing risk, or if you cannot stay safe, use urgent support. In New Zealand you can call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support. For sexual harm, Safe to Talk can help with confidential support and ACC Sensitive Claims pathways.

NZ support starting points: 1737, GP or mental health team, Healthify PTSD information, Mental Health Foundation PTSD information, ACC Sensitive Claims / Find Support for sexual abuse or assault, Safe to Talk, and family violence support such as Are You OK if trauma is linked with current unsafe relationships.

Generational trauma, family patterns, and breaking cycles

What this is for: inherited family or whānau patterns, shame, silence, fear, anger, addiction patterns, disconnection, colonisation or historical trauma, cultural loss, harsh survival rules, or wanting to parent, relate, recover, or live differently.

When to use it: use this when you notice an old pattern repeating and you want one safer, smaller way to respond rather than blaming yourself or blaming your whole family.

When not to rely on this alone: get specialist support if there is current violence, coercive control, sexual harm, unsafe housing, threats, child-safety concerns, substance withdrawal, self-harm risk, or risk of harming someone else. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. For mental health support, call or text 1737.

Why this helps

Patterns can pass through families and communities when people have had to survive danger, loss, racism, colonisation, poverty, disconnection, violence, addiction, or silence. Naming a pattern can reduce shame. It does not mean blaming ancestors, whānau, culture, or yourself. It means making one more choice available now.

Name the pattern without shaming yourself

Pattern I noticeWhat it may be trying to protectOne cycle-breaking step
We do not talk about feelings.Avoiding shame, conflict, or being overwhelmed.Name one feeling privately, then choose one safe person to tell a little more.
Anger gets loud quickly.Trying to regain control or stop feeling powerless.Pause, leave the room safely, lower stimulation, then repair later if it is safe.
I over-function and never ask for help.Trying to keep everyone safe or prevent disappointment.Ask for one specific, small piece of help.
I disconnect from culture, whenua, language, faith, or community.Trying to avoid pain, judgement, grief, or not feeling “enough.”Choose one gentle reconnection step that feels respectful and safe.
I repeat a pattern I promised I would not repeat.A learned survival response, not a character flaw.Use support, make one repair, and plan one different response for next time.

One small cycle-breaking plan

  1. Stabilise first: if you are flooded, angry, numb, ashamed, or unsafe, use grounding, space, sleep, food, or support before deep reflection.
  2. Choose one pattern only: do not try to solve a whole family history at once.
  3. Separate responsibility from blame: you may not have caused the pattern, but you can choose one safer next step.
  4. Choose a boundary or repair: this might be saying no, asking for help, taking a pause, apologising without taking all the blame, or choosing not to continue a harmful conversation.
  5. Reconnect gently: culture, whānau, whakapapa, whenua, community, faith, peer support, creative practice, or therapy can be part of healing, but only at a pace that feels safe enough.
  6. Bring in support: some patterns need counselling, kaupapa Māori, Pacific-led, culturally grounded, whānau/family, addiction, violence, or trauma-informed support. You can ask whether a service can include whānau, cultural support, an interpreter, or a worker who understands your context.

Example

A person notices that conflict in their whānau usually becomes silence for weeks. They cannot change everyone. Their cycle-breaking step is to pause before reacting, send one clear text — “I need some space tonight. I want to talk when we can both be calmer” — and later speak with a counsellor about boundaries.

Common difficulties

  • “I feel disloyal naming this.” Naming a pattern can be an act of care. It does not require rejecting your whānau or culture.
  • “I do not know where this started.” You do not need a perfect history to choose one safer response today.
  • “Culture feels complicated.” Reconnection can be slow, private, practical, and supported. There is no single correct way to begin.
  • “People may push back.” Boundaries can unsettle old roles. Keep the first boundary small and use support.
  • “I keep slipping.” Repeating a pattern under stress is common. Repair, reduce shame, and practise again.

If this does not work

If the pattern involves violence, coercion, addiction harm, sexual harm, unsafe caregiving, threats, or being monitored, do not rely on self-help alone. Use specialist support and a safety plan. If reflection increases trauma flooding, voices, paranoia, self-harm urges, or thoughts of harming someone, pause and contact support. In New Zealand call 111 for immediate danger or call/text 1737 for mental health support.

Support routes to consider: a GP or trusted clinician, counselling or therapy, kaupapa Māori or culturally grounded services where wanted, Family Services Directory for local whānau/family support, Te Rau Ora information about kaupapa Māori and whānau-centred wellbeing, and 1737 for free mental health support.

Perimenopause, menopause, and mental health

What this is for: mood changes, anxiety, irritability, sleep disruption, brain fog, hot flushes, body changes, relationship strain, work stress, or identity shifts during perimenopause or menopause.

When to use it: use this when symptoms feel confusing, you are wondering whether hormones, sleep, stress, mental health, or life load are overlapping, and you need one calm next step.

When not to rely on this alone: seek medical review for heavy or unusual bleeding, new pain, severe sleep disruption, faintness, chest pain, sudden confusion, medication concerns, severe low mood, suicidal thoughts, or a sudden change in mood, energy, impulsivity, or behaviour. Call 111 in New Zealand if there is immediate danger, or call/text 1737 for 24/7 mental health support.

Why this helps

Perimenopause is the years leading up to menopause when hormone levels can fluctuate. Symptoms are real, but they are often mixed with stress, caregiving, work pressure, trauma history, relationship strain, and sleep loss. A simple pattern check can make the next health conversation clearer.

Sort what is happening

What you noticeOne useful next step
Mood swings, anxiety, tearfulness, irritability, or rage spikesTrack sleep, cycle/bleeding pattern if relevant, hot flushes, stress load, caffeine/alcohol, and what was happening before the shift.
Sleep disruption, night sweats, or waking wiredUse sleep-support basics, reduce late pressure where possible, and discuss persistent sleep disruption with a GP or nurse.
Brain fog, memory slips, or concentration problemsUse written reminders, reduce multitasking, check workload, and seek review if symptoms are new, severe, or affecting safety.
Body changes, sexual discomfort, libido change, or confidence lossUse non-shaming language. Ask a clinician about physical symptoms, options, and whether medication, MHT, contraception, or other treatments need review.
Identity, ageing, relationship, parenting, caring, or work pressureName the life transition as well as the symptoms. Choose one support conversation and one load-reduction step.

Try this step by step

  • Write the pattern, not a verdict: “For two weeks I noticed sleep, mood, hot flushes, brain fog, bleeding changes, stress load, and what helped.”
  • Protect the basics first: food, hydration, movement that fits your body, wind-down time, and one realistic sleep-support step.
  • Reduce one pressure point: one boundary, one delegated task, one appointment, one conversation, or one expectation lowered for this week.
  • Prepare for a health conversation: bring your symptom notes, current medicines/supplements, main worries, and questions about options and side effects.
  • Bring in support: tell one trusted person what you are noticing and what practical help would make this week easier.

Short example

A person notices poor sleep, anxiety spikes, hot flushes, and brain fog before work. They track symptoms for two weeks, reduce one late-night task, ask their partner for morning backup, and book a GP appointment to discuss perimenopause, mood, sleep, and treatment options.

Common difficulties

  • “I thought I was just failing.” Try: “This may be a health and life-transition issue. I can ask for help without proving I am desperate.”
  • “I cannot tell what is hormones and what is stress.” You do not need to know before asking for help. A short pattern note is enough to start.
  • “I am worried about medication, hormones, or supplements.” Ask a GP, pharmacist, prescriber, or menopause-informed clinician about benefits, risks, interactions, and what to watch for. Do not stop prescribed medication suddenly without medical advice.

If this does not work

If symptoms are worsening, daily life is becoming difficult, sleep is severely disrupted, mood feels unsafe, or you feel dismissed, seek another clinical conversation. Ask directly: “Could perimenopause or menopause be part of this, and what options are safe for me?” If you feel at risk of harming yourself or someone else, call 111 for immediate danger or call/text 1737 for mental health support.

NZ support starting points: Healthify menopause and MHT information; Health New Zealand menopause information; Sexual Wellbeing Aotearoa menopause appointments; GP, nurse practitioner, pharmacist, prescriber, or menopause-informed clinician for personalised advice. General education only.

Online harm, sextortion, scams, and digital safety

What this is for: cyberbullying, online harassment, image-based abuse, sextortion, scam pressure, account compromise, financial manipulation, or the shame-and-panic spiral that can happen after something harmful occurs online.

When to use it: use this when your nervous system is saying “fix this now” and you need a safer first plan before replying, paying, deleting everything, confronting someone, or trying to handle it alone.

When not to rely on this alone: get urgent help if there are threats to physical safety, sexual harm, child exploitation, stalking, blackmail, family violence, suicide risk, or pressure to meet someone, send more images, pay money, or keep secrets. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111.

First: slow the panic loop

What may be happeningSafer first response
Someone is threatening, shaming, or pressuring you.Pause before replying. Move near a safe person if possible. Do not negotiate alone.
Private images, messages, or identity details are involved.Preserve evidence. Get support before deleting everything. Report through the safest route.
A scammer is demanding money or secrecy.Do not rush. Contact your bank if money or accounts are involved. Use official reporting routes.
A young person is affected.Stay calm, reduce shame, keep them close to support, and involve trusted adults or services.

Step-by-step

  • Get physically safer first. Sit somewhere private enough to think, but not isolated if you feel unsafe with yourself.
  • Do not act under pressure. If someone is demanding an immediate reply, payment, meeting, image, password, or secrecy, treat that as a danger signal.
  • Preserve evidence safely. Keep usernames, messages, links, screenshots, payment details, dates, and account names where possible. Do not share harmful material further.
  • Protect accounts and money. Change passwords from a safe device, enable two-factor authentication, contact your bank if money is involved, and avoid clicking links from the person or message.
  • Tell one safe person. Use a short script: “Something harmful happened online. I feel ashamed and panicky. I need help not to deal with this alone.”
  • Choose the right reporting route. Netsafe can help with harmful digital communications, harassment, image-based abuse, and online safety advice. NCSC/CERT NZ can help with cyber incidents and scams. Police 105 may be appropriate for non-urgent criminal matters; call 111 if danger is immediate.

Short example

A teenager receives a threat to share private images. Their first urge is to reply and beg. Instead, they put the phone down, tell a trusted adult, keep screenshots, avoid sending anything else, and contact Netsafe for help with the next step.

Common difficulties

  • “I am too ashamed to tell anyone.” Shame is part of how online harm keeps power. Start with one safe person or a confidential support service.
  • “I want to delete it all.” That is understandable. Before deleting, check whether evidence may help with reporting, takedown, bank support, or Police advice.
  • “They said it will get worse if I report.” Threats and urgency are common control tactics. Get support before responding.
  • “My child will get in trouble.” Lead with safety, not punishment. A calm adult response makes it more likely they will accept help.

If this does not work

If panic, shame, or fear is so strong that you might harm yourself, harm someone else, run away, meet the person, pay under threat, or cannot think clearly, involve urgent support now. In New Zealand call 111 for immediate danger, call or text 1737 for mental health support, and use specialist online harm support such as Netsafe for reporting and advice.

NZ support starting points: Netsafe for harmful digital communications, image-based abuse, cyberbullying and online safety advice; NCSC/CERT NZ and Own Your Online for cyber incidents and scam guidance; Police 105 for non-urgent criminal reporting; 111 for immediate danger; Safe to Talk for sexual harm support; 1737 for mental health support after distressing online harm.

Separation, divorce, and family breakdown

What this is for: relationship ending, separation, divorce, co-parenting stress, housing disruption, money pressure, loneliness, grief, anger, or trying to support children through family change.

When to use it: use this when your mind is trying to solve everything at once and you need one calmer next step for today.

When not to rely on this alone: get specialist support if there is family violence, coercive control, stalking, threats, sexual harm, unsafe housing, child-safety concerns, financial control, legal pressure, or fear about what someone may do. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111.

Why this helps

Family breakdown can affect nearly every part of life at once: sleep, money, housing, parenting, whānau relationships, identity, loneliness, anger, and grief. A small written plan can reduce the pressure to fix everything in one conversation.

First stabilising steps

AreaOne safer next stepWho or what may help
EmotionsUse grounding before sending messages, making decisions, or having hard conversations.Trusted person, counsellor, 1737, GP, peer support.
ChildrenKeep adult conflict away from children where possible. Use simple reassurance: “This is adult stuff. You are loved.”Parenting Through Separation, school/kura support, whānau, child-focused counselling.
MoneyList urgent bills, income, debts, shared costs, and what needs advice.MoneyTalks, financial mentor, Work and Income, trusted support person.
HousingName the immediate housing question: tonight, this week, or longer-term.Friend/whānau, tenancy advice, Work and Income, family violence service if unsafe.
Legal/parenting arrangementsWrite down questions before seeking advice. Avoid making rushed agreements while flooded.Ministry of Justice information, Family Dispute Resolution, lawyer, CAB, community law.
SafetyIf fear, monitoring, threats, stalking, or coercion are present, do not rely on ordinary relationship repair.111, Are You OK, Police, Women’s Refuge/Shine or local family violence support.

One-page plan

  1. Right now: what needs attention in the next 24 hours?
  2. Not today: what can wait, even if it feels urgent?
  3. Children/whānau: what do they need to hear, see, or not be exposed to?
  4. Practical support: who can help with money, housing, transport, childcare, food, or paperwork?
  5. Emotional support: who can listen without inflaming conflict?
  6. Safety: is anyone afraid, monitored, threatened, or unsafe?
Example: A person wants to send a long angry message at midnight. They put the phone down, write the message in notes without sending it, sleep, then the next day write three practical questions: childcare this week, school pickup, and bills. They ask a trusted person to read the message before sending.

Common difficulties

  • “I need to fix it tonight.” Most separation issues need staged decisions. Start with safety, children, sleep, and the next practical step.
  • “If I do not reply, things will get worse.” If it is safe, delay replying until grounded. If it is not safe, contact specialist support rather than negotiating alone.
  • “The children need to know everything.” Children usually need calm, truthful, age-appropriate reassurance, not adult detail or pressure to take sides.
  • “I should know the legal answer.” Kete cannot give legal advice. Write questions and seek appropriate advice.

If this does not work

If conflict is escalating, children are being pulled into unsafe adult conflict, money or housing is unstable, or you feel unable to stay safe, bring in support early. Use a GP, counsellor, trusted whānau, legal/financial advice, family violence support, or emergency support as relevant. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. For free mental health support, call or text 1737.

This handout supports planning and coping. It is not legal, financial, parenting, or family violence advice. Use specialist support where safety, legal, or child wellbeing issues are involved.

High-control groups, coercive communities, and rebuilding choice

What this is for: questioning, leaving, or recovering from a high-control group, cult-like group, coercive religious or ideological community, controlling wellness/business group, or another group where belonging depended on obedience, fear, shame, secrecy, isolation, or giving up your own judgement.

When to use it: use this when you feel confused, frightened, guilty, loyal, ashamed, cut off from people outside the group, or unsure what you are “allowed” to think, say, read, believe, or choose.

When not to rely on this alone: get specialist or urgent support if there are threats, stalking, violence, sexual harm, child-safety concerns, forced labour, financial control, immigration or housing pressure, surveillance, suicide risk, or fear that someone will punish you for questioning or leaving. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111.

Why this helps

High-control groups often work by shrinking choices, relationships, information, privacy, and trust in your own judgement. Recovery usually starts with safety, privacy, small choices, and reconnecting with support outside the control system. You do not have to decide today whether a label like “cult” is correct before getting support.

Notice the control pattern

PatternWhat it may feel likeOne safer next step
IsolationOutside people are framed as dangerous, weak, evil, disloyal, or unable to understand.Reconnect with one safer outside person, service, or peer-support space.
Fear and guiltLeaving or questioning feels like betrayal, danger, spiritual failure, or losing everyone.Name the fear as pressure. Write down what you need to decide slowly and safely.
Control of informationYou feel anxious reading, searching, asking questions, or hearing outside views.Use a safer device or private place. Read slowly. Stop if your body feels overwhelmed.
Identity pressureYou are unsure who you are outside the group, role, belief system, or rules.Start with small choices: food, clothes, music, routine, values, support, rest.
Relationship pressureFamily, friends, leaders, or members may monitor, shame, threaten, love-bomb, or pressure you.Plan contact carefully. Do not announce plans if it could increase risk.
Practical dependenceMoney, housing, work, immigration, transport, childcare, or education may be tied to the group.Write one practical need and one service/person that could help with that need.

Step-by-step

  1. Protect immediate safety and privacy. If devices, accounts, location, messages, money, or transport are monitored, use a safer phone, browser, place, or trusted person before making plans.
  2. Do not force a final decision while flooded. Write: “The next safe step is _____.” You can question, pause, gather support, or plan without making everything public.
  3. Name one control pattern. For example: fear, isolation, obedience, shame, secrecy, information control, financial dependence, sexual pressure, or punishment for questions.
  4. Reconnect with one outside anchor. This might be a trusted person, GP, counsellor, peer group, legal/community support, family violence service, sexual harm support, or practical welfare support.
  5. Rebuild small choices. Choose one low-risk personal choice today: rest, food, clothing, music, values, movement, reading, faith/spiritual pause, or who you speak with.
  6. Plan for backlash or pull-back. It is common to miss people, feel guilty, doubt yourself, or be pressured to return. Prepare one support contact for those moments.

Short example

A person begins questioning a controlling group but feels terrified of losing everyone. They do not confront leaders immediately. They write down what feels unsafe, use a private device to read support information, reconnect with one old friend, book a GP appointment for sleep and anxiety, and contact a peer-support group for people leaving high-control groups.

Common difficulties

  • “Maybe I am the problem.” High-control systems often train people to doubt themselves. You can seek support without proving everything first.
  • “I still love people in the group.” Leaving or questioning does not erase love, grief, loyalty, faith, culture, or good memories. Mixed feelings are normal.
  • “I do not know who I am now.” Identity rebuilding is slow. Start with small choices and safe relationships rather than trying to rebuild a whole life at once.
  • “They might find out.” Treat privacy as safety. Use safer devices, passwords, locations, and support people where needed.
  • “Everyone says I should just leave.” Leaving can involve housing, money, family, immigration, work, children, faith, and safety. Careful planning is not weakness.

If this does not work

If fear, pressure, threats, isolation, unsafe relationships, sexual harm, violence, financial control, child-safety worries, or self-harm thoughts are present, bring in specialist support rather than trying to manage it alone. In New Zealand, call 111 for immediate danger and call or text 1737 for mental health support. If sexual harm or family violence is involved, use specialist support and a safer device if needed.

NZ support starting points: 1737, GP or primary care, Decult support directory, Olive Leaf Network New Zealand, Uncult Ōtautahi peer support, Gloriavale Leavers’ Support Trust where relevant, Safe to Talk for sexual harm support, Are You OK for family violence, and local community/legal/welfare support for practical safety needs. Check current details before relying on a specific group or meeting time.

Identity, belonging, discrimination, and minority stress

What this is for: times when rainbow identity, culture, ethnicity, migration, refugee background, language, faith, disability, racism, discrimination, or not feeling safe to be yourself is affecting mood, anxiety, sleep, confidence, relationships, work, study, or help-seeking.

When to use it: use this when the problem is not just “inside you”, but also connected to how people, systems, workplaces, schools, services, or communities respond to who you are.

When not to rely on this alone: get extra support if there are threats, harassment, family violence, sexual harm, forced outing, housing insecurity, workplace exploitation, racism, self-harm thoughts, suicidal thoughts, or risk to someone else. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. For mental health support, call or text 1737.

Why this helps

Minority stress can build up when you have to hide, explain yourself, scan for danger, deal with racism or discrimination, translate between cultures, or choose between belonging and safety. Naming the outside pressure can reduce shame and help you choose support that fits your identity, culture, whānau, and situation.

Name the pressure accurately

What is happening?What it can do to wellbeingOne safer next step
Racism, discrimination, or being treated as “less than”.Anger, shame, exhaustion, mistrust, anxiety, or not wanting to seek help.Name it plainly. Talk with someone who will not minimise it. Consider support or a complaint route if safe.
Rainbow identity feels unsafe, hidden, questioned, or rejected.Isolation, fear, low mood, body tension, or feeling split between parts of life.Choose one affirming person, peer space, helpline, or online support route.
Migrant, refugee, or minority-culture stress.Loneliness, grief for home, language fatigue, service confusion, pressure to cope quietly.Look for culturally safe support, interpreting help, community support, or a GP/social-service starting point.
Belonging pressure from family, faith, community, workplace, or school.Guilt, loyalty conflict, hiding, over-explaining, anger, or shutdown.Set one small boundary, reduce one unsafe exposure, or prepare one support conversation.
A service does not feel culturally or identity safe.Not returning, withholding important information, or feeling worse after asking for help.Ask about options: another worker, interpreter, advocate, cultural support, rainbow-affirming support, or a different service.

Choose one support route

  1. Write one sentence that names the pressure: “I am dealing with _____, and it is affecting _____.”
  2. Choose the safest level of support: private grounding, trusted person, peer support, GP/nurse, counsellor, cultural support, rainbow support, migrant/refugee support, school/work support, or a rights/complaints pathway.
  3. Decide what you need from the person or service: listening, practical help, safety planning, advocacy, translation/interpreting help, information, or clinical support.
  4. Prepare one sentence: “I need support that understands _____ and does not minimise it.”
  5. After contact, check: did I feel safer, more respected, and clearer? If not, the fit may be wrong; it does not mean help is not possible.

Example

A person feels anxious at work after repeated comments about their accent and culture. They write down what happened, talk to a trusted colleague, book a GP appointment for stress and sleep, and look up confidential discrimination advice before deciding whether to make a formal complaint.

Questions for culturally safer support

  • Can I bring whānau, family, a support person, interpreter, advocate, or cultural support person?
  • Can my name, pronouns, language, culture, faith, disability, rainbow identity, whakapapa, or migration/refugee background be respected in the way support is offered?
  • Is there a Māori, Pacific, rainbow-affirming, migrant/refugee-informed, disability-informed, or language-matched option if that would feel safer?
  • How will privacy work if my community is small or I am worried about being recognised?
  • What happens if I feel misunderstood, stereotyped, rushed, or blamed?

Common difficulties

  • “Maybe I am overreacting.” Repeated small experiences can still be harmful. You do not need to prove distress perfectly before seeking support.
  • “I do not want to betray my family or community.” Support can include protecting relationships where possible while still protecting your safety and identity.
  • “The first service did not understand.” Fit matters. It is reasonable to ask for a different worker, cultural support, interpreter, or more identity-affirming service.
  • “Talking about it makes me more upset.” Start with grounding, write notes instead of explaining everything, or bring a trusted person if that helps.

If this does not work

If identity stress, racism, discrimination, harassment, family rejection, migration stress, or service harm is making life feel unsafe, use support early. For immediate danger call 111. For mental health support call or text 1737. If there is discrimination or harassment, consider confidential advice before deciding whether to complain formally. If you are a young person or dependent adult, involve a safe adult or service.

NZ support starting points: 1737, GP or primary care, OutLine Aotearoa for rainbow peer support, Te Kāhui Tika Tangata Human Rights Commission for discrimination information, Le Va for Pasifika wellbeing resources, Healthpoint for local mental health/addiction services, and refugee/migrant support pathways where relevant.

Youth, young adults, study stress, and asking for help

What this is for: use this when school, study, exams, first relationships, friendship conflict, identity, family pressure, online life, gaming, body image, work, money, or future pressure is affecting your mental health.

When not to rely on this alone: get direct help if there are self-harm urges, suicidal thoughts, threats, violence, sexual harm, exploitation, unsafe bullying, intoxication, command voices, escalating paranoia, or you cannot stay safe. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. You can also call or text 1737, contact Youthline, or tell a trusted adult or service.

What this might feel like

AreaWhat it can look likeOne useful next step
School or study pressureExams, assignments, attendance, perfectionism, shame, or feeling behind.Write the next smallest task and ask one person what support is available.
Friendships or relationshipsConflict, break-ups, pressure, jealousy, loneliness, or fear of being left out.Pause before messaging, ground first, then talk to someone safe.
Identity and belongingQuestions about culture, sexuality, gender, faith, body, values, or where you fit.Look for one affirming adult, peer group, counsellor, or youth service.
Online life and gamingLate nights, comparison, bullying, sextortion, doomscrolling, conflict, or escape.Protect sleep and safety first; get help for threats, blackmail, or image-based harm.
Body image and food worriesChecking, shame, rigid rules, dieting content, or feeling unsafe in your body.Talk to a trusted adult, GP, nurse, counsellor, or eating-support service early.

One small help-seeking plan

  1. Name the problem in plain words: “I am not coping with school,” “I feel unsafe online,” “I keep thinking about hurting myself,” or “I need help talking to my family.”
  2. Choose one safer person or service: parent, caregiver, whānau member, teacher, dean, school counsellor, GP, nurse, youth worker, campus support, Youthline, What’s Up, 1737, or another trusted adult.
  3. Use a simple script: “I do not need you to fix it right now, but I need help and I do not want to handle this alone.”
  4. Ask for one practical next step: an appointment, a check-in, help emailing school, a safer transport plan, a break from unsafe contact, or help contacting a service.
  5. If the first person is not helpful: try another adult or service. Not being understood once does not mean you do not deserve help.

Example

A student has stopped attending classes, is gaming late, and feels ashamed. They text Youthline first because talking feels easier by text, then ask a school counsellor for help making a catch-up plan. The first goal is not to fix the whole year; it is to be less alone and make the next week safer.

Common difficulties

  • “I do not want to worry anyone.” People who care about you would usually rather know early than find out after things become unsafe.
  • “My problem is not serious enough.” You do not need to be at breaking point to ask for support.
  • “Adults will overreact.” Choose the safest adult you can. You can ask for calm help and explain what would make things worse.
  • “I already tried and it did not help.” Try a different route: GP, nurse, school/campus support, Youthline, 1737, What’s Up, a trusted whānau member, or a youth worker.
If this does not feel safe enough: if you might hurt yourself or someone else, are being threatened, sexually harmed, blackmailed, exploited, badly bullied, or cannot stay safe, tell a trusted adult or service now. Call 111 for immediate danger. Call or text 1737 for free mental health support. Youthline and What’s Up can be good first contacts for young people in Aotearoa.
This handout is general support information. It does not replace medical, counselling, school, youth, family violence, sexual harm, or emergency support.

Men, dads, shutdown, anger, and asking for help

What this is for: use this when distress is showing up as shutting down, snapping, overworking, drinking more, withdrawing, feeling ashamed, feeling useless, parenting stress, separation stress, anger, or not knowing how to ask for help.

When not to rely on this alone: get direct support if you might hurt yourself or someone else, feel out of control with anger, are frightening people at home, are drinking or using substances unsafely, are driving/working unsafely, cannot sleep, feel trapped after separation, or are thinking about suicide. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. Call or text 1737, contact Lifeline, talk with a GP, counsellor, trusted mate, whānau member, workplace support, or a men’s/dad support service.

What this might look like

SignalHow it can show upOne useful next step
Shutdown or withdrawalGoing quiet, avoiding people, staying at work, doomscrolling, gaming, or saying “I’m fine” when you are not.Send one low-pressure message: “I’m not doing great. Can you check in later?”
Irritability or angerSnapping, driving aggressively, slamming things, frightening people, or feeling close to losing control.Leave the room safely, lower stimulation, do not keep arguing, and use support before repair.
Overworking or alcoholTrying to outrun feelings with work, drinking, risk-taking, or staying busy so you do not have to stop.Choose one harm-reduction step: delay drinking, do not drive, eat, sleep, or contact support.
Dads and parenting pressureFeeling useless, left out, criticised, angry, exhausted, or unsure how to connect with children.Choose one small reliable action: a routine, play, school pickup, bedtime step, or support call.
Separation or identity stressGrief, shame, money pressure, housing stress, co-parenting conflict, or feeling you have failed.Do not handle it alone. Use practical/legal/financial support and one mental health contact.

One safe help-seeking plan

  1. Name one honest signal: “I’m shutting down,” “I’m drinking more,” “I’m scaring myself with anger,” “I’m not safe alone tonight,” or “I keep thinking people would be better off without me.”
  2. Choose the least impossible contact: mate, partner, sibling, whānau member, GP, counsellor, EAP/work support, 1737, Lifeline, Father & Child, Mensline Christchurch, or another local men’s support service.
  3. Use a blunt script: “I do not need a lecture. I need help staying steady and making a plan.”
  4. Reduce immediate risk: step away from conflict, do not drive angry or intoxicated, move away from weapons or tools if unsafe, pause alcohol/substances, and ask someone to stay nearby if needed.
  5. Pick one practical next step: book a GP appointment, text a helpline, sleep somewhere safe, ask for parenting support, or write down what needs sorting tomorrow.

Example

A dad notices he is snapping at the kids, drinking most nights, and staying late at work to avoid going home. He texts a mate, books a GP appointment, and tells his partner he needs to take ten minutes outside before hard conversations. The first goal is not to become a perfect parent; it is to stop the pattern getting more unsafe.

Common difficulties

  • “Talking will make me weak.” Getting support is a way to protect people, not a failure of strength.
  • “I do not know what I feel.” Start with behaviour: sleep, alcohol, anger, withdrawal, risk-taking, work, appetite, or thoughts of death.
  • “I already caused harm.” Accountability matters. Safety comes first, then repair, support, and a plan to change the pattern.
  • “I do not want to be judged.” Ask for a practical style of help: “I need a plan, not a big emotional conversation right now.”
If risk is rising: if you might hurt yourself or someone else, are thinking about suicide, feel out of control with anger, are frightening people at home, are using alcohol or drugs unsafely, or cannot stay safe, use direct support now. Call 111 for immediate danger. Call or text 1737 for free mental health support. Lifeline and local men’s/dad support services can be good first contacts if talking to someone you know feels too hard.
NZ support starting points: 1737, Lifeline Aotearoa, GP or primary care, workplace EAP if available, Father & Child Trust, Mensline Christchurch, Healthpoint, and local counselling or community supports. If there is family violence or fear at home, use family-violence support rather than treating it as ordinary conflict.

Parenting overwhelm beyond the baby stage

What this is for: use this when parenting older children or teenagers feels too much: shouting, guilt, sensory overload, blended-family stress, co-parenting conflict, high-needs children, school pressure, sleep deprivation, or the thought “I love my kids, but I am not coping.”

When not to rely on this alone: get direct support now if you are scared you might hurt a child, partner, yourself, or someone else; if a child is unsafe; if violence, threats, coercive control, sexual harm, neglect, intoxication, or severe exhaustion is present; or if you cannot step away safely. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111. Call or text 1737 for free mental health support. For parenting advice, Parent Help can be a practical first step.

What this might feel like

Pressure pointHow it can show upOne safer first step
OverloadNoise, mess, demands, sibling conflict, screens, routines, or transitions feel impossible.Lower input for five minutes: fewer words, one room, water, headphones if safe, or a brief reset.
Guilt after shoutingReplay, shame, avoidance, or trying to fix everything at once.Repair simply: “I shouted. That was not okay. I am taking a pause and I will come back calmer.”
Co-parenting or blended-family stressDifferent rules, loyalty conflicts, changeovers, money pressure, or feeling undermined.Write the next practical issue only. Save bigger conflict for a calmer, supported conversation.
High-needs child or sensory loadExtra appointments, meltdowns, school calls, disability needs, neurodivergence, or constant vigilance.Choose one support ask: respite, school contact, whānau help, disability support, GP, or parent helpline.

A safer five-minute reset

  1. Make the space safer: put down objects, step back, lower your voice, move to another room if safe, or ask another adult to take over.
  2. Name the state: “I am overloaded. This is a pause, not a punishment.”
  3. Reduce words: use one short instruction or pause the conversation. Long lectures usually make overload worse.
  4. Regulate first: feet on floor, cold water, slower breathing, outside air, or one grounding object.
  5. Choose the next 10 minutes: food, quiet, separation between siblings, screen pause, bedtime simplification, or calling support.
  6. Repair later: when calmer, name what happened, take responsibility for your part, and say what you will try next time.

Short example

Example: A parent has yelled after a long evening of sibling conflict and school emails. They put the kettle on, move to the hallway, text their partner “I need ten minutes before I say something worse,” and later tell the children: “I shouted. I am sorry. The rule still stands, and I am going to use a pause next time.”

If support is involved

  • For parenting advice: call Parent Help or talk with a GP, school pastoral staff, Plunket/Well Child Tamariki Ora provider where age-appropriate, counsellor, social worker, or local family service.
  • For safety: if there is violence, intimidation, fear, threats, or you are worried you might hurt someone, use specialist family violence or crisis support. Safety comes before ordinary parenting advice.
  • For high-needs children: ask for support around the child’s needs and your load. Your stress does not mean you do not love your child.

Common difficulties

  • “I should be able to handle this.” Parenting load can exceed anyone’s capacity. The useful question is: what support or load reduction is needed?
  • “Repair means giving in.” Repair is about safety and trust. You can repair your tone and still hold a boundary.
  • “I only lose it when I am exhausted.” Treat sleep loss, hunger, pain, alcohol, financial pressure, and conflict as risk factors, not moral failings.
  • “I am scared to ask for help.” Start with one practical sentence: “I am overloaded with parenting and I need advice before things get worse.”
If this does not work: if yelling, threats, physical harm, scary driving, unsafe supervision, self-harm thoughts, violence, or substance use are becoming part of the pattern, get direct support. Call 111 for immediate danger. Call or text 1737 for mental health support. Parent Help, a GP, school, local family service, or a trusted whānau/support person can help you make the next step safer.
This handout is general support information. It does not replace emergency, family violence, child-safety, parenting, legal, medical, counselling, or disability support.

Supporting someone else without losing yourself

What this is for: when you are supporting a partner, child, parent, friend, flatmate, colleague, or whānau member who is struggling with mental distress, addiction, psychosis, trauma, suicidality, disability, serious illness, or emotional volatility.

When not to rely on this alone: do not carry risk by yourself if someone may be at immediate risk of suicide, violence, overdose, withdrawal, neglect, abuse, unsafe driving, psychosis escalation, or harm to a child or vulnerable person. Use direct support, crisis services, medical advice, or 111 where needed.

What you might notice

What can happenWhat it may meanOne safer next step
You are checking, rescuing, or monitoring constantly.The situation may need a shared safety plan or more support people.Write down who else can be involved and what is no longer safe to hold alone.
You feel guilty whenever you rest, say no, or step back.Your care role may be crowding out your own wellbeing.Choose one small boundary: time, money, transport, phone contact, or crisis responsibility.
You are frightened by anger, threats, intoxication, voices, paranoia, or self-harm talk.This is a safety issue, not just a communication issue.Move toward direct support. Use 111 for immediate danger or 1737 for urgent mental health support.
You feel numb, resentful, exhausted, or unable to feel compassion.This can be compassion fatigue or carer burnout.Plan one recovery step and one practical support request this week.

A safer support plan

  • Name the role: “I am supporting someone, but I am not their whole support system.”
  • Check immediate safety: is there risk of suicide, violence, overdose, withdrawal, medical danger, neglect, or unsafe supervision? If yes, involve urgent support.
  • Share the load: list two people, services, or professionals who can help. Do not wait until you are completely depleted.
  • Set one boundary: be specific: “I can talk for 20 minutes tonight,” “I cannot lend more money,” “I will call help if safety is at risk.”
  • Keep connection without taking over: use statements like “I care about you, and this is bigger than I can hold alone.”
  • Protect your own basics: sleep, food, movement, medication, appointments, work, parenting, and time away from the crisis are not selfish.

If they are at risk but refuse help

You can still seek advice for yourself. You can contact a GP, crisis team, 1737, a helpline, a family violence service, addiction support, or a trusted professional to ask, “What should I do if I am worried about someone’s safety?” If there is immediate danger, call 111.

Short example

A person is supporting their sibling through alcohol use, depression, and angry late-night calls. They decide they will answer one call before 9pm, not send money while drinking is active, save crisis numbers, tell another whānau member what is happening, and contact Family Drug Support for advice.

Common difficulties

  • “If I step back, something bad will happen.” That fear may mean the situation needs more people involved, not more pressure on one supporter.
  • “They will be angry if I tell someone.” Safety can matter more than secrecy when risk is high.
  • “I should know what to say.” You do not need perfect words. Calm presence, clear limits, and support routes are often more useful.
  • “I feel resentful, so I must be a bad person.” Resentment can be a signal that the load is too high or boundaries are missing.

Support script

I care about you. I can stay with this conversation for ______ minutes. I cannot hold this alone if safety is at risk. The next support step is ______.

If this does not work

If the situation is escalating, unsafe, secretive, violent, or beyond what one person can manage, bring in outside support. This might be a GP, crisis team, addiction service, family violence service, disability support, carer support, counsellor, whānau meeting, or emergency response.

Safety note: call 111 in New Zealand if there is immediate danger, violence, overdose, severe withdrawal, threats, a medical emergency, or someone cannot be kept safe. Call or text 1737 for free 24/7 mental health support. If you are supporting someone affected by alcohol or drugs, Family Drug Support Aotearoa NZ can help family/whānau and friends.

Kete handout · general education · not a replacement for professional, crisis, family violence, addiction, disability, medical, or legal support.

Chronic illness, disability, pain, fatigue, and medical trauma

What this is for: use this when a long-term health condition, disability, chronic pain, fatigue, flare-ups, medical appointments, medication burden, or previous healthcare harm is affecting mood, identity, relationships, work, study, parenting, or hope.

When not to rely on this alone: get direct medical help for sudden or severe symptoms, chest pain, severe breathlessness, fainting, serious infection signs, new weakness, falls, severe dehydration, sudden confusion, unsafe medication effects, withdrawal risk, or symptoms that feel medically urgent. Use 111 for immediate danger.

What you might notice

AreaWhat it can feel likeOne useful next step
Flare-ups and uncertaintyGood days and bad days, cancelled plans, fear of overdoing it, or never knowing what your body will allow.Use pacing: choose the smallest safe version, add rest before you crash, and keep one support person informed.
Body grief and identity lossMissing the person you used to be, shame about needing help, anger at your body, or feeling left behind.Name the loss without arguing with it: “This is grief, not failure.” Choose one value that still matters in a smaller form.
Pain, fatigue, or brain fogLow mood, irritability, sleep disruption, concentration problems, or feeling unreliable.Track one pattern for a week: activity, rest, pain/fatigue level, sleep, medication, and what helped.
Service invalidation or medical traumaDreading appointments, feeling dismissed, freezing when questioned, or avoiding care after a bad experience.Take written notes, bring a support person if possible, and write three questions before the appointment.
Medication and natural therapiesSide effects, many medicines, supplements, herbs, cannabis products, sleep aids, or wellness advice from others.Ask a GP, pharmacist, prescriber, or clinician about interactions before changing or combining treatments.

A pacing and support plan

  • Start with dignity: “My body has limits today. That does not make me lazy, weak, dramatic, or difficult.”
  • Choose the current load: pain, fatigue, appointments, mobility, sensory load, medication, money, work, parenting, transport, or service stress.
