  • Use the 50 percent rule: choose a version smaller than the one you think you “should” manage. Stop before the crash where possible.
  • Build rest into the plan: rest is not the reward after everything is done; it is part of the treatment environment.
  • Prepare appointments: write symptoms, examples, questions, medication/supplement list, and what you need help deciding.
  • Bring support if you can: a trusted person can help remember information, ask questions, and notice when you are overwhelmed.

If you feel dismissed or harmed by services

You can still seek care while protecting yourself. Try: “I need this taken seriously,” “Can you write down the plan?”, “What should make me seek urgent help?”, “Can I have a second opinion?”, or “I would like a support person present.” If you need help raising concerns about health or disability care, a health and disability advocate may be able to support you.

Short example

A person with chronic pain and fatigue keeps pushing through work and parenting until they crash for two days. They choose one smaller daily task, schedule rest before the school pickup, write a medication and symptom list for the GP, and ask a friend to come to the appointment.

Common difficulties

  • “People think I am exaggerating.” You do not need to prove your suffering perfectly to deserve support.
  • “I should be able to do what I used to do.” Grief about change is real. A smaller version of life is still life, not giving up.
  • “Natural treatments feel safer.” Natural does not always mean safe. Supplements, herbs, alcohol, cannabis products, and over-the-counter products can interact with medicines or health conditions.
  • “Appointments make me freeze.” Written notes, a support person, and asking for a written plan can reduce pressure in the room.

If this does not work

If pain, fatigue, disability stress, or medical trauma is making daily life unsafe, speak with a GP, pharmacist, specialist, disability support service, counsellor, advocate, or trusted support person. Use urgent medical help for sudden or severe physical symptoms. Use 111 if there is immediate danger. Call or text 1737 if distress, hopelessness, or safety risk is rising.

Kete is not medical advice. Do not start, stop, increase, reduce, or combine medication, supplements, herbs, natural therapies, or over-the-counter products without advice from a GP, pharmacist, prescriber, or qualified clinician.

Older adults, later life, and staying connected

What this is for: use this when later life brings loneliness, bereavement, retirement change, loss of independence, chronic pain, caring for a spouse, worries about memory, alcohol changes, or feeling less like yourself.

When not to rely on this alone: get direct help if there is elder abuse or neglect, sudden confusion, falls, unsafe alcohol or medication use, suicidal thoughts, threats, family violence, or someone is not safe at home.

What you might notice

AreaWhat it can look likeOne useful next step
Loneliness or isolationLong days alone, fewer invitations, avoiding calls, or feeling forgotten.Choose one low-pressure contact: phone call, neighbour, Age Concern, community group, GP practice, or trusted whānau member.
Grief and life changeBereavement, retirement, moving house, giving up driving, or feeling less useful.Name the loss without judging it. Add one small routine that keeps meaning, movement, or connection in the week.
Health, pain, or fatigueChronic pain, sleep disruption, medication burden, reduced mobility, or fear of being a burden.Book a GP/pharmacist review if symptoms, medication side effects, pain, sleep, or alcohol are affecting safety or mood.
Memory or cognitive worriesForgetting appointments, getting lost, repeated confusion, money mistakes, or family noticing a change.Write down examples and ask a GP or trusted person to help arrange a review. Sudden confusion needs urgent medical help.
Elder abuse or pressureFeeling frightened, controlled, neglected, financially pressured, spoken down to, or unsafe with someone who helps you.Use a safe phone or trusted person. Call 111 if in danger. For elder abuse support, contact Age Concern or the Elder Abuse Response Service.

Try this step by step

  • Start with dignity: say, “This is a real pressure. It does not mean I am weak, difficult, or just old.”
  • Pick the closest pressure: loneliness, grief, pain, memory, alcohol, caring, money, transport, safety, or independence.
  • Choose one small stabiliser: eat something simple, drink water, take medication as prescribed, sit in daylight, use a mobility aid, or write one note for an appointment.
  • Add one connection: phone someone, send a short text, ask a neighbour, call Age Concern, contact a community group, or ask a GP practice what local support exists.
  • Check safety: if someone is frightened, neglected, being pressured for money, unsafe at home, or thinking about suicide, involve direct support rather than trying to manage it privately.

Short example

A person stops going to their usual group after their partner dies. They feel flat and irritable, and they are drinking more in the evening. They write down the pattern, book a GP appointment, call one friend, and ask Age Concern about local connection options.

Common difficulties

  • “I do not want to be a burden.” Asking for support is not the same as losing independence. It can protect independence.
  • “Everyone says it is just ageing.” Depression, pain, alcohol problems, medication side effects, elder abuse, and cognitive changes deserve support and review.
  • “I do not want everyone in town to know.” Ask about phone, online, GP, Age Concern, or national helpline options if privacy is a barrier.

If this does not work

If mood is worsening, routine has collapsed, alcohol use has increased, memory changes are worrying, pain is unmanaged, or caring responsibilities are overwhelming, use a GP, nurse practitioner, pharmacist, Age Concern, whānau support, or local older-person support service. Call 111 for immediate danger. Call or text 1737 for mental health support. For elder abuse concerns, contact Age Concern or the Elder Abuse Response Service.

NZ support starting points: Age Concern New Zealand, Elder Abuse Response Service, Healthify NZ later-life depression and social-isolation information, 1737, GP or primary care, pharmacist medication review, Healthpoint, and local community or older-person support services.

Crisis and urgent help

Crisis Help

If you or someone else is in immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111 now. Kete is not a crisis service and cannot monitor safety.

Use this section when the priority is safety, support, and getting through the next short period. Do not try to solve everything during peak distress.

Choose the safest next step

Start with the level of risk, not with the skill. Grounding and breathing can help, but they are not enough when immediate safety is uncertain.

Immediate danger

Call 111 now. Use emergency help if someone may die, be seriously hurt, cannot stay safe, is violent, has taken an overdose, or needs Police, Fire, or Ambulance.

Might act soon

Do not stay alone with the risk. Move away from means of harm if possible, contact a trusted person, local crisis team, 1737, or emergency services.

Very distressed, not immediate danger

Use one stabilising action, then contact support if distress stays high. Choose one small next step rather than trying to solve the whole problem.

Unsafe relationship or monitored device

Do not make a visible plan on a device someone else can check. Use a safer phone, trusted person, specialist service, or 111 if danger is immediate.

1. Move toward safety

Move away from immediate means of harm if possible. Go near another person, a public area, or a safer room. Ask someone to stay with you if needed.

2. Contact support

Use your crisis plan, contact a trusted person, call your local crisis team if known, or use national support such as 1737 for free call/text support in New Zealand.

3. Use one stabilising action

Feet on floor, cold water, name five things you see, slow your out-breath, hold a grounding object, or reduce stimulation.

If you are supporting someone

Stay calm, listen, and do not leave the person alone if immediate safety is uncertain. It is okay to ask directly about suicide or self-harm. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111.

Ask clearly

Use simple wording: “Are you thinking about suicide or hurting yourself?” Asking does not put the idea there; it can help people feel less alone.

Reduce immediate risk

Move away from means of harm if possible, stay nearby, and involve trusted whānau, support people, crisis services, or emergency help.

Get backup

Use 1737, local crisis contacts, the person’s plan, GP/mental health team, or 111 if danger is immediate.

Kete note · informed by NZ suicide prevention and helpline guidance · verify local pathways before formal use.

NZ crisis and support contacts

Key details checked against official NZ source pages in May 2026. Re-check before public release because services, eligibility, phone numbers, and opening hours can change.

EmergencyImmediate danger to life or safety. 111 connects to Police, Fire, or Ambulance.Urgent emergency helpCall 111
1737Free call, text, or webchat support in New Zealand, available 24/7 with trained counsellors and peer support workers.Crisis and emotional supportOpen
LifelineTelephone support for people in distress.Crisis support0800 543 354
YouthlineSupport for young people and their whānau.Youth support0800 376 633 / text 234
SamaritansConfidential emotional support.Listening support0800 726 666
Suicide Prevention HelplinePhone support when suicide risk is present. Use 111 first if there is immediate danger.Suicide crisis support0508 828 865
Manawa — My Own Survival PlanHealthify-reviewed NZ safety-planning app for creating a survival plan, coping steps, and support contacts for times when things feel really bad. Use alongside urgent help when risk is high.Safety planning appOpen
Are You OKFamily violence information, safer-site guidance, and support for people experiencing or worried about unsafe relationships. Phone 0800 456 450.Family violence supportOpen
Safe to TalkFree confidential 24/7 support for anyone affected by sexual harm. Call 0800 044 334, text 4334, or use webchat/email; interpreter support is available.Sexual harm supportOpen
Gambling Helpline NZFree 24/7 support for people affected by gambling harm. Call 0800 654 655 or text 8006.Gambling harm supportOpen

Mini safety plan

Warning signs

  • Sleep changes, isolation, hopelessness, agitation, unsafe urges.
  • Voices or beliefs becoming more frightening or commanding.
  • Substance use, conflict, or feeling unable to stay safe alone.

Protective actions

  • Move near safe people or a safer place.
  • Reduce access to means where possible.
  • Use one grounding action, then contact support.
  • Use professional or emergency help if safety cannot wait.
Trust, scope, and safety

About Kete

Kete is an early-stage educational resource for Aotearoa New Zealand. It helps people find practical mental health skills, printable handouts, crisis guidance, and support routes.

Use Kete as one support tool. If safety, medication, diagnosis, withdrawal, family violence, sexual harm, psychosis, severe distress, or suicide risk is involved, use Kete alongside appropriate professional, whānau, peer, or crisis support.
About Kete Ara

What this site is for

What Kete can do

  • Offer general mental health education.
  • Suggest practical coping and therapy-informed skills.
  • Provide printable handouts for reflection, practice, or appointments.
  • Support between-appointment or between-support practice.
  • Support conversations with a trusted support person, GP, counsellor, nurse, peer worker, pharmacist, prescriber, or mental health team.

What Kete cannot do

  • Diagnose, assess, monitor, or treat anyone.
  • Replace professional care, crisis care, prescribed treatment, or local policy.
  • Provide personalised medication, risk, legal, relationship-safety, or emergency advice.
  • Provide live monitoring, alerts, follow-up, or emergency response.

How to use Kete safely

  1. Start with safety before skills.
  2. Choose one page, one handout, or one tiny step.
  3. Stop or switch to grounding if reading makes distress worse.
  4. Take useful handouts to support rather than trying to explain everything from memory.

When self-help is not enough

Use urgent, professional, or specialist support when distress feels unmanageable, symptoms are new or severe, sleep loss is escalating, voices or beliefs feel unsafe, alcohol or drugs are increasing risk, medication questions are urgent, or another person may harm or control you.

Privacy and data

This standalone prototype does not require login, cloud storage, saved plans, analytics, forms, accounts, reminders, app integrations, or personal health data. Avoid adding any data collection or AI chat features without formal privacy, clinical, cultural, and safety review.

Digital safety note

For people experiencing family violence, sexual harm, stalking, coercive control, or monitored devices, even browser history, printed pages, notifications, shared accounts, saved PDFs, and location settings can affect safety. Use safer devices or specialist support where needed.

Accessibility note

Kete should remain usable with keyboard navigation, screen readers, browser zoom, reduced-motion settings, mobile touch controls, and printed handouts. Formal testing with disabled users and assistive technologies is still needed before public or organisational use.

Review and governance

Before formal clinical use, public service endorsement, or organisational deployment, Kete needs clinical review, Māori and cultural review, privacy review, accessibility testing, lived-experience feedback, version control, named review dates, and a clear process for updating support links.

Review status

This version is a public prototype draft. It uses source-informed, rewritten, plain-English content rather than copied worksheets. Services, eligibility, phone details, app availability, and clinical guidance can change, so support links and source details need scheduled review before public launch.

Source transparency and limits still needing review

Current content base

  • Uploaded self-help resource lists, workbook directories, and existing Kete prototype material.
  • CBT, ACT, DBT-informed, grounding, crisis planning, sleep, behavioural activation, anxiety, psychosis, alcohol, gambling, relationship-safety, and wellbeing resources.
  • NZ-first resource passes covering 1737, Healthify, Mental Health Foundation NZ, Health New Zealand, Māori wellbeing, family violence, sexual harm, gambling harm, and youth support.

This run strengthened

  • Plain-English trust, limitation, and safe-use guidance.
  • Health-literacy alignment: lower reading burden, clearer next steps, and reduced source-transparency wall of text.
  • Digital self-help caution: privacy, app safety, crisis limits, and avoiding unnecessary data collection.

How sources are used

  • Prefer NZ public-health, Healthify, Ministry of Health, Health New Zealand, Mental Health Foundation, and credible public clinical resources.
  • Adapt principles into brief Kete-style guidance.
  • Avoid copying full worksheets or making unsupported clinical claims.

Limits still needing review

  • Kete is not yet formally clinically, culturally, legally, privacy, or accessibility reviewed.
  • Links and service details require scheduled re-checks.
  • Any future data collection, accounts, analytics, reminders, or AI features require a separate safety case.

Prototype content review date: May 2026. Recommended next step: clinical review, Māori/cultural review, privacy review, lived-experience review, accessibility testing, and a formal update schedule before public or organisational use.

Curated, selective, NZ-first

Resource Library

Selected NZ-first and credible resources for skills practice, handouts, crisis support, cultural wellbeing, and self-help alongside support. This is intentionally not a full directory; Kete should signpost useful resources without overwhelming people.

Use alongside judgement. Check age range, local availability, privacy settings, and whether professional or crisis support is needed rather than self-help alone.

Open one category at a time. Urgent and safety routes are kept near the top; more specific resources sit inside grouped sections.

Start here and local supportFirst routes, crisis map, local services

3 resource groups

Fast NZ support map Use when you know the type of help needed but not the right service
1737 — Need to Talk?Free confidential 24/7 mental health support in Aotearoa. Call, text, or chat with 1737 for brief support, advice, and information from trained counsellors or peer support workers.Mental health support / 24-7Open
Lifeline AotearoaFree confidential distress and crisis support. Lifeline: 0800 543 354 or text 4357. Suicide Crisis Helpline: 0508 828 865. Call 111 for immediate danger.Distress / suicide crisisOpen
YouthlineSupport for young people and their parents, whānau, and friends. Call 0800 376 633, free text 234, or use webchat/contact options on Youthline.Youth / whānau supportOpen
Healthpoint — mental health and addictions directoryNZ service directory for local mental health, addiction, helpline, kaupapa Māori, youth, peer, and specialist supports. Useful when looking for regional options.Find local servicesOpen
Safe to Talk — sexual harm supportFree confidential 24/7 support for anyone affected by sexual harm. Call 0800 044 334, text 4334, email, or webchat. Use 111 for immediate danger.Sexual harm / 24-7Open
Are You OK / Shine / Women’s RefugeUse family violence services if there is coercive control, threats, stalking, violence, unsafe housing, financial control, or fear at home. Shine helpline: 0508 744 633. Women’s Refuge crisisline: 0800 733 843.Family violence safetyOpen
Alcohol Drug Helpline and Gambling HelplineAlcohol Drug Helpline: 0800 787 797 or text 8681. Gambling Helpline: 0800 654 655 or text 8006. Use medical or emergency help for withdrawal, overdose, severe intoxication, or immediate danger.Addiction / gambling harmOpen
OutLine AotearoaFree confidential rainbow support line, online chat, peer support, trans/non-binary peer support, and conversion-practices survivor support. Call 0800 688 5463 during service hours.Rainbow / LGBTQIA+ supportOpen
MoneyTalksFree financial helpline and financial mentor connection for money stress, debt pressure, separation, housing stress, or gambling/addiction-related financial strain. Call 0800 345 123 or text 4029.Money stress / financial mentorsOpen
Family Services DirectorySearch for local family, whānau, parenting, disability, violence, counselling, youth, and community services by area and support type.Local community supportsOpen
Start here — Aotearoa New Zealand resources
Just a ThoughtFree NZ online CBT courses for anxiety, depression, OCD, insomnia, perinatal mental health, and substance use. Available in NZ Sign Language.E-therapy / CBTOpen
SmallstepsFree NZ digital toolkit with practical strategies for stress, calming, mood, breathing, relaxation, and self-worth. No sign-up needed.Brief self-helpOpen
Beating the BluesNZ-funded online CBT programme for adults with depression and anxiety. Can be self-accessed or GP-referred.Structured CBTOpen
Healthify He Puna WaioraTrusted Aotearoa New Zealand health information, including mental wellbeing, sleep, nutrition, medication, and condition information.Patient educationOpen
Manaaki OraHealthify-reviewed NZ wellbeing app based on Māori models of health, including Te Whare Tapa Whā and Manaaki Tangata, with tools for emotions, support planning, and whānau support.Māori wellbeing appOpen
Aunty DeeNZ problem-solving tool developed by Le Va for Pasifika and Māori young people, usable by anyone wanting a simple structured problem-solving process.Problem solvingOpen
SPARXFree NZ game-based CBT support for young people aged 12–19 with mild to moderate depression, anxiety, and stress.Youth / CBTOpen
HeadstrongFree NZ app for rangatahi using CBT, mindfulness, positive psychology, interpersonal skills, and Te Whare Tapa Whā.Youth / relationshipsSearch app store
The LowdownNZ youth website for depression, anxiety, low mood, real stories, resources, and online chat support.RangatahiOpen
Local and regional support finder Use when you need help near your city, town, rohe, or region

How to use this map

Start with the safest route first. For immediate danger, call 111. For urgent mental health risk, use a crisis team or emergency service. For ongoing support, search by location, issue, age, culture, or service type. You can ask a GP, nurse, social worker, school, workplace, CAB, or trusted support person to help make the first contact.

Health NZ crisis assessment teams by regionUse this page to find regional crisis assessment team phone numbers. Call 111 instead if there is immediate danger or someone may be seriously hurt right now.Regional crisis contactsOpen
Find wellbeing support — Health NZFind free mental wellbeing support options, including Access and Choice services, kaupapa Māori, Pacific-led, and youth wellbeing services. Some services can be contacted directly without a GP referral, depending on area and service type.Free wellbeing support finderOpen
Citizens Advice BureauFree confidential information and referral support. CAB can help people work out where to go locally for housing, money, legal, family, employment, health, community, and practical support.Local advice/referral supportOpen
Mental Health Foundation — accessing mental health servicesPlain-language guidance on finding low-cost or free counselling, talking with a GP, and using Healthpoint, Family Services Directory, Wellbeing Support, or CAB to find support nearby.Help finding supportOpen
HealthEd — helplines and local mental health servicesA printable/downloadable HealthEd resource with national helplines and local mental health service contact details. Useful to save offline or print for a fridge, wallet, workplace, whānau, or support plan.Printable local-contact directoryOpen
Local support note: services, hours, referral rules, and regions change. Check the linked service page before relying on details. If a local service is closed and risk is rising, use 1737, a crisis team, urgent medical care, or 111 depending on urgency.
Urgent, safety, trauma, and harm supportViolence, sexual harm, online harm, psychosis, trauma

5 resource groups

Safety, family violence, sexual harm, and gambling support
Are You OKNew Zealand family violence information, safer-site guidance, and help understanding unsafe relationships and finding the right service. Phone 0800 456 450.Family violence supportOpen
Women’s Refuge24/7 crisisline and local refuge support for women and children experiencing family violence. Crisisline: 0800 REFUGE / 0800 733 843.Family violence crisislineOpen
ShineFree confidential 24/7 family violence helpline and webchat for people affected by domestic violence. Phone 0508 744 633.Family violence helplineOpen
Safe to Talk — Kōrero mai, ka oraFree confidential 24/7 sexual harm helpline for anyone affected by sexual harm. Call 0800 044 334, text 4334, email, webchat, and interpreter options.Sexual harm supportOpen
Gambling Helpline NZFree 24/7 confidential support for people affected by gambling harm. Call 0800 654 655 or text 8006.Gambling harm supportOpen
E Tū WhānauKaupapa Māori movement and resources supporting whānau to stand together against violence and uphold mana, tapu, aroha, whakapapa, manaaki, whanaungatanga, and kōrero awhi.Māori whānau wellbeingOpen
Trauma and post-traumatic stress supports PTSD information, sexual harm support, and safer first steps

Mental Health Foundation — PTSDNZ information and links for post-traumatic stress, including 1737 and other support starting points.Trauma informationOpen
Find Support — ACC Sensitive ClaimsSupport pathway for people seeking ACC-funded help after sexual abuse or assault. Safe to Talk can help with finding a provider.Sexual harm / ACC pathwayOpen
Online harm, scams, and digital safety supports Cyberbullying, image-based abuse, scams, sextortion, and reporting options

Netsafe — report online harmful contentReporting forms for bullying and harassment, nudes or private content, child sexual exploitation, school incidents, and personal or financial information.Report online harmOpen
Netsafe — image-based abuseInformation and support for non-consensual sharing or threats to share intimate images. Netsafe can often help with options to remove content and reduce harm.Image-based abuseOpen
NCSC / CERT NZ — report a cyber security issueOfficial NZ cyber security reporting and guidance for scams, account compromise, suspicious links, and cyber incidents.Cyber incidents and scamsOpen
Own Your OnlineNZ Government cyber security guidance for people affected by online scams or incidents, including practical steps to secure accounts and devices.Cyber safety guidanceOpen
NZ Police — online child safetyPolice advice about online child safety and what to do if someone is offending against children online. Use 111 if there is immediate danger.Child online safetyOpen
Voices, paranoia, psychosis, and early support
Healthify — PsychosisNZ plain-English information about psychosis, hallucinations, unusual beliefs, treatment options, recovery, and when to seek help.NZ informationOpen
Mental Health Foundation — PsychosisNZ information about psychosis, coping strategies, treatment, support, avoiding alcohol/drugs where relevant, and keeping in contact with a GP or mental health team.Condition informationOpen
Hearing Voices Network Aotearoa NZPeer information, support groups, and online forums for people who hear voices, see visions, or have other unusual sensory perceptions.Peer supportOpen
Talking Minds NZAccessible Aotearoa psychosis information and support links for people, families, whānau, and supporters.Psychosis informationOpen
Healthify — Psychosis supportNZ support page for psychosis services and information, including community support options. Check local availability and referral criteria.Support directoryOpen

Health NZ Canterbury — Totara HouseSpecialist early intervention in psychosis service for young people in Canterbury. Check current referral criteria and local availability.Canterbury early interventionOpen
High-control groups, cult recovery, and coercive-community supports Peer support, leaver support, sexual harm/family violence safety, and practical recovery routes
Decult — resources and support groupsAotearoa-focused directory of peer support groups and resources for people recovering from high-control groups and cultic harm. Check current meeting details before attending.Cult recovery / peer supportOpen
Olive Leaf Network New ZealandProvides aid, advocacy, resources, and awareness for former members of cult-like and high-demand religious sects.High-demand religious groupsOpen

7 resource groups

Therapy approaches, counselling, and treatment choices Directories, types of help, medication questions, and supplement safety

Workbooks and printable worksheets
CCI — Stepping Out of Social AnxietyFree structured CBT modules covering social anxiety cycles, thought challenging, avoidance, behavioural experiments, stepladders, safety behaviours, attention, self-imagery, core beliefs, and setback planning.Social anxiety workbookOpen
Centre for Clinical InterventionsFree clinician-developed CBT workbooks for anxiety, panic, social anxiety, depression, sleep, self-compassion, self-esteem, perfectionism, procrastination, distress tolerance, and more.Best all-round workbook libraryOpen
Healthify — Life skills and problem solvingNZ health-information page signposting practical life-skills courses and a problem-solving worksheet for everyday problems.Problem solving / NZOpen
CCI — Problem-solving moduleStructured CBT self-help module explaining the difference between worry and problem-solving, with steps for defining a real problem, generating options, choosing a plan, acting, and reviewing.Problem solving worksheetOpen
Therapist AidFree CBT, DBT, ACT, mindfulness, relationship, anger, grief, and coping worksheets designed for therapy but usable as handouts.WorksheetsOpen
Get Self HelpLarge collection of CBT information sheets, thought records, and self-help guides, including worry, depression, panic, anger, PTSD, psychosis, and paranoia.CBT handoutsOpen
ACT MindfullyFree ACT worksheets and handouts covering defusion, values, acceptance, psychological flexibility, anxiety, depression, trauma, and relationships.ACT handoutsOpen

Conservation Volunteers NZNature-based volunteering and community restoration opportunities that combine activity, connection, and green space.Nature / connectionOpen
Connection and loneliness
Healthify — Social isolationNZ plain-English information about social isolation, loneliness, small connection steps, and community options such as libraries, groups, volunteering, marae, churches, and local supports.Loneliness / connectionOpen

Lifestyle foundations: movement, food and wellbeing

Healthify — Physical activity and mental healthNZ information on the links between physical activity and mental wellbeing, including starting small and making activity realistic.Movement / moodOpen
Health New Zealand — Eating well for mood and wellbeingOfficial NZ guidance on regular eating, protein, fluids, healthy fats, and Mediterranean-style eating patterns as mood-supportive foundations.Food and moodOpen
Healthify — Food and moodNZ plain-English information about how eating patterns can affect mood and energy. Use without dieting, weight shame, or perfectionism.Nutrition / wellbeingOpen
Healthify — Mental health and your bodyShort NZ overview linking sleep, food, movement, and basic body care with mental wellbeing. Useful for simple foundation conversations.Body foundationsOpen
Mental Health Foundation NZ — Five Ways to WellbeingNZ wellbeing framework: connect, be active, take notice, keep learning, and give. Useful as a simple menu of everyday wellbeing actions.Wellbeing frameworkOpen
Lifestyle foundations for mood
Five Ways to WellbeingConnect, Be Active, Keep Learning, Give, and Take Notice. Useful as a simple wellbeing-planning framework.Wellbeing planSee handout
Food & Mood CentreFree information and practical resources on the relationship between nutrition, gut health, and mental wellbeing. Use as education, not dieting advice.Nutrition and moodOpen

Quitline NZFree Aotearoa New Zealand support to stop smoking or vaping, including phone, text, online support, quit planning, and nicotine replacement support.Smoking/vaping supportOpen
Social prescribing / local community groupsCommunity groups, volunteering, creative spaces, libraries, gardens, peer support, sports, and recovery colleges can support routine and connection.Connection and rolesAsk locally
Digital safety, privacy and mental health apps
Healthify — Mental health and wellbeing appsNZ-reviewed app library for mental health and wellbeing apps. Useful for choosing tools with more caution than random app-store searching; still check privacy, cost, age range, and crisis-safety limits.App choice / reviewOpen
Netsafe — Technology-facilitated family violenceNZ guidance about technology-facilitated coercive control and online safety. Use when device monitoring, location tracking, account access, or digital harassment may affect safety.Digital safety / family violenceOpen

7 resource groups

Pregnancy, birth, and early parenting supports Perinatal mental health, parenting advice, and support for whānau
Healthify — perinatal depression or anxietyPlain-language New Zealand information about anxiety and depression during pregnancy and after birth, including when to get help.Perinatal mental health informationOpen
PADA — Perinatal Anxiety & Depression AotearoaAotearoa New Zealand perinatal mental health charity with information, advocacy, resources, and support-service navigation for pregnancy, childbirth, and early parenting.Perinatal mental health resourcesOpen
PADA — support services directoryDirectory-style support information for perinatal anxiety and depression, including local and national support options. Check current availability before relying on a local listing.Support directoryOpen
PlunketLineFree 24/7 parenting and child health advice from Plunket nurses. Call 0800 933 922.Parenting and child health helplineOpen
Mothers HelpersSupport for mothers who are stressed or distressed during pregnancy or while caring for young children, including appointment and recovery-course options.Perinatal supportOpen
Separation, divorce, and family breakdown supports Parenting arrangements, legal information, money stress, housing pressure, and safety
GOVT.NZ — Separating from your spouse or partnerPlain-language NZ government information about separation, separation agreements, relationship property, children, and practical next steps. Use as information, not legal advice.NZ government / separation informationOpen
Ministry of Justice — Parenting arrangementsInformation and tools for parenting arrangements, including Parenting Through Separation and Family Dispute Resolution pathways.Parenting after separationOpen
Ministry of Justice — Find a service for disputesTool to find Parenting Through Separation and Family Dispute Resolution services. Useful when parenting arrangements need structured support.Parenting support / mediationOpen
MoneyTalksFree NZ financial helpline and financial mentoring support for debt, bills, pressure about money, or practical financial stress during separation.Financial mentoringOpen
Are You OKFamily violence information and support if a relationship ending involves fear, coercive control, monitoring, threats, intimidation, stalking, or violence.Family violence supportOpen
New Zealand Law Society — When your relationship endsPublic legal information about relationship break-up, separation, property, children, and legal advice. This does not replace advice from your own lawyer.Legal informationOpen
Grief, traumatic loss, and serious illness supports Sudden death, bereavement, serious prognosis, and whānau support
Healthify — grief and loss supportNZ support page for grief and loss, including 1737 as a free 24/7 call or text option for any type of grief or loss.NZ grief supportOpen
Healthify — grief and loss informationPlain-language NZ information on grief, coping, and supporting someone who is grieving, including practical ways to help with daily tasks and respect different cultural or religious grieving processes.NZ patient educationOpen
Manaaki Tāngata Victim SupportFree nationwide 24/7 emotional and practical support for people affected by crime and traumatic events, including whānau and witnesses. Phone 0800 842 846.Traumatic events / sudden deathOpen
Hospice New Zealand — bereavement supportInformation about accessing free, confidential grief and bereavement support through local hospice services where available.Bereavement / serious illnessOpen
Healthify — supporting someone who is seriously unwellNZ guidance for whānau and friends on practical and emotional ways to support someone with a serious illness or condition.Serious illness / whānau supportOpen
Youth, young adults, and student supports School pressure, study stress, identity, online life, self-harm risk, and asking for help

Start with safety and one trusted contact

Young people may need different routes depending on age, school, study, family safety, privacy, and whether talking or texting feels easier. If there is immediate danger, call 111. If self-harm or suicide risk is present, use urgent support and involve a safe adult or service.

YouthlineFree confidential 24/7 support for young people in Aotearoa: free text 234, call 0800 37 66 33, webchat, email, and WhatsApp options.Youth helpline / text supportOpen
0800 What’s UpFree counselling phone and web-chat service for children and teenagers. Healthpoint lists it for young people aged 5–19; the service lists 0800 942 8787 and webchat at whatsup.co.nz.Children and teens counsellingOpen
Healthify NZ — mental health services for young peopleNZ information that demystifies mental health services for young people and links to youth mental health topics and support options.Youth mental health informationOpen
InsideOUT KāroaResources, information, and support pathways for rainbow, takatāpui, and intersex young people, whānau, schools, and communities across Aotearoa.Rainbow youth supportOpen
1737 — Need to talk?Free call or text 1737 any time to talk or text with a trained counsellor or peer support worker. Useful when distress is high and you need help now but it is not an immediate 111 emergency.24/7 mental health supportOpen
Men, dads, and help-seeking supports Shutdown, irritability, suicide risk, fatherhood, separation stress, anger, alcohol, and practical support

Use a practical doorway

Some men find it easier to start with sleep, alcohol, anger, work, parenting, or one practical problem rather than saying “mental health”. If risk is rising, use direct support early. If there is immediate danger, call 111.

Lifeline AotearoaFree confidential distress and crisis support. Lifeline: 0800 543 354 or text 4357. Suicide Crisis Helpline: 0508 828 865. Call 111 for immediate danger.Distress / suicide crisisOpen
Mental Health Foundation — men’s wellbeing informationInformation on men’s mental health and help-seeking barriers, including shame, practical barriers, and the need for support routes that fit.Men’s mental health contextOpen
Father & Child TrustSupport and information for expectant dads, new dads, solo dads, teen dads, co-parenting, bonding, discipline, and father-child connection.Dads / fatherhood supportOpen
Mensline ChristchurchChristchurch-based confidential counselling/support for men around relationships, family, friends, children, and issues affecting life.Local men’s support / counsellingOpen
Canterbury Men’s CentreCanterbury men’s and couples counselling/support, including anxiety, depression, relationships, anger management, male-survivor support, ACC-funded services, and community support.Canterbury men’s supportOpen
Parenting overwhelm and family support beyond the baby stage Shouting, guilt, high-needs children, co-parenting, sensory load, child safety, and practical parenting help

Lower the load before things boil over

Parenting support is not only for babies. If you are shouting more, scared you might lose control, feeling trapped, or managing high-needs children with little support, start with safety, one practical support contact, and one smaller next step. Call 111 for immediate danger.

Parent Help — free parenting helplineParent Help lists 0800 568 856 for compassionate, non-judgemental support and advice on parenting issues, 9am to 9pm, 7 days a week.Parenting advice / practical supportOpen
Family Services DirectorySearchable NZ directory for local family, parenting, financial, community, and whānau support services. Useful when you need practical help near you.Local family support finderOpen
Whānau Āwhina PlunketSupport for the development, health, and wellbeing of tamariki under five and their whānau, including PlunketLine and local community supports.Under-five / whānau supportOpen
Carer and support-person wellbeing For people supporting someone through mental distress, addiction, disability, illness, suicidality, trauma, or emotional volatility
Carers NZInformation, advice, resources, and referrals for family carers. Carers NZ lists the National Carer Resource Centre helpline as 0800 777 797 during business hours.Carer support / referralsOpen
Healthify — carers need care tooPlain-language information for people caring for someone close to them, including why carers need support, rest, and their own wellbeing plan.Carer wellbeingOpen
Mental Health Foundation — support for othersGuidance for supporting someone through mental distress while also looking after your own wellbeing, energy, time, and perspective.Supporting someoneOpen
Family Drug Support Aotearoa NZSupport, information, and groups for family, whānau, and friends affected by someone else’s alcohol or drug use. FDS lists 0800 337 877 and 03 281 8740.Alcohol/drug whānau supportOpen
MSD — A Guide for CarersInformation on looking after your own physical and emotional health as a carer, and reducing stress and isolation.Carer informationOpen
Identity, culture, rainbow, migrant, and neurodivergent supportBelonging, racism, culture, rainbow support

6 resource groups

Māori, Pacific, and culturally safer support starting points Wellbeing models, culturally grounded services, and questions to ask before choosing support

Use culture as a strength, not a checkbox

Culturally safer support can include kaupapa Māori or Pacific-led services, whānau or family involvement, language support, rainbow-affirming care, migrant/refugee-informed care, disability access, spiritual/cultural practices, or simply a worker who listens without stereotyping. Ask what options exist before deciding a service is not for you.

Te Whare Tapa Whā — Mental Health FoundationPlain-language introduction to Tā Mason Durie’s Māori wellbeing model, using the wharenui image and the taha of wellbeing. Useful as a reflective framework, not a replacement for Māori-led or clinical support.Māori wellbeing modelOpen
Te Rau Ora — Māori mental health and whānau-centred wellbeingKaupapa Māori and whānau-centred mental health and wellbeing information, workforce context, lived-experience kaupapa, and Māori health resources.Kaupapa Māori / whānau wellbeingOpen
Fonofale model — Mental Health FoundationPacific wellbeing model that uses the image of a fale to describe holistic wellbeing, including family, culture, spiritual, physical, mental, and wider contextual supports.Pacific wellbeing modelOpen
Le Va — Pasifika wellbeing resourcesPasifika-led mental health, addiction, youth wellbeing, violence prevention, and cultural responsiveness resources for families, communities, young people, and the workforce.Pasifika wellbeing / resourcesOpen
Healthpoint — search by culture, location, and service typeUse Healthpoint to search for local mental health, addiction, kaupapa Māori, Pacific, youth, peer, and specialist supports. Check eligibility, location, referral route, and current availability.Local service directoryOpen
Māori wellbeing and kaupapa Māori supports
Healthify — Te Whare Tapa Whā and wellbeingNZ plain-English explanation of Te Whare Tapa Whā, including the four taha and whenua as the foundation of the wharenui.Hauora frameworkOpen
Ministry of Health — Te Whare Tapa WhāOfficial NZ overview of the Māori health model developed by Tā Mason Durie, including whānau, tinana, hinengaro, and wairua.Māori health modelOpen
Kaupapa Māori wellbeing servicesTe Whatu Ora wellbeing support page for free services delivered by Māori, for Māori, using mātauranga Māori and whānau-centred support.Find Māori-led supportOpen
Healthpoint — Kaupapa Māori mental health and addictionsDirectory of kaupapa Māori mental health and addiction services across Aotearoa New Zealand. Check local availability and referral details.Service directoryOpen
Healthify — Māori mental health videosCurated videos and information about Māori approaches to mental health and wellbeing.Learning resourceOpen
Generational trauma, whānau patterns, and cultural wellbeing supports Family patterns, culturally grounded support, whānau wellbeing, and local counselling/service navigation
Healthify NZ — mental health supportPlain-language NZ support information for people and whānau looking after mental wellbeing or finding support.NZ mental health supportOpen
Mental Health Foundation — accessing mental health servicesGuidance on finding mental health support, including low-cost or free counselling options such as Family Services Directory, Wellbeing Support, or Citizens Advice Bureau.Finding supportOpen
Family Services DirectoryNZ directory for local family, whānau, counselling, social, budgeting, housing, and community support services.Local family/whānau supportsOpen
Mental Health Foundation — wellbeing and discrimination contextWellbeing information that recognises racism, poverty, violence, discrimination, and social conditions as barriers to mental wellbeing.Wellbeing contextOpen
Identity, belonging, discrimination, and minority-stress supports Rainbow support, racism/discrimination advice, migrant/refugee support, and culturally grounded wellbeing resources
OutLine AotearoaFree, confidential rainbow peer support by phone or online chat for LGBTQIA+ people and whānau in Aotearoa.Rainbow / LGBTQIA+ peer supportOpen
Te Kāhui Tika Tangata Human Rights CommissionFree, confidential information and dispute-resolution support for unlawful discrimination and other human-rights concerns.Discrimination / rights adviceOpen
Red Cross — specialist refugee mental health servicesCulturally safe mental health care for refugees, former refugees, and whānau affected by trauma before or after arriving in Aotearoa.Refugee mental healthOpen
Healthpoint — refugee / migrant mental health servicesDirectory page for refugee and migrant mental health and addiction services in Aotearoa New Zealand.Local service directoryOpen
Youth and rangatahi wellbeing supports
YouthlineFree support for young people and their whānau, including phone, text, webchat, and other youth-focused contact options.Youth supportOpen
KidsHealth — online tools for mental wellbeingNZ guidance for tamariki and rangatahi about digital tools and programmes that teach skills for feeling down, anxious, or stressed.Youth / whānau guidanceOpen
Healthify — mental health services for young peopleNZ overview of support types for young people, including helplines, GP support, counselling, apps, peer support, and community services.Youth support overviewOpen
Manu Ka Rere — Canterbury youth wellbeingHealth-funded Canterbury mental health and addictions service for young people aged 13–24 with mild to moderate concerns. Check access and referral details.Canterbury youth serviceOpen

Resources deliberately not emphasised

The supporting documents list many apps, including AI chatbots, mood trackers, and broad wellbeing apps. Kete keeps these secondary because the site’s identity is a trusted skills and handout hub, not an app catalogue or AI therapy substitute. Apps may still be useful when chosen carefully with attention to privacy, crisis safety, age range, and clinical fit.

Neurodivergent and sensory support
Autism NZ — understanding autismAutism NZ information, including practical explanation of overwhelm and meltdowns.Autism / whānau supportOpen
Healthify — sensory processingNZ health information on sensory overload, sensory differences, and support needs.Sensory processingOpen
Healthify — sensory modulation videosCounties Manukau / Waitematā videos on sensory modulation for calming, aimed at healthcare providers.Clinician resourceOpen
ADHD New ZealandNZ ADHD information, community, and whānau support.ADHD supportOpen
Health, medication, sleep, ageing, and disabilityMedicines, sleep, pain, disability, later life

6 resource groups

Medicines and side-effect information
Healthify — Medicines and side effectsNZ plain-English information on medicine side effects, including keeping a side-effect diary, seeking advice, and not suddenly stopping a medicine unless a prescriber tells you to.NZ medicine safetyOpen
Healthify — Medicines that affect moodNZ information about medicines that can affect mood, behaviour, and sleep, with advice to discuss concerns with a healthcare provider or pharmacist rather than stopping suddenly.Mood and medicinesOpen
Healthify — Medicines listA NZ guide to keeping an up-to-date medicines list, sometimes called a yellow card, to support safer appointments, pharmacy visits, hospital care, and emergencies.Medicines listOpen
Health New Zealand — AntipsychoticsOfficial NZ information about antipsychotic medicines, including stopping cautions, weight-gain monitoring, and talking with your healthcare provider before stopping.Antipsychotic safetyOpen
Health New Zealand — Mental health during and after pregnancyOfficial NZ pregnancy and maternity information noting that many mental health medicines can be used in pregnancy or breastfeeding, and that a doctor can discuss risks and benefits.Pregnancy / medicinesOpen
Healthify — Medicines and pregnancyNZ consumer information about making medicine decisions before and during pregnancy. Useful for preparing questions for a GP, pharmacist, midwife, prescriber, or specialist team.Pregnancy questionsOpen
Medsafe — Consumer Medicine InformationOfficial NZ source for Consumer Medicine Information and data sheets. Useful for checking formal medicine information, noting that data sheets can be technical.Official NZ medicine informationOpen
Medsafe / CARM — Report a side effectOfficial NZ pathway for reporting suspected adverse reactions to medicines, vaccines, and natural health products. Discuss urgent symptoms with a clinician rather than relying on reporting alone.Side-effect reportingOpen
Sleep and insomnia resources
Healthify — Sleep tipsNZ plain-English sleep hygiene guidance covering habits, routine, light, environment, and practical changes that may take time to help.NZ sleep hygieneOpen
Healthify — InsomniaNZ information on insomnia, including bed as a sleep cue, getting up when unable to sleep, clock-watching, and when to seek help.NZ insomnia informationOpen
Healthify — Sleep condition topicsNZ directory of common sleep conditions, including insomnia, sleep apnoea, restless legs, sleeping tablets, snoring, and narcolepsy.Sleep conditions directoryOpen
Just a Thought — Managing InsomniaNZ online CBT-style course for insomnia, including sleep window, worry, and behaviour patterns that keep insomnia going.NZ e-therapyOpen
CCI — Sleep self-help resourcesClinician-developed information sheets and worksheets for sleep hygiene, insomnia, sleep diary, nightmares, and sleep-related thinking patterns.Sleep worksheetsOpen
Health New Zealand — SleepOfficial NZ overview of why sleep matters, causes of poor sleep, self-care for sleep, getting help, and when to see a healthcare provider.Official NZ sleep guidanceOpen
Healthify — Sleep and mental healthNZ information on the two-way link between sleep and mental health, including anxiety, depression, PTSD, trauma nightmares, and emotional resilience.Sleep and mental healthOpen
Perimenopause, menopause, and midlife wellbeing supports Mood, sleep, brain fog, body changes, and treatment conversations
Healthify — MenopausePlain-language NZ information about perimenopause and menopause, including anxiety, insomnia, brain fog, body changes, and when to seek help.NZ health informationOpen
Healthify — Menopausal hormone therapyNZ information about MHT, possible benefits, risks, and questions to discuss with a clinician. Use for preparation, not self-prescribing.Medication / treatment informationOpen
Health New Zealand — MenopauseOfficial NZ health information about menopause symptoms and treatment options, including physical and emotional symptoms.Official NZ health informationOpen
Sexual Wellbeing Aotearoa — menopause supportNZ sexual and reproductive health service with menopause appointments and information about talking with a clinician about MHT and symptoms.Clinical appointment optionOpen
Eating, body distress, and eating-disorder supports Eating disorders, disordered eating, carers, whānau, and specialist pathways
Healthify — Eating disordersNZ plain-English information about eating disorders, effects on health and wellbeing, and getting help. Useful for understanding warning signs without blame.NZ health informationOpen
EDANZ — Eating Disorders Association of New ZealandNZ support, hope, and resources for parents, carers, whānau, and supporters of people with eating disorders. Helpline: 0800 2 EDANZ / 0800 233 269.Carer / whānau supportOpen
EDANZ — Getting helpGuidance on starting with a GP, asking questions, and finding eating-disorder services in Aotearoa New Zealand.Service navigationOpen
KidsHealth NZ — Eating disorders: getting help for your young personNZ guidance for parents and carers. It recommends seeking help straight away if you are concerned about a child or young person.Young people / whānauOpen
Health NZ — South Island Eating Disorders ServiceSpecialist eating-disorder service information for the South Island. Check current referral pathways and availability through GP or local services.South Island specialist serviceOpen
Older adults, later life, and elder safety supports Loneliness, later-life depression, independence changes, caring roles, and elder abuse concerns

Respect independence and check safety

Older adults may need connection, practical help, health review, cultural support, privacy, or safety support. Do not assume distress is “just ageing”. If there is immediate danger, call 111. If someone feels frightened, controlled, neglected, or financially pressured, use elder-abuse support.

Age Concern New ZealandInformation, local Age Concern services, connection support, and elder abuse response pathways. Age Concern lists 0800 65 2 105 and, where local elder-abuse support is not found, 0800 EA NOT OK / 0800 32 668 65.Older adults / elder abuse supportOpen
Elder Abuse Response Service — Te Tari Kaumātua / Office for SeniorsFree confidential 24-hour elder-abuse helpline: 0800 32 668 65, text 5032, email support@elderabuse.nz. Call 111 if immediate danger is present.Elder abuse / safetyOpen
Healthify NZ — depression in later lifePlain-language NZ health information about depression after age 65, including that depression is not simply a normal part of ageing and can be missed or untreated.Later-life mental healthOpen
Healthify NZ — social isolationInformation about social isolation, loneliness, and practical steps to reconnect. Useful when transport, health, grief, retirement, or confidence barriers have reduced contact.Loneliness / connectionOpen
Healthify NZ — ageing supportNZ ageing-support links, including elder-abuse contact details and community supports. Useful as a starting point for local and practical support options.Ageing support / directoryOpen
Chronic illness, disability, pain, fatigue, and medical trauma supports Pacing, pain/fatigue support, disability access, advocacy, and safer health conversations

Start with safety, pacing, and the right support route

Long-term conditions can affect mood, work, whānau life, identity, and trust in services. Kete can help you prepare a next step, but sudden or severe physical symptoms need medical help, not self-help alone.

Healthify — chronic painNZ plain-language information about chronic pain, including how pain can link with low mood, anxiety, fatigue, sleep disruption, and stress.Chronic pain informationOpen
Healthify — pain supportSupport services and information for people affected by pain and pain-related conditions.Pain support directoryOpen
Healthify — ME/CFSInformation about ME/CFS, including overwhelming physical and mental fatigue, symptoms, diagnosis, and support options.Fatigue / ME-CFS informationOpen
Disability Support Services — Needs Assessment ServicesInformation on NASC needs assessment and service coordination. Consent is needed for referral, and services vary by age, region, eligibility, and need.Disability support / NASCOpen
Arthritis New ZealandInformation and support for people living with arthritis. Arthritis NZ lists 0800 663 463 for advice and support.Arthritis / pain supportOpen
Health and Disability Advocacy ServiceFree independent support for people and whānau who want to understand rights, ask questions, or raise concerns about health or disability care. The service lists 0800 555 050.Health/disability care concernsOpen
Addiction, gambling, alcohol, and compulsive patternsSubstances, gambling, habits, family support

1 resource group

Addiction, substances, habits, and family support
Alcohol Drug Helpline NZFree confidential 24/7 support about your own or someone else's drinking or drug use. Phone 0800 787 797, text 8681, or use webchat. Useful before a high-risk drinking/use occasion or when cutting down feels hard.NZ alcohol/drug supportOpen
Healthify — alcohol informationNZ health information on alcohol, harmful drinking, standard drinks, benefits of drinking less, and where to get help. Useful for checking current NZ guidance rather than relying on overseas unit information.NZ patient educationOpen
Healthify — addiction supportNZ support page with Alcohol Drug Helpline details and links to alcohol, drug, gambling, and other addiction supports. Useful when a person is unsure where to start.NZ support directoryOpen
Health New Zealand — getting help with alcohol or drug addictionOfficial NZ information explaining that people with heavy addiction often need professional help and ongoing support. Useful for framing change as supported recovery rather than willpower.Official NZ guidanceOpen
Just a Thought — alcohol and drug support coursesFree NZ online CBT-informed courses for thinking about change, taking action, and getting back on track with alcohol or drug use. Best used with extra support when risk, withdrawal, psychosis, or severe distress is present.NZ e-therapy / AODOpen
Family Drug Support Aotearoa NZSupport for whānau, family, and friends affected by someone else's alcohol or drug use. Useful when you need help setting boundaries, staying connected, and looking after your own wellbeing.Whānau / family supportOpen
Healthpoint — Mental Health & Addictions directoryNZ directory for finding local mental health and addiction services, including alcohol, drug, gambling, and other addiction counselling/support options by region.NZ service directoryOpen
Gambling Helpline NZFree 24/7 support for people affected by gambling harm. Call 0800 654 655 or text 8006.Gambling harm supportOpen
Work, money, study, legal, and practical stressBurnout, study pressure, practical next steps

1 resource group

Burnout, work stress, and caregiving load supports Work stress, carer support, fatigue, and overload
Healthify — work stress and burnoutNew Zealand plain-language tips for avoiding work stress and burnout, including boundaries, recovery, and support conversations.Work stress informationOpen
WorkSafe NZ — work-related stressInformation about work-related stress and fatigue, including why stress and fatigue matter for physical, emotional, and workplace safety.Workplace health and safetyOpen
Healthify — carers need care tooNew Zealand information for people caring for someone close to them, including support options and ways to refill your own cup.Carer wellbeingOpen
Carers NZInformation and resources for family, whānau, and aiga carers. Carers NZ lists a helpline on 0800 777 797.Carer supportOpen
Service navigation

Choose a support route

Start with the kind of help that matches the level of risk and the problem in front of you. You do not need the perfect service name before asking for help.

Immediate danger

Call 111 in Aotearoa New Zealand if someone might be seriously hurt, is in immediate danger, or cannot be kept safe right now.

High distress but not immediate danger

Call or text 1737 any time for free mental health support, or use a crisis/helpline option below if it fits the situation better.

Ongoing support

Start with a GP, counsellor, peer support, kaupapa Māori, Pacific-led, rainbow-affirming, migrant/refugee-informed, or other culturally safer service, school/work support, Healthpoint, or a local community directory.

Specialist safety issues

Use specialist services for family violence, sexual harm, addiction, gambling harm, scams, eating distress, perinatal concerns, or unsafe relationships.

Kete provides general education and practical self-help skills. It is not a crisis service, diagnostic tool, or replacement for professional care. If there is immediate danger in New Zealand, call 111.
